### 2.1 Architecture of a Modern Digital Hearing Aid

The modern hearing aid is a **micro-DSP (Digital Signal Processor)** system composed of four principal sub-systems:

#### Step 1 — Acoustic Transduction (Microphone)
- **Function:** Converts acoustic pressure variations into an analog electrical signal.
- **Typical Specification:** Sensitivity of **−44 dB to −54 dB re 1 V/Pa**.
- **Common Type:** Electret condenser or MEMS microphone.

#### Step 2 — Analog Front-End (Pre-amplifier + ADC)
- **Function:** Conditions the low-amplitude analog signal and converts to a 16-bit or 24-bit digital stream.
- **Sampling Rate:** Typically **16 kHz – 48 kHz** (Nyquist constraint to capture up to 8 kHz speech band).

#### Step 3 — Digital Signal Processor (DSP Core)
- **Function:** Performs the algorithmic heavy-lifting:
  - Wide Dynamic Range Compression (**WDRC**)
  - Adaptive Noise Reduction (ANR)
  - Feedback Cancellation (AFC)
  - Directional Microphone Steering
  - Frequency Shaping (Multi-channel equalization)

#### Step 4 — Output Transduction (Receiver)
- **Function:** Converts the processed digital/analog signal back into acoustic energy via a miniature loudspeaker (balanced armature or dynamic receiver).
- **Limiter:** Hard-clips at **MPO (typically 110 – 135 dB SPL)** to prevent further auditory damage.

### 2.2 Cochlear Implant (CI) — Bypassing the Cochlea

A cochlear implant is the world's most successful neural prosthesis. It is indicated for **bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss** where conventional hearing aids yield < 50% speech discrimination.

**Two-Stage Subsystem Architecture:**

1. **External Sound Processor (Worn behind ear):**
   - Microphone array → Speech Processor (DSP) → Transmitter Coil (RF link at ~5 MHz).

2. **Internal Implant (Surgically placed):**
   - Receiver/Stimulator (decodes RF) → Electrode Array (12–22 channels inserted into Scala Tympani of cochlea) → Electrical pulses stimulate spiral ganglion neurons → Auditory nerve → Brain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A hearing aid **amplifies** acoustic energy for residual hair cells. A cochlear implant **electrically stimulates** the auditory nerve directly, completely replacing the transducer function of the cochlea.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables / Constants | Engineering Utility |
|---|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $L_p$ = SPL (dB), $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $P_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | Converting acoustic pressure to dB SPL |
| 2 | $G = L_{\text{out}} - L_{\text{in}}$ | $G$ = Gain (dB), $L_{\text{out}}, L_{\text{in}}$ in dB SPL | Hearing aid acoustic gain |
| 3 | $L_{I} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $L_I$ = SIL (dB), $I_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ | Sound intensity reference |
| 4 | $\text{HTL} = \text{dB SPL} - \text{RETFL}_{\text{ANSI S3.6}$ | Threshold shift accounting | Audiometric threshold normalization |
| 5 | $\text{SSPL}_{90} = \text{Max output at 90 dB input}$ | ANSI S3.22 specification | MPO verification |
| 6 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | Nyquist rate, $f_s$ = sampling freq | ADC selection for DSP |
| 7 | $\text{WDRC ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Input (dB)}}{\Delta \text{Output (dB)}}$ | Compression ratio | Fitting formula for hearing aids |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ (Receiver impedance matching) | $V$=voltage, $I$=current | Output stage design |
| 9 | $N_{\text{channels}} = \dfrac{\text{BW}_{\text{audio}}}{f_{\text{elec}}}$ | Cochlear implant channel count | Electrode array design |
| 10 | $\text{SII} = \sum_{i} a_i \cdot w_i$ | Speech Intelligibility Index | Speech discrimination prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical note for tables:** All absolute value bars (`|x|`) in any subsequent equation must be rendered as `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown table syntax. Use `\mid` for conditional dividers.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

- **Why Microphone Directionality?** Because in real environments, the **Speech-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is typically only +3 dB; adaptive directional arrays can improve SNR by **4 – 6 dB**.
- **Why WDRC Compression?** Because recruitment (a sensorineural symptom) makes loud sounds unbearable; WDRC maps a 100 dB input dynamic range into a 50 dB output range the damaged cochlea can tolerate.
- **Why Feedback Cancellation?** Because acoustic leakage from receiver → microphone causes the dreaded **whistling**; adaptive notch filters cancel this in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Acoustic Gain Required for a Patient

> **Problem Statement:** A patient exhibits a hearing threshold of **65 dB HL** at 1000 Hz. The comfortable listening level is **30 dB SL (Sensation Level)**. Determine the required hearing aid insertion gain, given that the residual ear canal resonance provides **+10 dB** of natural amplification (REIG = Real-Ear Insertion Gain).

**Step 1 — Identify the Hearing Threshold Shift (HTS):**

The audiometric threshold is referenced to ANSI S3.6 RETFL (Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level). For 1000 Hz, the RETFL is **7 dB SPL**. Hence:

$$
\text{HTL}_{\text{dB HL}} = L_{\text{threshold (SPL)}} - \text{RETFL}_{1000\,\text{Hz}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Threshold to dB SPL:**

$$
L_{\text{threshold (SPL)}} = 65 + 7 = 72 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

This means the patient requires the sound to be amplified until it is perceived at a comfortable level above their threshold.

**Step 3 — Compute Target Output Level at Tympanic Membrane:**

$$
L_{\text{target}} = L_{\text{threshold}} + \text{Desired SL} = 72 + 30 = 102 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for Real-Ear Unaided Gain (REUG):**

The natural ear canal resonance contributes +10 dB. The hearing aid only needs to supply the residual gain:

$$
G_{\text{required}} = L_{\text{target}} - L_{\text{threshold}} - \text{REUG}
$$

$$
G_{\text{required}} = 102 - 72 - 10 = 20 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Safety Margin (NAL-NL2 Prescription Formula):**

Modern prescriptive fitting (NAL-NL2) adds an adjustment factor $\alpha = 1.0$ for an adult:

$$
G_{\text{final}} = G_{\text{required}} \cdot \alpha = 20 \cdot 1.0 = \boxed{20 \,\text{dB}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The hearing aid must deliver at least 20 dB of insertion gain at 1000 Hz.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Output Limiter Threshold

The MPO protects against acoustic trauma. From the Loudness Discomfort Level (LDL) measured at, say, **110 dB HL**:

$$
\text{MPO}_{\text{dB SPL}} = \text{LDL}_{\text{dB HL}} + \text{RETFL} + \text{RECD}_{\text{avg}}
$$

Where **RECD** = Real-Ear-to-Coupler Difference ≈ **5 dB** for an adult.

$$
\text{MPO} = 110 + 7 + 5 = 122 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

This sets the **hard-clip ceiling** of the receiver.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hearing Aid Selection

| Patient Profile | Recommended Device | Justification |
|-----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Mild–Moderate flat loss, elderly** | Behind-The-Ear (BTE) with open-fit dome | Easy handling, powerful, reduced occlusion |
| **Severe high-frequency loss (steep ski-slope)** | Receiver-In-Canal (RIC) with frequency-lowering | Open fit avoids feedback; frequency transposition improves speech cues |
| **Profound bilateral loss, post-lingual adult** | **Cochlear Implant** | Hearing aids provide < 50% speech scores; CI bypasses dead cochlea |
| **Chronic otitis media (active discharge)** | **Bone-Anchored Hearing System (BAHS)** | Avoids occluding the canal; stimulates cochlea via skull vibration |
| **Single-sided deafness (SSD)** | **CROS / BiCROS** system | Routes signal from deaf ear to better ear via wireless link |
| **Profound deafness + absent auditory nerve** | **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI)** | Stimulates cochlear nucleus directly |
| **Child < 5 yrs with fluctuating loss** | Soft-band BAHS / loaner BTE | Non-surgical, adjustable with growth |

### 3.4 Derivation of Speech Intelligibility Index (SII)

The SII quantifies the proportion of speech cues audible to a listener:

$$
\text{SII} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot \left[ \frac{L_i - L_{\text{thresh},i}}{30} \right] \cdot w_i
$$

where:
- $L_i$ = band SPL at band *i*
- $L_{\text{thresh},i}$ = audiometric threshold in band *i*
- $w_i$ = band importance weight (sums to 1)
- $N$ = number of bands (typically 21)

If thresholds exceed the input level, the term is clamped to **0** (in-band inaudibility).

For our patient (Step 3.1) at 1000 Hz with input at 65 dB SPL and threshold at 72 dB SPL:

$$
\text{SII}_{\text{band}} = \frac{65 - 72}{30} \rightarrow \text{clamped to } 0
$$

Hence, **without amplification**, this band contributes zero to intelligibility — justifying the 20 dB prescribed gain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Hearing Aid Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acoustic Sound Waves] --> B[MEMS Microphone Array]
    B --> C[Analog Preamplifier]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[WDRC Compression]
    F --> G[Adaptive Noise Reduction ANR]
    G --> H[Feedback Cancellation AFC]
    H --> I[Directional Beamforming]
    I --> J[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    J --> K[Receiver Speaker]
    K --> L[Ear Canal Tympanic Membrane]
    L --> M[Cochlea Residual Hair Cells]
    M --> N[Auditory Nerve]
    N --> O[Auditory Cortex Brain]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style O fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#fff4cc
    style F fill:#fff4cc
    style G fill:#fff4cc
    style H fill:#fff4cc
    style I fill:#fff4cc
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant Two-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL SOUND PROCESSOR"]
        E1[Microphone Picks Up Sound] --> E2[Speech Processor DSP]
        E2 --> E3[RF Transmitter Coil]
    end

    E3 -.RF Wireless Link 5 MHz.-> I1

    subgraph INT["INTERNAL IMPLANT SURGICAL"]
        I1[Receiver Stimulator Decodes RF] --> I2[Pulses Generated Bipolar Monopolar]
        I2 --> I3[Electrode Array Scala Tympani 12 to 22 channels]
    end

    I3 --> I4[Spiral Ganglion Neurons Stimulated]
    I4 --> I5[Auditory Nerve Electrical Pulses]
    I5 --> I6[Brainstem Cochlear Nucleus]
    I6 --> I7[Auditory Cortex Sound Perception]

    style EXT fill:#e1ffe1
    style INT fill:#ffe1f5
    style I7 fill:#ffe1e1
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Device Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Patient Presents Hearing Loss] --> T[Pure Tone Audiometry 250 Hz to 8 kHz]
    T --> Q1{Speech Discrimination Score greater than 50 percent?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Configuration of Loss?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Is the Cochlear Nerve Intact?}
    Q3 -- YES --> CIm[Cochlear Implant Recommended]
    Q3 -- NO --> ABI[Auditory Brainstem Implant ABI]
    Q2 --> Q4{Type: Conductive or Mixed with Chronic Discharge?}
    Q4 -- YES --> BAHS[Bone Anchored Hearing System]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Unilateral Loss?}
    Q5 -- YES --> CROS[CROS or BiCROS Routing System]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6{Degree: Mild to Severe}
    Q6 -- MILD --> BTE1[Open Fit BTE]
    Q6 -- MODERATE --> BTE2[Standard BTE or RIC]
    Q6 -- SEVERE --> BTE3[High Power BTE with Earmold]

    style S fill:#e1f5ff
    style CIm fill:#ffd6cc
    style ABI fill:#ffd6cc
    style BAHS fill:#fff4cc
    style CROS fill:#fff4cc
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Section of Human Ear and Intervention Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pinna Auricle] --> P2[External Auditory Canal]
    P2 --> P3[Tympanic Membrane Eardrum]
    P3 --> P4[Ossicles Malleus Incus Stapes]
    P4 --> P5[Oval Window]
    P5 --> P6[Cochlea Scala Vestibuli Scala Media Scala Tympani]
    P6 --> P7[Organ of Corti Hair Cells]
    P7 --> P8[Spiral Ganglion Auditory Nerve]
    P8 --> P9[Brainstem]
    P9 --> P10[Auditory Cortex]

    H1[HAID fits in P2 P3] -.Amplifies.-> P3
    CI1[CI Electrode Array enters P6] -.Stimulates.-> P8
    BC1[BAHS vibrates skull to P6] -.Skull Vibration.-> P6

    style P10 fill:#ffe1e1
    style H1 fill:#cce5ff
    style CI1 fill:#ffd6cc
    style BC1 fill:#fff4cc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**

> Differentiate between **conductive**, **sensorineural**, and **mixed** hearing loss. Mention one rehabilitative technology appropriate for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Conductive Hearing Loss (1 mark):** Pathology in the outer or middle ear (e.g., wax, otitis media, otosclerosis) that impedes sound transmission to a normally functioning cochlea. *Sound is attenuated before reaching the inner ear.* Rehabilitative technology: **Conventional air-conduction hearing aid** or **Bone-Anchored Hearing System (BAHS)** if chronic discharge is present.
- **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (1 mark):** Pathology in the **cochlea** (damaged hair cells) or **auditory nerve**. Most common cause is presbycusis or noise-induced hearing loss. Rehabilitative technology: **Digital hearing aid** (for mild–severe) or **Cochlear Implant** (for severe–profound).
- **Mixed Hearing Loss (1 mark):** Simultaneous presence of conductive and sensorineural components. Treatment combines medical/surgical correction of the conductive element with amplification or implant for the sensorineural element.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

> List any **six components** of a digital hearing aid and state the function of any **two**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Microphone | Converts acoustic waves to electrical signals |
| 2 | Pre-amplifier | Boosts the weak microphone signal |
| 3 | ADC | Converts analog signal to digital |
| 4 | DSP | Performs compression, noise reduction, feedback cancellation |
| 5 | DAC | Converts processed digital signal back to analog |
| 6 | Receiver (Speaker) | Re-converts electrical signal to acoustic output |
| 7 | Battery | Power source (typically Zinc-Air or Li-ion) |
| 8 | Volume Control / Program Button | User interface for adjustment |

*(List any 6 for 2 marks; describe any 2 functions for 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **digital hearing aid**. Discuss the role of **WDRC** and **Adaptive Feedback Cancellation** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient has an audiometric threshold of **70 dB HL at 2 kHz**. The comfortable listening level is **25 dB SL**. The Real-Ear Unaided Gain (REUG) at 2 kHz is **+8 dB**. Calculate the required insertion gain. State the recommended device style. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & WDRC/AFC Explanation:**

The block diagram (refer Section 4.1) shows the path: **Microphone → ADC → DSP [WDRC + ANR + AFC + Beamforming] → DAC → Receiver → Ear Canal**.

**WDRC — Wide Dynamic Range Compression (3 marks):**
- Patients with sensorineural loss have a **shrunk dynamic range** (e.g., input range 0–100 dB but tolerance only 50–90 dB).
- WDRC maps the wide input range into a narrow comfortable range using **non-linear gain**.
- Compression Ratio (CR) = Input change (dB) / Output change (dB). Typical CR for soft sounds: 2:1; for loud sounds: 1:1 (linear).
- It **preserves audibility of soft consonants** (essential for speech intelligibility) while **preventing loudness discomfort**.

**AFC — Adaptive Feedback Cancellation (2 marks):**
- Acoustic feedback occurs when receiver output leaks back to microphone → causes **whistling/howling**.
- AFC uses an **adaptive filter** (often NLMS — Normalized Least Mean Squares) that estimates the feedback path and subtracts a cancelling signal in real time.
- Allows higher gain without instability.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** WDRC + AFC together enable **high-gain, comfortable, stable amplification** — the hallmark of modern digital hearing aids.

---

**Part (b) — Gain Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Threshold = 70 dB HL @ 2 kHz
- Desired SL = 25 dB
- REUG @ 2 kHz = +8 dB
- ANSI RETFL @ 2 kHz = 9 dB SPL

**Step 1 — Convert threshold to dB SPL:**
$$
L_{\text{threshold (SPL)}} = 70 + 9 = 79 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Target Comfortable Level:**
$$
L_{\text{target}} = 79 + 25 = 104 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine Required Insertion Gain (subtracting natural ear gain):**
$$
G_{\text{insert}} = L_{\text{target}} - L_{\text{threshold}} - \text{REUG}
$$
$$
G_{\text{insert}} = 104 - 79 - 8 = 17 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Prescribe Device (2 marks):**

With 17 dB of required gain at 2 kHz and assuming similar loss across frequencies, a **Behind-The-Ear (BTE)** or **Receiver-In-Canal (RIC)** device with a custom earmold is appropriate. The patient should be fitted using **NAL-NL2** or **DSL-v5** prescriptive targets.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly:
> 1. Forget to convert **dB HL → dB SPL** (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Forget to **subtract REUG** in the gain equation (over-prescribes).
> 3. State the wrong device — at this severity, **ITC/CIC are unsuitable**; recommend BTE/RIC.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **anatomy of the human ear** with a labelled diagram. How does a **cochlear implant** differ from a hearing aid in its mechanism of action? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **Auditory Brainstem Implants (ABI)** and **Bone-Anchored Hearing Systems (BAHS)**? State their indications and the patient populations in which each is prescribed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Ear Anatomy & CI vs Hearing Aid (7 marks):**

**Anatomy (3 marks)** — Refer Section 4.4 schematic:
- **Outer Ear:** Pinna + External Auditory Canal — collects and funnels sound.
- **Middle Ear:** Tympanic membrane + Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes) — mechanical impedance matching, ~22× amplification.
- **Inner Ear:** Cochlea (3 fluid-filled chambers) + Organ of Corti (with ~15,000 hair cells).
- **Neural Pathway:** Spiral Ganglion → Auditory Nerve (CN VIII) → Brainstem (Cochlear Nuclei) → Auditory Cortex.

**Mechanism Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Site of action | Outer/Middle ear + Residual cochlea | Auditory nerve directly |
| Requires residual hair cells? | **Yes** (essential) | **No** (bypasses them) |
| Signal form | Amplified acoustic waves | Pulsed electrical stimulation |
| Indication (severity) | Mild–Severe | Severe–Profound (>90 dB HL) |
| Surgery required? | No | Yes (mastoidectomy + cochleostomy) |
| Speech discrimination | 50–80% | 60–90% post-implant |

**Key Concept (1 mark):** A hearing aid *amplifies*; a CI *transduces* (acoustic → electrical).

---

**Part (b) — ABI and BAHS (7 marks):**

**Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI) (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A neural prosthesis that stimulates the **cochlear nucleus** in the brainstem via a surface electrode array, completely bypassing the cochlea and auditory nerve.
- **Indication:** Patients with **Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)** who underwent auditory nerve transection during tumor removal; or **cochlear nerve aplasia**.
- **Outcome:** ABI provides environmental sound awareness but **limited open-set speech recognition** (~10–15%), unlike cochlear implants.

**Bone-Anchored Hearing System (BAHS) (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A semi-implantable device that transmits sound as **vibratory mechanical energy through the skull bone** directly to the cochlea, circumventing the outer/middle ear.
- **Components:** External sound processor + percutaneous (or transcutaneous) titanium abutment screw + bone vibrator.
- **Indications:** 
  - Chronic suppurative otitis media
  - Congenital atresia of the ear canal
  - Single-sided deafness (SSD)
  - Chronic otitis externa
- **Contraindications:** Insufficient skull bone thickness (<3 mm in children), poor hygiene around abutment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Three types** of hearing loss: conductive, sensorineural, mixed. Each has a distinct device pathway.
- ☐ **Reference value:** 0 dB HL = 20 μPa. Remember to **add RETFL** when converting dB HL → dB SPL.
- ☐ **Hearing aid components:** Mic → Pre-amp → ADC → DSP [WDRC + ANR + AFC + Beamforming] → DAC → Receiver.
- ☐ **WDRC** maps wide input range to narrow dynamic range — protects against recruitment.
- ☐ **AFC** uses adaptive filters (NLMS) to cancel acoustic feedback — eliminates whistling.
- ☐ **MPO** is set below LDL to prevent acoustic trauma; typical MPO = 110–135 dB SPL.
- ☐ **Cochlear Implant** is the standard of care for severe-to-profound sensorineural loss when HA gives < 50% speech discrimination.
- ☐ **CI = electrical stimulation** of spiral ganglion neurons; **HA = acoustic amplification** for residual hair cells.
- ☐ **BAHS** for conductive loss with chronic discharge; **ABI** for absent auditory nerve (NF2).
- ☐ **CROS/BiCROS** for unilateral deafness.
- ☐ **Prescriptive formulas:** NAL-NL2 (adults), DSL-v5 (children) — use these for any fitting calculation.
- ☐ **Speech Intelligibility Index (SII)** quantifies proportion of audible speech cues — used to justify amplification.
- ☐ **Gain formula:** $G = L_{\text{out}} - L_{\text{in}}$, but for **insertion gain** subtract REUG.
- ☐ **Nyquist sampling** mandates $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ — typically 16–48 kHz ADC used.
- ☐ KTU examiners often test: (a) block diagrams, (b) dB conversion, (c) device selection rationale, (d) CI vs HA mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheelchairs: Manual Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wheelchairs: Manual Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Manual Wheelchair** is a manually propelled, wheeled mobility device designed to provide postural support and enable independent or assisted locomotion for individuals with impaired walking ability due to physical disability, injury, or chronic medical conditions. It is operated by the user (self-propulsion via upper limbs) or by an attendant (transit/pushchair mode) without reliance on external power sources such as batteries or motors.

According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 7176 series)** and the **WHO Wheelchair Guidelines**, a manual wheelchair must satisfy three functional requirements:
1. **Mobility Provision** – Enabling efficient movement across varied terrains.
2. **Postural Support** – Maintaining segmental alignment of the pelvis, trunk, and lower limbs.
3. **Pressure Distribution** – Minimizing interface pressure over ischial tuberosities to prevent pressure ulcers (decubitus ulcers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Manual wheelchairs form the foundation of mobility assistive technology. Unlike powered wheelchairs, they leverage human biomechanical energy (typically upper limbs) and require careful prescription based on user anthropometry, functional capacity, and environmental context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a manual wheelchair as a **"wearable, rolling exoskeleton"**. Just as a pair of shoes is selected based on foot size, terrain, and activity, a manual wheelchair must be prescribed based on the user's **body dimensions, strength, environment, and lifestyle**.

Imagine pushing a heavy office chair on a carpet versus a lightweight racing bicycle on smooth tarmac — the difference in energy expenditure is enormous. Similarly, a **standard hospital wheelchair (~18–20 kg)** may be suitable for short indoor use, but a **custom ultra-lightweight rigid wheelchair (~6–8 kg)** dramatically reduces upper-limb strain for an active full-time user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** *Rolling Resistance* (measured in Newtons) is a critical differentiator between wheelchair categories. A well-designed ultra-lightweight chair can have rolling resistance **less than 5 N**, while a poorly maintained standard chair can exceed **30 N** on carpet.

## 1.3 Classification of Manual Wheelchairs (Overview)

Manual wheelchairs are broadly classified as:

| Class | Typical Mass | Primary User Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard / Depot Wheelchair** | **18 – 22 kg** | Temporary users, institutional use, indoor mobility |
| **Lightweight / Folding Wheelchair** | **12 – 16 kg** | Moderate active users, occasional outdoor use |
| **High-Strength Lightweight** | **10 – 14 kg** | Regular full-time users |
| **Ultra-Lightweight (Rigid)** | **6 – 9 kg** | Highly active users, sports, long-term independence |
| **Pediatric Wheelchair** | Variable | Children, growth-adjustable frames |
| **Bariatric Wheelchair** | **> 25 kg**, reinforced frame | Users > 100 kg body mass |
| **Transit / Push Wheelchair** | **12 – 15 kg**, small rear wheels | Attendant-propelled only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **prescription process, anthropometric measurements, components, and selection criteria** for manual wheelchairs. Memorize the relationship between user class, frame material (steel vs. aluminum vs. titanium vs. carbon fiber), and propulsion efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biomechanical Principles of Manual Propulsion

Manual wheelchair propulsion is a complex cyclic biomechanical task that involves the upper limbs applying tangential force on the handrim (pushrim) to generate forward motion. The fundamental energy equation governing wheelchair locomotion on a level surface is:

$$F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{slope} + F_{aero}$$

Where:
- $F_{total}$ = Total resistive force opposing motion (N)
- $F_{roll}$ = Rolling resistance (N)
- $F_{slope}$ = Gravitational slope resistance (N)
- $F_{aero}$ = Aerodynamic drag (N)

### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot (M_{user} + M_{chair}) \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\mu_r$ = Coefficient of rolling friction (dimensionless); **0.005 – 0.015** for pneumatic tires on smooth surface
- $M_{user} + M_{chair}$ = Combined mass of user and chair (kg)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity, **9.81 m/s²**
- $\theta$ = Slope angle (radians)
- $\cos\theta$ = Cosine of slope angle

### 2.1.2 Slope Resistance

$$F_{slope} = (M_{user} + M_{chair}) \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 2.1.3 Aerodynamic Drag

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level)
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (≈ 0.8 – 1.2 for a seated human)
- $A$ = Frontal area (≈ **0.4 – 0.6 m²** for seated person)
- $v$ = Forward velocity (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> At typical self-propulsion speeds (**0.5 – 1.5 m/s**), aerodynamic drag is **negligible (<2% of total resistance)**. Rolling resistance dominates the energy budget.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | Gravitational resistance | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$ | Air drag | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $C_{rr}$ (Camber Coefficient) | Effective mechanical advantage due to wheel camber | **0° – 7°** typical |
| 5 | Mechanical Advantage (MA) = D / d | Ratio of pushrim diameter to wheel diameter | D = pushrim Ø, d = wheel Ø |
| 6 | Push Frequency | Cyclic rate of propulsion | **0.8 – 1.6 Hz** typical |
| 7 | Push Length | Arc length traversed per stroke | **0.6 – 0.9 m** typical |
| 8 | Effective Seat Width (ESW) | Hip width + **2 × 25 mm** (clearance) | mm |
| 9 | Effective Seat Depth (ESD) | Buttock-to-popliteal length − **25 – 50 mm** | mm |
| 10 | Backrest Height | Seat-to-scapular/shoulder height (per clinical need) | mm |
| 11 | Seat Slope (Squeeze / Dump) | Rear seat height − Front seat height | **0 – 50 mm** |
| 12 | Camber Angle | Angle of rear wheels from vertical | **0°, 3°, 6°, 9°** |
| 13 | Wheelbase | Distance between front and rear axles | **350 – 550 mm** |
| 14 | Overall Mass (mass budget) | Sum of frame + wheels + accessories | **6 – 22 kg** |
| 15 | ISO 7176-8 Static Stability Test | Tip-back angle (rear wheel locked) | **≥ 10°** minimum |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Formatting Note (Anti-Latex-Table-Break):** All absolute values, conditions, and bracket symbols in the formulas above use vertical bars in display math (`$...$`) rather than inside table cells to preserve the markdown table integrity as mandated by KTU engine standards.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Propulsion Analysis

The manual propulsion cycle comprises four phases:

1. **Push Phase (Contact):** Hands grip the pushrim, force is applied tangentially to generate forward torque.
2. **Push-Off Phase:** Maximum torque application; wheel begins rotating.
3. **Recovery Phase (Non-Contact):** Hands lift and return to the start position; no force applied.
4. **Pre-Contact / Loading Phase:** Hands re-engage the pushrim for the next cycle.

The **mechanical advantage (MA)** of a wheelchair is governed by:

$$MA = \frac{D_{pushrim}}{D_{wheel}}$$

- If $D_{pushrim} = 0.53$ m and $D_{wheel} = 0.61$ m, then $MA = 0.53 / 0.61 \approx 0.87$
- A **lower MA (smaller pushrim)** → more force required, less distance per stroke (good for acceleration).
- A **higher MA (larger pushrim)** → less force, more distance per stroke (good for endurance).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles are vital in:
- **Sports Wheelchair Design:** Racing chairs use extreme camber (**12° – 20°**), three-wheel geometry, and ultra-light carbon fiber to maximize velocity and lateral stability.
- **Occupational Therapy Prescription:** Therapists match MA, seat-to-axle position, and backrest angle to user's shoulder range of motion and trunk control.
- **Ergonomics Research:** Studying cumulative trauma disorders (e.g., **carpal tunnel syndrome** in long-term wheelchair users) directly links propulsion biomechanics to upper-limb injury prevention.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Expenditure per Unit Distance

The **cost of transport (COT)** for a manual wheelchair is the energy required per unit distance per unit body mass. This is a key metric in rehabilitation engineering and is highly emphasized in KTU Module 3 problem-solving.

### Step 1 — Total Resistive Force on Level Ground
On a level surface ($\theta = 0^\circ$, hence $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$):

$$F_{total} = F_{roll} + 0 + F_{aero}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Each Term
Substituting the standard expressions:

$$F_{total} = \mu_r \cdot (M_u + M_c) \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

### Step 3 — Power Required at Constant Velocity
Power is force times velocity:

$$P = F_{total} \cdot v$$

$$P = \mu_r \cdot (M_u + M_c) \cdot g \cdot v + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3$$

### Step 4 — Energy per Unit Distance
Energy per unit distance is power divided by velocity:

$$E/d = \frac{P}{v} = \mu_r \cdot (M_u + M_c) \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

### Step 5 — Specific Energy (Cost of Transport)
Normalize by total mass:

$$COT = \frac{E/d}{(M_u + M_c)} = \frac{\mu_r \cdot g \cdot (M_u + M_c) + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2}{M_u + M_c}$$

$$COT = \mu_r \cdot g + \frac{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot (M_u + M_c)}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The first term ($\mu_r \cdot g$) is **mass-independent friction loss** — a function of tire and surface.
> - The second term is the **velocity-dependent air drag contribution** — dominant at high speed.
> - Lighter chairs and lower $\mu_r$ directly reduce COT, validating the design of ultra-lightweight wheelchairs.

### Step 3.2 Worked Example Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A user with body mass **$M_u = 70$ kg** uses a manual wheelchair of mass **$M_c = 8$ kg** on a flat floor. The rolling friction coefficient is $\mu_r = 0.010$. Calculate:
1. Total resistive force.
2. Power required to maintain **$v = 1.0$ m/s**.
3. Cost of transport.

> **Solution:**

**Part (i): Total Resistive Force** (neglect aerodynamic drag at low speed)

$$F_{total} = \mu_r \cdot (M_u + M_c) \cdot g$$

$$F_{total} = 0.010 \cdot (70 + 8) \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{total} = 0.010 \cdot 78 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{total} = 7.6518 \, \text{N}$$

**Part (ii): Power at v = 1.0 m/s**

$$P = F_{total} \cdot v = 7.6518 \cdot 1.0 = 7.65 \, \text{W}$$

**Part (iii): Cost of Transport**

$$COT = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v / v}{M_u + M_c} = \frac{F_{total}}{M_u + M_c} = \frac{7.6518}{78} = 0.098 \, \text{J/(m·kg)}$$

> Alternatively, using the unit-normalized form:

$$COT = \mu_r \cdot g = 0.010 \cdot 9.81 = 0.0981 \, \text{J/(m·kg)}$$

This matches experimental data for efficient manual wheelchair propulsion (**0.08 – 0.15 J/m·kg**), confirming the derivation is correct.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component-Wise Breakdown

| Component | Function | Material Options | Design Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frame** | Primary structural skeleton | Steel, Aluminum (6061/7000 series), Titanium, Carbon Fiber | Weight vs. cost vs. durability |
| **Rear Wheels** | Propulsion & support | Spoked, Composite (molded), Mag | Mass vs. stiffness vs. maintenance |
| **Pushrims (Handrims)** | Force transmission | Aluminum, Plastic-coated, Titanium, Anti-slip | Grip vs. heat dissipation vs. mass |
| **Front Casters** | Steering, stability | Pneumatic, Solid PU, Foam-filled | Vibration damping vs. rolling resistance |
| **Seat** | Pressure distribution, posture | Sling nylon, rigid padded, cushion-mounted | Comfort vs. weight vs. postural support |
| **Backrest** | Trunk support | Tension-adjustable strap, rigid shell, lumbar-contoured | Mobility vs. postural stability |
| **Armrests** | Forearm support | Fixed, flip-back, detachable, height-adjustable | Function vs. transfer ease |
| **Footrests/Legrests** | Lower limb support | Fixed, swing-away, elevating, amputee-specific | Knee angle vs. transfer ease |
| **Wheel Locks (Brakes)** | Stationary stability | Push-to-lock, pull-to-lock, scissor, hub-lock | Strength vs. dexterity to operate |

## 3.4 Camber and Axle Position Analysis

The **rear wheel camber angle** $\phi$ is the angle of the wheel from the vertical plane. Camber affects:
- **Lateral Stability:** Higher camber = greater base of support.
- **Pushrim Reach:** Higher camber positions pushrims closer to the user's shoulders.
- **Turning Effort:** Higher camber = more efficient pushrim access during turns.

**Geometric Effect on Effective Wheelbase:**

The lateral separation between the bottom contact points of the two rear wheels when cambered is:

$$W_{eff} = W_{frame} + 2 \cdot r \cdot \sin\phi$$

Where $r$ is the rear wheel radius. For a typical chair with $W_{frame} = 0.40$ m, $r = 0.305$ m, and $\phi = 6°$:

$$W_{eff} = 0.40 + 2 \cdot 0.305 \cdot \sin(6°)$$

$$W_{eff} = 0.40 + 2 \cdot 0.305 \cdot 0.1045$$

$$W_{eff} = 0.40 + 0.0638 = 0.4638 \, \text{m}$$

Thus, a 6° camber adds **~64 mm** of effective lateral stability — a critical factor in preventing tip-overs during high-speed maneuvers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Manual Wheelchair Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Body] --> B[Seating Subsystem]
    B --> B1[Cushion / Sling Seat]
    B --> B2[Backrest / Trunk Support]
    B --> B3[Lateral Trunk Supports]
    B1 --> C[Frame Subsystem]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Cross Brace / Rigid Joint]
    C --> C2[Axle Plate / Camber Tube]
    C --> C3[Footrest Mount]
    C1 --> D[Wheel Subsystem]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Rear Wheel + Pushrim]
    D --> D2[Front Caster + Fork]
    D --> D3[Anti-Tip Wheel]
    A --> E[Wheel Locks]
    C3 --> F[Lower Extremity Support]
    F --> F1[Heel Loop / Footplate]
    F --> F2[Legrest Hanger]
    E --> G[Stationary Stability]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Safety and ISO 7176 Compliance]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Wheelchair Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Assessment]) --> Q1{Long-term or\nTemporary Use?}
    Q1 -- Temporary --> Std[Standard Depot Chair]
    Q1 -- Long-term --> Q2{Active Daily Use?}
    Q2 -- No --> Light[Lightweight Folding Chair]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{User can self-propel\nefficiently?}
    Q3 -- No --> Transit[Transit / Push Chair]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Environment?}
    Q4 -- Indoor/Smooth --> Rigid1[Rigid Ultra-Light]
    Q4 -- Mixed Terrain --> Rigid2[Rigid w/ Adjustable Axle]
    Q4 -- Sports/High Activity --> Sport[Sports-Specific Chair]
    Q1 --> Body[Body Mass > 100 kg]
    Body --> Bariatric[Bariatric Reinforced Chair]
    Start --> Paed[Pediatric / Growth]
    Paed --> PedCh[Adjustable Pediatric Frame]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Forces Acting on a Manual Wheelchair on a Slope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forces
    A[User + Chair Mass M g] --> B[Normal Force N]
    B --> C[Friction Friction Force]
    A --> D[Gravity Component along slope]
    D --> E[Slope Angle theta]
    E --> F[Net Forward / Backward Force]
    C --> F
    F --> G{Stationary?\nStatic Friction}
    G -- Yes --> H[Equilibrium]
    G -- No --> I[Kinetic Motion]
    end
    subgraph Resistive Elements
    J[Rolling Resistance µr N cos theta] --> F
    K[Aerodynamic Drag 0.5 rho Cd A v²] --> F
    end
```

## 4.4 Propulsion Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Hand Contact on Pushrim] --> P2[Push Phase - Force Application]
    P2 --> P3[Push-Off - Max Torque]
    P3 --> P4[Recovery Phase - No Force]
    P4 --> P5[Hand Return to Start]
    P5 --> P1
    P1 -.Energy Expended.-> Energy[(Biomechanical Energy Source\nUpper Limbs + Trunk)]
    P3 -.Forward Motion.-> Velocity[Velocity Output]
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Manual Wheelchair Frame Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Top[Top View of Wheelchair] --> RearAxle[Rear Axle Position]
    Top --> FrontCaster[Front Caster Position]
    RearAxle --> WB[Wheelbase Distance L]
    FrontCaster --> WB
    RearAxle --> TrackL[Rear Track Width]
    TrackL --> Camber[Camber Angle phi]
    Camber --> EffectiveTrack[Effective Lateral Stability]
    RearAxle --> SeatAngle[Seat-to-Axle Angle]
    FrontCaster --> SeatAngle
    SeatAngle --> MassDist[Mass Distribution]
    MassDist --> Stability{Stability and\nManeuverability}
    TrackL --> Stability
    WB --> Stability
    EffectiveTrack --> Stability
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**Q: Differentiate between a "depot" wheelchair and an "ultra-lightweight rigid" wheelchair. List three clinical situations where an ultra-lightweight chair is indicated.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Depot Wheelchair | Ultra-Lightweight Rigid |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass** | 18 – 22 kg | 6 – 9 kg |
| **Frame** | Folding, steel, adjustable | Rigid, aluminum/titanium/carbon fiber |
| **Use Case** | Temporary, institutional, indoor | Active long-term users, full-time propulsion |
| **Customization** | Minimal | Highly individualized geometry |
| **Cost** | Low | High |

**Three Clinical Indications for Ultra-Lightweight Chairs:**
1. **Spinal Cord Injury (SCI):** Long-term users with high propulsion demand; reduced upper-limb strain reduces risk of repetitive strain injuries.
2. **Spina Bifida / Pediatric Long-term Use:** Active children/adolescents who need energy-efficient mobility.
3. **Amputee with Active Lifestyle:** Bilateral lower-limb amputees using a chair for primary mobility require rigid frames to handle asymmetric loading.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Q: Define "camber angle" of a manual wheelchair. Explain its effect on lateral stability and pushrim accessibility.**

**Model Answer:**

The **camber angle ($\phi$)** is the inclination of the rear wheel from the vertical (sagittal) plane, measured in degrees, such that the bottom of the wheel is closer to the midline of the chair than the top.

**Effects:**
- **Lateral Stability:** Increases the effective base of support. A 6° camber adds approximately **2 × r × sin(6°)** to the rear track, significantly reducing tip-over risk.
- **Pushrim Accessibility:** The pushrims move closer to the user's shoulders, reducing shoulder abduction angle and improving mechanical advantage during stroke.
- **Drawback:** Increased camber increases the chair's overall width, making it difficult to navigate narrow doorways (**standard doorway = 810 mm**).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Describe the components of a manual wheelchair with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss how the choice of frame material (steel vs. aluminum vs. titanium vs. carbon fiber) affects the wheelchair's performance, durability, and cost. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manual wheelchair user weighing **80 kg** (including a 10 kg chair) needs to climb a **5° ramp**. Calculate:
   (i) The slope resistance force.
   (ii) The rolling resistance force, given $\mu_r = 0.012$.
   (iii) The total force the user must overcome to maintain a steady climb. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Components of a Manual Wheelchair:**

1. **Frame** – Steel, aluminum, titanium, or carbon fiber; provides structural integrity.
2. **Rear Wheels** – 24" / 25" / 26" diameter, spoked or composite, with pushrims.
3. **Front Casters** – 5" – 8" diameter, pneumatic or solid.
4. **Seat and Backrest** – Sling, padded, or rigid with postural inserts.
5. **Armrests** – Fixed, flip-back, height-adjustable.
6. **Footrests / Legrests** – Swing-away, elevating, or amputee supports.
7. **Wheel Locks** – Push-to-lock, pull-to-lock, scissor, or hub-lock.
8. **Anti-Tip Wheels** – Rear-mounted safety wheels.

**Frame Material Comparison:**

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Strength | Mass Impact | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steel** | 7.85 | High | Heavy (18–22 kg) | Low | Depot chairs, bariatric use |
| **Aluminum (6061)** | 2.70 | Moderate | Light (10–14 kg) | Moderate | Lightweight folding chairs |
| **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V)** | 4.43 | Very High | Very light (7–10 kg) | High | Active long-term users |
| **Carbon Fiber (Composite)** | 1.55 – 1.80 | Very High (directional) | Ultra-light (6–8 kg) | Very High | Sports, racing, premium ultralight |

**Performance Implications:** Lighter materials reduce rolling inertia, lower COT, and reduce upper-limb fatigue. Durability depends on fatigue life (steel ≈ titanium > aluminum >> carbon fiber in transverse loads). Cost escalates non-linearly with material technology.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $M_u + M_c = 90$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\theta = 5°$, $\mu_r = 0.012$

**(i) Slope Resistance:**

$$F_{slope} = (M_u + M_c) \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$F_{slope} = 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(5°)$$

$$F_{slope} = 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0872$$

$$F_{slope} = 76.97 \, \text{N}$$

**(ii) Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot (M_u + M_c) \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$F_{roll} = 0.012 \cdot 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(5°)$$

$$F_{roll} = 0.012 \cdot 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9962$$

$$F_{roll} = 10.55 \, \text{N}$$

**(iii) Total Resistive Force:**

$$F_{total} = F_{slope} + F_{roll} = 76.97 + 10.55 = 87.52 \, \text{N}$$

The user must generate at least **87.52 N** of forward thrust to maintain a steady climb on a 5° ramp. This is within the capability of most healthy upper limbs, but for SCI users with limited grip, this may necessitate a **powered assist add-on**.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper – Alternative]

**Q: (a)** Explain the WHO 8-Step Wheelchair Service Delivery Model. List the critical anthropometric measurements required during the prescription of a manual wheelchair. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the biomechanics of manual wheelchair propulsion, including the four phases of the propulsion cycle. Explain how **pushrim diameter, wheel camber, and axle position** influence the mechanical advantage of the propulsion system. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) WHO 8-Step Wheelchair Service Delivery:**

1. **Referral and Appointment** – User identified as requiring a wheelchair.
2. **Assessment** – Physical, functional, environmental, and lifestyle assessment.
3. **Prescription** – Selection of wheelchair type, size, and features.
4. **Funding and Ordering** – Procurement through appropriate channels.
5. **Product Preparation** – Assembly, fitting adjustments.
6. **Fitting** – User-specific adjustments (seat width, depth, backrest, footrest).
7. **User Training** – Safe transfer, propulsion, maintenance, and emergency techniques.
8. **Follow-up, Maintenance, and Repair** – Periodic review and repairs.

**Critical Anthropometric Measurements:**

| Measurement | Description | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hip Width** | Maximum breadth across hips | Effective Seat Width (Hip + 50 mm) |
| **Buttock-to-Popliteal Length** | Seat depth to popliteal fossa | Effective Seat Depth (length − 25 mm) |
| **Popliteal Height** | Sole to popliteal fossa | Footrest length |
| **Elbow Height (seated)** | Seat surface to elbow | Armrest height |
| **Shoulder Height (seated)** | Seat surface to acromion | Backrest height |
| **Body Mass** | Total user mass | Frame strength selection |
| **Trunk Control** | Clinical assessment | Determines backrest type |
| **Lower Leg Length** | Popliteal to heel | Footplate position |

**(b) Biomechanics of Manual Propulsion:**

**Four Phases of Propulsion Cycle:**
1. **Contact / Push Phase** – Hand engages pushrim; tangential force applied.
2. **Push-Off / Loading Phase** – Peak torque generation; wheel accelerates.
3. **Recovery Phase** – Hand lifts; no force; arm returns forward.
4. **Pre-Contact Phase** – Hand repositions for next stroke.

**Pushrim Diameter Influence:**

$$MA = \frac{D_{pushrim}}{D_{wheel}}$$

A larger pushrim (relative to wheel) → higher MA → less force per stroke, more distance traveled. Recommended for users with **weaker grip strength** (e.g., post-polio, elderly). A smaller pushrim → lower MA → more force, less distance → better for acceleration but higher fatigue.

**Camber Influence:**
- Camber brings the pushrim closer to the user's shoulder girdle.
- Reduces shoulder abduction angle, decreasing risk of **impingement syndrome**.
- The biomechanically effective stroke arc increases with camber.

**Axle Position Influence:**
- Moving the rear axle **forward** (toward the front casters) reduces the wheelbase → **more maneuverable, less stable**.
- Moving the axle **rearward** increases wheelbase → **more stable, harder to turn**.
- Adjustable axle plates (e.g., on Quickie, TiLite chairs) allow tuning of this trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add user mass to chair mass** in rolling resistance calculations. Always use $(M_u + M_c) \cdot g$ for the normal force.
> 2. **Confusing camber angle with caster angle** — camber is on the **rear** wheels; caster trail is on the **front** wheels.
> 3. **Mixing up "Effective Seat Width" formula:** Students often write "hip width + 50 mm" but forget to mention **clearance for clothing or postural asymmetry** (typically 25 mm per side).
> 4. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers (e.g., "10" could be N, kg, or mm). Always state **N** for force.
> 5. **Missing clinical justification** in prescription questions — a KTU examiner expects the answer to link biomechanics to user functional capacity, not just list numbers.
> 6. **Omitting the four-phase propulsion cycle** entirely in a "biomechanics" question — this is a high-frequency 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A manual wheelchair is a human-powered mobility device governed by ISO 7176 standards.
- **Three Main Classes:** Depot, Lightweight Folding, Ultra-Lightweight Rigid.
- **Key Resistance Forces:** Rolling friction, slope gravity, and aerodynamic drag.
- **Rolling Resistance Equation:** $F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$
- **Slope Resistance Equation:** $F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin\theta$
- **Cost of Transport (COT):** $\mu_r \cdot g + \frac{\rho C_d A v^2}{2 M_{total}}$; dominated by rolling friction.
- **Frame Materials (Ascending Cost & Lightness):** Steel → Aluminum → Titanium → Carbon Fiber.
- **Camber Angle:** Typically 0°–9°; improves lateral stability and pushrim access; increases chair width.
- **Axle Position:** Forward = maneuverable; Rearward = stable.
- **Effective Seat Width:** Hip width + 2 × 25 mm.
- **Effective Seat Depth:** Buttock-to-popliteal − 25 to 50 mm.
- **WHO 8-Step Model:** Referral → Assessment → Prescription → Funding → Preparation → Fitting → Training → Follow-up.
- **Propulsion Cycle:** Contact → Push-Off → Recovery → Pre-Contact.
- **Mechanical Advantage (MA):** $D_{pushrim} / D_{wheel}$; lower MA = more force, higher MA = easier stroke.
- **Tip-Back Safety (ISO 7176-8):** Minimum 10° tip-back angle.
- **Standard Doorway Width:** 810 mm — limits maximum allowable camber.
- **Typical Self-Propulsion Speed:** 0.5 – 1.5 m/s.
- **Push Frequency:** 0.8 – 1.6 Hz.
- **Push Length (Stroke Arc):** 0.6 – 0.9 m per stroke.
- **Clinical Caution:** Long-term manual wheelchair use is associated with **carpal tunnel syndrome** and **shoulder impingement** — justify prescription of ultra-lightweight chairs for active users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Takeaway:** Mastering manual wheelchair prescription requires integrating **biomechanics (formulas), anthropometry (measurements), component design (materials, camber, axle), and clinical reasoning (WHO 8-step model)**. Numerical questions on rolling/slope resistance and conceptual questions on camber and propulsion are **guaranteed high-weightage topics** for the End Semester Examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Power Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Power Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Electric Power Wheelchair (EPW)**, also called a **Powered Wheelchair** or **Motorized Wheelchair**, is a wheeled mobility device propelled by one or more electric motors, deriving its energy from an onboard rechargeable battery pack, and controlled by the user through an electronic input interface (commonly a joystick). Unlike manual wheelchairs that rely on human muscular power, EPWs are prescribed medical devices classified under **Assistive Technology (AT)** and are engineered for individuals who cannot self-propel a manual chair due to conditions such as spinal cord injury (SCI), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), muscular dystrophy, cerebral palsy, multiple sclerosis, or advanced arthritis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"An electric power wheelchair is a battery-operated mobility device that uses one or more DC motors coupled to a microcontroller-based drive system, allowing individuals with limited upper-limb strength or coordination to achieve independent, sustained, and safe ambulation across varied environments."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Powered Recliner on Wheels"

Imagine a standard **recliner chair in your living room**. Now attach **two quiet electric scooter motors** to the rear wheels, strap a **car's 12V battery** underneath, and wire a **gaming joystick** to the armrest. Push the joystick forward, the chair glides ahead. Push it left, the chair pivots gracefully. That, in essence, is a power wheelchair.

The deeper analogy: just as a **modern car's drive-by-wire system** translates a small steering input into large mechanical movements, an EPW's **controller** translates a tiny thumb-twitch on the joystick into controlled, safe motion for a person weighing **75–150 kg**.

## 1.3 Key Specifications & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-Mandated Performance Metrics to Memorize:**

| Parameter | Standard Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Speed** | 6 – 10 | km/h |
| **Range per Charge** | 15 – 40 | km |
| **Battery Voltage** | 12 / 24 / 48 | V (DC) |
| **Motor Power (each)** | 150 – 400 | W |
| **User Weight Capacity** | 100 – 180 (Standard) / up to 250+ (Heavy-duty) | kg |
| **Turning Radius** | 0.5 – 1.2 | m |
| **Climbable Gradient** | 6° – 12° | degrees |

## 1.4 Classification of Electric Power Wheelchairs

KTU 2024 scheme classifies EPWs along **three primary axes**:

**A. By Drive Configuration (Wheel Position):**
1. **Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD)** – Drive wheels at back; large front casters.
2. **Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD)** – Drive wheels in center under the user; six wheels total.
3. **Front-Wheel Drive (FWD)** – Drive wheels at front; rear casters.

**B. By Functional Class (per RESNA / ISO 7176):**
1. **Class A (Indoor Use)** – Compact, short range, low speed.
2. **Class B (Mixed Indoor/Outdoor)** – Moderate range and speed.
3. **Class C (Outdoor Use)** – Robust, longer range, higher speed.

**C. By User Input Method:**
1. **Standard Joystick Control**
2. **Sip-and-Puff (pneumatic)**
3. **Chin Control / Head Control**
4. **Switch / Scanning Input**
5. **Voice / Eye-Gaze Control (advanced)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of an EPW

The EPW is fundamentally a **closed-loop electromechanical servo system**. Its architecture can be decomposed into four interacting sub-systems:

### Sub-System I — Power Subsystem
- **Battery Pack:** Series-connected sealed lead-acid (SLA), AGM, gel-cell, or increasingly **lithium-ion (LiFePO₄)** cells.
- **Function:** Stores electrochemical energy and supplies regulated DC voltage to motors and electronics.

### Sub-System II — Drive (Actuator) Subsystem
- **DC Permanent Magnet (PMDC) Motors** — One per drive wheel.
- **Planetary Gearboxes** — Step down motor RPM (typically 3000–5000 RPM) to wheel RPM (~50–150 RPM), multiplying torque.
- **Electromagnetic or Motor-Brake** — Spring-applied, electrically released; engages when joystick returns to neutral (fail-safe).

### Sub-System III — Control Subsystem
- **Microcontroller / DSP-based Controller** — Receives joystick position (typically via 0–5V analog or PWM), implements **PID control loop**, generates **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM)** drive signals to MOSFET/IGBT H-bridge.
- **Joystick Module** — Two-axis Hall-effect potentiometer producing X (turn) and Y (forward/reverse) analog signals.

### Sub-System IV — Mechanical / Chassis Subsystem
- **Frame:** Tubular steel or aluminium, foldable or rigid.
- **Wheels:** Drive wheels (pneumatic or solid polyurethane); caster wheels.
- **Seating:** Standard sling, captain's seat, or rehab seat with tilt / recline / elevate actuators.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Exists — Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why-How" Logic for the Drive Train:**

- **Why PMDC motors and not AC induction?** → DC PMDC motors offer excellent low-speed torque, simple H-bridge control, low cost, and brush-type versions are easily replaceable. AC motors require complex VFDs.
- **Why planetary gearbox?** → Highest torque density in a compact cylindrical package; coaxial input-output simplifies frame layout.
- **Why PWM and not linear voltage regulation?** → Linear regulators waste $(V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I$ as heat. PWM switches at >20 kHz, achieving efficiencies of **85–95%**, extending battery range.
- **Why spring-applied brake?** → A spring holds the brake engaged. Power is needed only to *release* it. If battery dies, the chair stops — **inherently fail-safe**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in KTU Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power from motor torque and angular velocity | W (rad/s) |
| 2 | $V = I \cdot R_a + k \cdot \omega$ | DC motor armature voltage equation (back-EMF) | V, A, Ω, rad/s |
| 3 | $T = k_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque proportional to armature current | N·m, A |
| 4 | $E_{batt} = V_{batt} \times C_{Ah}$ | Battery stored energy | Wh |
| 5 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{consumption}} \times v_{avg}$ | Range in km | km |
| 6 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | PWM duty ratio | % |
| 7 | $V_{avg,motor} = D \times V_{batt}$ | Average motor voltage via PWM | V |
| 8 | $F_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Force needed to ascend a slope | N |
| 9 | $I_{total} = n \cdot I_{motor}$ | Total battery current draw for *n* motors | A |
| 10 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{gear} \times \eta_{controller}$ | Cumulative system efficiency | dimensionless |

**Symbol Glossary (KTU Standard):**
- $T$ = torque (N·m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $V, I, R_a$ = armature voltage, current, resistance
- $k, k_t$ = motor voltage and torque constants
- $C_{Ah}$ = battery ampere-hour capacity
- $D$ = PWM duty cycle
- $m$ = total mass (chair + user)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\theta$ = incline angle
- $\eta$ = efficiency

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EPW design principles transfer directly to:
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR)** in warehouses — same differential-drive kinematics.
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in hospitals carrying meals/medication.
- **Personal Mobility Vehicles** for the elderly.
- **Mars Rover drivetrains** (NASA's Curiosity uses a similar rocker-bogie + motorized wheel architecture).

Understanding EPW control = understanding the foundation of **all modern battery-electric mobile robotics**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Back-EMF and Speed Regulation of an EPW Motor

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):** *"A 24 V DC PMDC motor in a power wheelchair has an armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and motor constant $k = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$. When the wheelchair carries a 100 kg user on a level surface, the motor draws 10 A. Calculate the no-load speed, the loaded speed, and the developed torque."*

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the armature voltage equation:**

$$V_a = I_a \cdot R_a + E_b$$

where $E_b = k \cdot \omega$ is the back-EMF.

**Step 2 — Rearrange for angular velocity $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a \cdot R_a}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the loaded speed:**

$$\omega_{loaded} = \frac{24 - (10)(0.5)}{0.05} = \frac{24 - 5}{0.05} = \frac{19}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{loaded} = 380 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the no-load speed (set $I_a \approx 0$):**

$$\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_a}{k} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM for intuition:**

$$N_{loaded} = \omega_{loaded} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 380 \times 9.549 \approx 3628 \text{ RPM}$$

$$N_{no\text{-}load} = 480 \times 9.549 \approx 4583 \text{ RPM}$$

> *Note: After the 30:1 planetary gearbox, wheel RPM is $\approx 121 \text{ RPM}$, suitable for wheelchair speeds of ~6 km/h.*

**Step 6 — Calculate developed torque using $T = k_t \cdot I_a$** (for PMDC, $k_t = k$ in SI units):

$$T = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ N·m (at motor shaft)}$$

$$T_{wheel} = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ N·m (after gearbox)}$$

**Step 7 — Verify power balance:**

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 0.5 \times 380 = 190 \text{ W (mechanical, at motor shaft)}$$

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I = 24 \times 10 = 240 \text{ W (electrical input)}$$

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{190}{240} = 79.2\% \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Range Estimation

**Problem Statement:** *"A 24 V, 50 Ah battery powers two 200 W motors in a power wheelchair. The controller efficiency is 90%. If the user travels at an average of 6 km/h on level ground with 70% motor loading, calculate the maximum range in km."*

**Step 1 — Energy stored in battery:**

$$E_{batt} = V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} = 24 \times 50 = 1200 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 2 — Useful power at wheels (account for loading and efficiency):**

$$P_{load} = 2 \times 200 \times 0.70 = 280 \text{ W (gross mechanical demand)}$$

$$P_{draw} = \frac{P_{load}}{\eta_{controller}} = \frac{280}{0.90} \approx 311.1 \text{ W (battery drain)}$$

**Step 3 — Operational time:**

$$t = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{draw}} = \frac{1200}{311.1} = 3.857 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Range using $R = v \cdot t$:**

$$R = 6 \text{ km/h} \times 3.857 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 23.14 \text{ km}}$$

> *This matches the KTU textbook range of 15–40 km for typical EPWs — our calculation is mid-range and physically realistic.*

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Climbing Ability

**Problem Statement:** *"A power wheelchair + user has total mass $m = 120 \text{ kg}$. The two drive motors combined deliver 30 N·m at the wheels. Wheel radius $r = 0.2 \text{ m}$. What is the steepest gradient (in degrees) the chair can climb without slipping, assuming coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.7$?"*

**Step 1 — Maximum tractive force before wheel slip:**

$$F_{max,slip} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g = 0.7 \times 120 \times 9.81 = 823.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Force available from motors:**

$$F_{motor} = \frac{2T_{wheel}}{r} = \frac{2 \times 30}{0.2} = 300 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — The motor force is the limiting factor** (since 300 < 823.6). Set:

$$F_{motor} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$\sin\theta = \frac{300}{120 \times 9.81} = \frac{300}{1177.2} = 0.2549$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.2549)$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 14.77° \text{ (i.e., ~26% gradient)}}$$

> *In practice, KTU standard EPWs are rated for 6°–12° inclines to include a safety margin against tipping and motor overload.*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drive Configuration Comparison

| Feature | RWD | MWD | FWD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turning Radius** | Large | Smallest | Medium |
| **Stability on Slopes** | Best (drive weight on slope) | Moderate | Poor (front lifts) |
| **Obstacle Climbing** | Moderate | Best (curbs) | Good |
| **High-Speed Handling** | Stable | Less stable (center CG) | Tail-wag |
| **Indoor Maneuverability** | Poor | **Excellent** | Good |
| **Outdoor Stability** | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| **KTU Exam "Best Indoor Choice"** | ❌ | ✅ **Correct** | ❌ |

## 3.5 Key PID Control Equation for EPW Drive

The closed-loop speed control follows:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $e(t) = v_{ref}(t) - v_{actual}(t)$ is the speed error and $u(t)$ is the PWM duty-cycle command to the H-bridge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — EPW Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input - Joystick SipPuff HeadArray] --> B[Analog Signal Conditioner]
    B --> C[Microcontroller DSP PID Controller]
    C --> D[PWM H-Bridge Driver]
    D --> E[Left DC Motor]
    D --> F[Right DC Motor]
    E --> G[Left Wheel Encoder]
    F --> H[Right Wheel Encoder]
    G --> C
    H --> C
    I[24V or 48V LiFePO4 Battery Pack] --> D
    I --> C
    J[Spring Applied Electromagnetic Brake] --> E
    J --> F
    K[Fault Detection Module] --> C
    L[Battery Management System BMS] --> I
    M[Display Panel Speedometer Battery] --> C
```

> *This is a closed-loop servo architecture: encoders feed back actual wheel speeds to the controller, which adjusts PWM signals to maintain the desired velocity despite load variations (ramps, bumps, user shift).*

## 4.2 State Machine — EPW Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> DriveForward: Joystick Y axis positive
    Standby --> DriveReverse: Joystick Y axis negative
    Standby --> TurnLeft: Joystick X axis negative
    Standby --> TurnRight: Joystick X axis positive
    DriveForward --> TurnLeft: Combined X and Y input
    DriveForward --> DriveReverse: Y axis crosses zero
    DriveForward --> Standby: Joystick returns to center
    TurnLeft --> Standby: Joystick returns to center
    DriveReverse --> Standby: Joystick returns to center
    Standby --> FaultMode: Overcurrent or low battery
    FaultMode --> Standby: Reset button
    DriveForward --> FaultMode: Tilt sensor triggers
```

```mermaid
    P1[24V 50Ah LiFePO4 Battery] --> Q1[Fuse 40A]
    Q1 --> R1[DC-DC Buck Converter 24V to 12V]
    Q1 --> R2[DC-DC Buck Converter 24V to 5V]

    R1 --> S2[Right Motor Driver H-Bridge]
    R2 --> S3[Controller Microcontroller]
    R2 --> S4[Joystick Sensors]
    R2 --> S5[LCD Display]
    S1 --> M1[Left PMDC Motor 200W]
    S2 --> M2[Right PMDC Motor 200W]
    M1 --> W1[Left Drive Wheel]
    M2 --> W2[Right Drive Wheel]
```
