# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three input devices used to control an electric power wheelchair by users with severe physical disabilities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(1) Sip-and-Puff Control (1 mark):** A pneumatic switch activated by the user's sip (inhale) or puff (exhale) into a straw-like tube. Used by individuals with C3–C4 spinal cord injuries who retain breath control but lack hand/arm movement. Sips typically control one direction (e.g., forward), puffs another (e.g., reverse), with harder sips/puffs selecting modes.

**(2) Chin Control (1 mark):** A miniature joystick mounted near the chin on a swing-away armrest bracket. The user manipulates it with chin movements. Suitable for high-level quadriplegics retaining head/neck mobility.

**(3) Head Array (1 mark):** A set of proximity switches embedded in the headrest and lateral supports. The user activates switches by tilting the head left, right, or backward against pads containing microswitches or force-sensitive resistors.

> *(Alternative accepted answers: switch/scanning interface, voice recognition, eye-gaze tracker, tongue-driven systems.)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) and Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD) power wheelchairs in terms of stability, turning radius, and indoor maneuverability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | RWD | MWD |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | High (drive mass over rear wheels prevents front lift on acceleration) | Moderate (center of gravity near pivot point) |
| **Turning Radius** | Large (chair pivots around a point behind user) | Smallest (rotates almost within its own footprint) |
| **Indoor Maneuverability** | Poor (cannot navigate tight corners or doorways easily) | Excellent (preferred for indoor use) |

**Conclusion (0 marks, optional):** *MWD is preferred for indoor/domestic environments; RWD is preferred for outdoor and high-speed use.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### **(a)** Describe the major components of an electric power wheelchair with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of the controller and explain the use of PWM for motor speed control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An electric power wheelchair (EPW) is a battery-powered mobility device designed for individuals unable to self-propel a manual wheelchair. It converts a small user input (e.g., joystick deflection) into controlled, safe motion of a 75–150 kg system.

**Major Components (4 marks):**

1. **Battery Pack (1/2 mark):** Provides DC power. Modern EPWs use 24V or 48V sealed lead-acid (SLA) or lithium-ion (LiFePO₄) batteries, rated 30–75 Ah.

2. **Drive Motors (1/2 mark):** Two 24V (or 48V) Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motors, each rated 150–400 W, coupled to drive wheels via planetary gearboxes (typical ratio 25:1 to 40:1).

3. **Controller (1 mark):** The brain of the EPW. Microcontroller-based (often ARM Cortex or DSP), receives joystick analog signals, applies PID control, and generates PWM signals to drive the H-bridge MOSFET/IGBT power stage. Also handles battery monitoring, fault detection, tilt sensing, and brake control.

4. **Joystick Module (1/2 mark):** A two-axis Hall-effect device producing 0–5V analog voltages corresponding to X (turn) and Y (forward/reverse) deflection. Often programmable for speed, acceleration, and tremor smoothing.

5. **Brake System (1/2 mark):** Spring-applied, electrically-released electromagnetic disc brake mounted on each motor. Engages automatically when joystick is centered or battery is disconnected (fail-safe).

6. **Wheels, Frame, and Seat (1/2 mark):** Two drive wheels (pneumatic or solid) + two/four caster wheels. Tubular steel/aluminium frame. Standard sling seat, captain's chair, or rehab seat with powered tilt/recline/elevate.

**PWM for Speed Control (2 marks):**

The controller cannot efficiently vary motor voltage using a linear regulator (wasteful heat dissipation). Instead, it switches the full battery voltage across the motor at high frequency (typically 20–25 kHz, above audible range) using an **H-bridge of MOSFETs**. The fraction of time the switches are ON is the **duty cycle (D)**.

$$V_{avg,motor} = D \times V_{batt}$$

When the joystick is fully forward, D = 100% (full speed). When slightly forward, D = 30% (slow speed). Because the motor's inductance smooths the pulsed current, the motor experiences an effective DC voltage proportional to D, achieving smooth, efficient speed control with minimal heat loss (efficiency >90%).

---

#### **(b)** A power wheelchair uses two 24 V DC motors each rated 250 W. The battery is rated 24 V, 60 Ah. Assuming a controller efficiency of 88% and average motor loading of 65%, calculate: **(i)** the loaded current drawn from the battery at rated power, **(ii)** the operational time on level ground, and **(iii)** the maximum range at 6 km/h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{batt} = 24$ V
- $C_{Ah} = 60$ Ah
- Motor power rating = 250 W each
- $\eta_{controller} = 0.88$
- Motor loading = 65%
- $v = 6$ km/h

**(i) Loaded current from battery (3 marks):**

Mechanical power required from both motors at 65% loading:
$$P_{mech} = 2 \times 250 \times 0.65 = 325 \text{ W}$$

Electrical power drawn from battery (accounting for controller efficiency):
$$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{controller}} = \frac{325}{0.88} = 369.32 \text{ W}$$

Loaded current:
$$I = \frac{P_{elec}}{V_{batt}} = \frac{369.32}{24} = 15.39 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 15.4 \text{ A}}$$

**(ii) Operational time (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{C_{Ah}}{I} = \frac{60}{15.4} = 3.896 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.9 \text{ hours}}$$

**(iii) Maximum range (2 marks):**

$$R = v \times t = 6 \text{ km/h} \times 3.896 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 23.4 \text{ km}}$$

> *This result lies within the standard EPW range of 15–40 km, validating our assumptions.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### **(a)** Explain the different drive configurations of electric power wheelchairs (RWD, MWD, FWD). Compare their relative advantages and disadvantages for indoor and outdoor use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Electric power wheelchairs are classified by the position of the drive wheels relative to the user's center of gravity. This seemingly simple design choice profoundly affects maneuverability, stability, and obstacle handling.

**Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) (2 marks):**
- Drive wheels located behind the user; large front caster wheels lead.
- **Advantages:** Excellent straight-line stability at high speed; drive weight provides traction on inclines; predictable handling for outdoor use.
- **Disadvantages:** Large turning radius (pivots around a point ~1 m behind the chair), making indoor navigation around furniture and doorways difficult. Tail-wag effect at high speed.

**Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD) (2 marks):**
- Drive wheels located directly under the user's center of gravity; front and rear caster wheels for support (six-wheel design).
- **Advantages:** Smallest turning radius; rotates nearly within its own footprint, ideal for tight indoor spaces. Excellent curb climbing because the chair "steps over" obstacles.
- **Disadvantages:** Slightly less stable at high speeds (center-pivot tendency); "high-center" feeling can cause anterior-posterior pitching on uneven terrain.

**Front-Wheel Drive (FWD) (2 marks):**
- Drive wheels at the front; rear caster wheels follow.
- **Advantages:** Excellent obstacle climbing (drives *over* curbs); good downhill stability since drive motors are on the descending end. Tight indoor turning.
- **Disadvantages:** Front wheels can lift under aggressive acceleration; unusual "tail-following" sensation for first-time users. Less efficient on long flat surfaces.

**Comparison Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Use Case | Best Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Indoor (home/office) | MWD | Smallest turning radius |
| Outdoor (sidewalks) | RWD | Stability and speed |
| Mixed / Curb-heavy | FWD | Best obstacle climbing |
| Long-distance outdoor | RWD | Best efficiency at constant speed |

---

#### **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the closed-loop speed control of a power wheelchair using PID control. A 24 V EPW motor is commanded to a speed of 100 rad/s. After reaching steady state, the speed drops to 92 rad/s when a 10° incline is encountered. The controller then adjusts the PWM duty cycle from 60% to 68% to restore speed. Determine the proportional gain $K_p$ of the controller (assume pure P-control). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The closed-loop speed control of an EPW is implemented as follows:

```
Reference Speed (ωref)
        │
        ▼
   ┌─────────┐
   │   e(t)  │  ← Comparator
   │ =ωref-ω │────┐
   └─────────┘    │
        ▲         ▼
        │   ┌──────────┐
        │   │ P  Ctrl  │  Kp·e(t) → PWM command
        │   │ (or PID) │────┐
        │   └──────────┘    │
        │                   ▼
        │            ┌──────────┐
        │            │ H-Bridge │
        │            │ (PWM)    │
        │            └────┬─────┘
        │                 ▼
        │            ┌──────────┐
        │            │ DC Motor │
        │            │ + Load   │
        │            └────┬─────┘
        │                 ▼
        │            ┌──────────┐
        └────────────│ Encoder  │ (Feedback)
                     └──────────┘
```

- The reference speed $\omega_{ref}$ comes from the joystick Y-axis (mapped to a speed value).
- The encoder on the motor shaft measures actual speed $\omega_{actual}$.
- The comparator computes error $e = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}$.
- The P-controller computes $u = K_p \cdot e$ → which sets the PWM duty cycle.
- The PWM signal drives the H-bridge, which powers the DC motor.
- Increased PWM duty increases average voltage → increases speed → reduces error.
- Integral term (in PID) eliminates steady-state error; derivative term damps oscillations.

**Proportional Gain Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\omega_{ref} = 100$ rad/s
- $\omega_{actual} = 92$ rad/s
- Initial duty cycle $D_1 = 60\% = 0.60$
- New duty cycle $D_2 = 68\% = 0.68$
- Pure P-control: $u = K_p \cdot e$

**Step 1 — Compute the steady-state error:**

$$e = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual} = 100 - 92 = 8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in control output (ΔD in percentage):**

$$\Delta u = D_2 - D_1 = 0.68 - 0.60 = 0.08 \text{ (i.e., 8\% change in duty cycle)}$$

**Step 3 — Relate Δu to error using $u = K_p \cdot e$:**

$$\Delta u = K_p \cdot \Delta e$$

At steady state, the error stabilized at 8 rad/s, and the controller settled at 68%. The duty cycle is proportional to error:

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta e} = \frac{0.08}{8} = 0.01 \text{ per rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 0.01 \text{ (dimensionless duty-cycle per rad/s error)}}$$

> *Physical interpretation: For every 1 rad/s of speed error, the controller increases PWM duty by 1%. To fully correct a 100% error (from 0 to 100 rad/s), it would demand 100% duty cycle — which is the saturation limit of the controller.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up MWD and RWD turning radii** — MWD has the *smallest*; RWD has the *largest*. Marks lost for the opposite.
> 2. **Forgetting controller efficiency in range calculations** — Always include $\eta_{controller}$ when computing battery drain. Marks deducted if range comes out unrealistically high (>50 km).
> 3. **Confusing armature voltage equation** — Remember $V = IR + k\omega$, not $V = IR - k\omega$. The back-EMF opposes applied voltage.
> 4. **Using wrong gear ratio units** — If motor speed is in RPM, divide by 60 to convert to rad/s, or use $N = 60\omega / 2\pi$.
> 5. **PWM equation mistakes** — $V_{avg} = D \times V_{batt}$, where D is a *decimal* (0.6), not a percentage.
> 6. **Skipping units in PID derivation** — Always quote $K_p$ with units (e.g., duty-cycle per rad/s, or V·s/rad).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electric Power Wheelchairs (Module 3)**

### ✅ Definitions
- EPW = battery-powered wheelchair propelled by electric motors, controlled by user via electronic input.
- RESNA/ISO 7176 classifies EPWs into Class A (indoor), B (mixed), C (outdoor).

### ✅ Drive Configurations
- **RWD** = best outdoor stability, largest turning radius.
- **MWD** = smallest turning radius, best indoor, six-wheel design.
- **FWD** = best curb climbing, drive *over* obstacles.

### ✅ Core Components
1. Battery (24V/48V, SLA or LiFePO₄)
2. Two PMDC motors with planetary gearboxes
3. Microcontroller-based PID controller
4. H-bridge MOSFET/IGBT power stage
5. Hall-effect joystick (or alternative input)
6. Spring-applied electromagnetic brake (fail-safe)
7. Encoders for closed-loop feedback
8. Battery Management System (BMS)

### ✅ Key Equations
- Motor voltage: $V = I_a R_a + k\omega$
- Motor torque: $T = k_t I_a$
- Power: $P = T\omega$
- Battery energy: $E = V \times C_{Ah}$
- PWM average: $V_{avg} = D \times V_{batt}$
- Range: $R = v \times (C_{Ah}/I_{draw})$
- PID law: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \, de/dt$
- Climbing force: $F = mg\sin\theta$

### ✅ Control Methods (for severely disabled users)
- Standard joystick → Chin control → Sip-and-puff → Head array → Switch/scanning → Eye-gaze → Voice/Brain-computer interface

### ✅ Safety & Standards
- Spring-applied brakes (fail-safe).
- Anti-tip wheels standard on MWD/FWD chairs.
- ISO 7176, RESNA WC standards apply.
- Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) compliance required.
- Tilt sensors and tip-over alarms mandatory on powered tilt/recline chairs.

### ✅ Typical Performance Range (must memorize)
- Speed: 6–10 km/h
- Range: 15–40 km
- Gradient: 6°–12°
- Battery: 24V/48V, 30–75 Ah
- Motor: 150–400 W each

> **Final Tip:** KTU exam questions on this topic are predominantly **numerical** (PWM, range, torque) and **comparison-based** (RWD vs MWD vs FWD). Master the formula sheet and drive-configuration comparison table above — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 3 paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multifunctional Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multifunctional Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multifunctional Wheelchair (MFW)** is an advanced mobility-assistive device that integrates two or more functional capabilities — beyond conventional manual transport — such as powered propulsion, postural adjustment, stair negotiation, environmental navigation, and physiological monitoring, all unified through a common mechatronic architecture governed by sensors, actuators, and an embedded controller.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OEBMT834 – Rehabilitation Engineering, Module 3), a multifunctional wheelchair is classified as a **Class-I Active Medical Device** under ISO 7176, designed to restore Activities of Daily Living (ADL) and Partial Participation (PP) for users with spinal cord injury (SCI), muscular dystrophy, cerebral palsy, polio, and geriatric mobility impairment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Multifunctional wheelchairs are intelligent, powered mobility platforms that augment basic wheeling with auxiliary functions: standing, reclining, tilt-in-space, stair climbing, and autonomous navigation. They are a core application of **Rehabilitation Mechatronics**.

## 1.2 The Five Functional Classes of MFW

| Class | Functional Core | Primary Clinical Benefit |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Standing Wheelchair | Powered lift to vertical posture | Pressure relief, bone density, digestion |
| Stair-Climbing Wheelchair | Tracked / legged / tri-wheel base | Architectural barrier access |
| Power-Assisted Wheelchair | Motorized hub or pushrim | Reduced upper-limb strain |
| Smart / Autonomous Wheelchair | Sensor-driven navigation | Cognitive/visual impairment support |
| Reclining / Tilt-in-Space Wheelchair | Backrest & seat plane actuators | Postural management, pressure care |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife on Wheels"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Plain English Explanation:**
> Think of a basic manual wheelchair as a simple wooden chair on roller-skates — it does *one* job well: rolling on a flat floor. A **Multifunctional Wheelchair** is the *Swiss Army knife* of mobility. Just as a Swiss Army knife has a blade, screwdriver, scissors, and corkscrew all in one handle, an MFW combines a seat, a lift, a stair-climber, and a brain (microcontroller) all in one frame. When a user presses a button, the chair can stand them up to reach a high shelf, tilt them back to relieve a pressure sore, climb three steps to enter a building, or even stop on its own when a wall is detected. It transforms the wheelchair from a *passive transport tool* into an *active rehabilitation partner*.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Step Height (Architectural):** **h_step = 150 mm to 200 mm** (per IS 4963 / ADA Standards)
- **Standard Doorway Clearance:** **D_door = 810 mm to 915 mm**
- **Maximum Safe Ramp Gradient (ADA):** **θ_ramp = 1:12 (≈ 4.76°)**
- **Adult Wheelchair User Mass Range:** **m_user = 40 kg to 120 kg**
- **Typical Powered Wheelchair Speed (Indoor):** **v = 0.5 to 1.5 m/s**
- **Standard Battery Voltage (Mobility):** **V = 24 V DC**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Questions on multifunctional wheelchairs often test whether the student can *quantify* a design — e.g., calculate the motor torque required to lift a 90 kg user against gravity through a linear actuator. Memorizing the constants above is essential for numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechatronic Architecture — Four Pillars

Every multifunctional wheelchair, irrespective of brand (Permobil, Pride, Karman, Sunrise Medical), is built on **four integrated mechatronic pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Mechanical Subsystem
- **Frame Material:** Aerospace-grade **6061-T6 aluminum alloy** (ρ ≈ 2.7 g/cm³) or **Ti-6Al-4V titanium** for premium models
- **Drive Configuration:** Rear-wheel drive (RWD), mid-wheel drive (MWD — 6-wheel), or front-wheel drive (FWD)
- **Actuators:** **Linear lead-screw actuators** (12 V/24 V DC) for seat lift, backrest recline, and legrest elevation

### Pillar 2 — Electrical / Power Subsystem
- **Battery:** Sealed Lead-Acid (SLA) or Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄), 24 V / 50 Ah typical
- **Drive Motor:** Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) or Brushless DC (BLDC), 200 W to 600 W per motor
- **Motor Controller:** PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) H-bridge, 50 A continuous

### Pillar 3 — Electronic / Control Subsystem
- **Microcontroller:** ARM Cortex-M3/M4 (e.g., STM32F4) running at 72–168 MHz
- **Input Devices:** Joystick (analog, 2-axis), sip-and-puff, head-array, EMG switch
- **Sensors:** Ultrasonic (HC-SR04, 2–400 cm), IR proximity, IMU (MPU6050 — 6-axis), wheel encoders

### Pillar 4 — Software / Decision Subsystem
- **Control Algorithms:** PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative), fuzzy logic, sensor fusion (Kalman filter)
- **Safety Layer:** Watchdog timer, emergency stop (E-stop), tilt-detection cutoff

## 2.2 Why Each Pillar is Necessary — The "Why-How" Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Walkthrough — Why a Multifunctional Wheelchair Needs ALL Four Pillars:**
> 1. **Mechanical** → provides the *physical body* that supports the user and withstands dynamic loads (e.g., 2× body weight during stair impact).
> 2. **Electrical** → provides the *energy and muscle* (motors, actuators) to execute motion commands.
> 3. **Electronic** → provides the *senses and reflexes* (sensors detect obstacles, encoders measure speed).
> 4. **Software** → provides the *intelligence* (the microcontroller decides *when* to brake, lift, or steer).
> **If any pillar is missing**, the wheelchair degenerates into either a manual chair (no pillar 2, 3, 4) or a non-functional "powered brick" (no pillar 3, 4).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $P = F \cdot v$ | P = power, F = tractive force, v = linear velocity | W, N, m/s | Motor sizing for flat propulsion |
| 2 | $T = F \cdot r$ | T = torque, F = tangential force, r = wheel radius | N·m, N, m | Wheel motor torque demand |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = T \cdot \omega$ | ω = angular velocity (rad/s) | W, N·m, rad/s | Mechanical power output |
| 4 | $E_{batt} = V \cdot C \cdot \eta$ | E = energy, V = voltage, C = capacity (Ah), η = efficiency | Wh, V, Ah | Battery endurance estimation |
| 5 | $\theta_{tip} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h_{cg}}\right)$ | θ_tip = tipping angle, d = wheelbase/2, h_cg = height of CG | degrees | Static stability margin |
| 6 | $F_{lift} = m_{total} \cdot g$ | m_total = user+chair mass, g = 9.81 m/s² | N | Vertical actuator force |
| 7 | $F_{stair} = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g \cdot h_{step}}{L_{contact} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | h_step = riser height, L_contact = contact length, η_mech = mech. efficiency | N, m | Stair-climbing force |
| 8 | $a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{drive} - F_{roll}}{m_{total}}$ | a = acceleration, F_roll = rolling resistance (μ_r · m · g) | m/s² | Maximum acceleration limit |
| 9 | $v_{max} = \omega_{max} \cdot r$ | v_max = linear speed, ω_max = max motor RPM × 2π/60 | m/s | Top speed calculation |
| 10 | $I_{load} = \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ (solid disc) | I = moment of inertia | kg·m² | Rotational dynamics for acceleration |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** In the table above, the `vert` operator has been replaced with explicit parentheses `\left( \right)` and `\dfrac{}{}` to ensure the markdown table structure is *not* corrupted. This is the standard KTU-compliant rendering style.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multifunctional wheelchair technology directly transfers to:
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in industrial automation — same sensor fusion and PID control
- **Exoskeletons** — same actuator sizing and stability analysis
- **Mobile robots (AMR)** in warehouses — same obstacle detection and path planning
- **Electric vehicles (EVs)** — same battery management and BLDC motor control
- **Mars rovers** — same all-terrain and autonomous navigation principles

This cross-domain transferability is why KTU examiners frequently pose **design-by-analogy** questions on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stability Condition for a Standing Wheelchair

When a user transitions from seated to standing posture, the **center of gravity (CG)** of the combined human-chair system shifts vertically upward. The wheelchair must NOT tip forward or backward during this transition.

### Step 1 — Define the System Geometry

Let the wheelbase (distance between front and rear wheel contact points) be $2d$. Let $h_{cg}$ be the height of the system CG above the ground. Define the tipping axis as the line connecting the front and rear wheel contact patches.

### Step 2 — Write the Static Equilibrium

The wheelchair is in equilibrium when the **gravity vector (m·g, downward through CG)** falls **inside** the support polygon (the rectangle formed by the four wheel contact points).

### Step 3 — Identify the Critical Tipping Condition

Tipping occurs the instant the gravity vector passes *through* the front or rear wheel contact line. Geometrically, the tipping angle $\theta_{tip}$ is the angle at which the CG-projection-on-ground touches the wheelbase boundary.

### Step 4 — Apply Trigonometric Relation

From the right-triangle geometry formed by the CG projection, the wheelbase center, and the tipping contact line:

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \dfrac{d}{h_{cg}}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Critical Tipping Angle

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h_{cg}}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Given: a multifunctional wheelchair with wheelbase $2d = 600$ mm (so $d = 300$ mm = 0.3 m) and CG height $h_{cg} = 1100$ mm = 1.1 m in the **standing configuration**.

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.3}{1.1}\right) = \arctan(0.2727) = 15.26^\circ
$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The wheelchair can be tilted up to **15.26°** from vertical before it tips. Since standard ramps are only **4.76°** (1:12 ADA), the chair is **safe on all standard ramps** in the standing configuration. However, sudden dynamic loads (e.g., a bump) can momentarily shift the *effective* CG, reducing this margin — hence the need for **anti-tip wheels** at the rear.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Torque for Standing Wheelchair Lift Actuator

### Step 1 — State the Problem

A linear actuator must lift a combined mass (user + seat assembly) of $m_{total} = 110$ kg from seated to standing position in $t = 8$ seconds over a vertical lift of $H = 350$ mm.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Gravitational Force

$$
F_g = m_{total} \cdot g = 110 \times 9.81 = 1079.1 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Required Vertical Velocity

$$
v = \dfrac{H}{t} = \dfrac{0.35}{8} = 0.04375 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Steady-State Mechanical Power

$$
P_{mech} = F_g \cdot v = 1079.1 \times 0.04375 = 47.21 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 5 — Account for Mechanical Efficiency

The lead-screw actuator has typical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 0.35$ (35%, due to sliding friction). Therefore, the electrical input power required is:

$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{47.21}{0.35} = 134.9 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Motor Torque

For a lead-screw with pitch $p = 4$ mm/rev, the motor must rotate at speed:

$$
n = \dfrac{v}{p} = \dfrac{0.04375 \text{ m/s}}{0.004 \text{ m/rev}} = 10.94 \text{ rev/s} = 656.25 \text{ RPM}
$$

The motor torque required is:

$$
T = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{2\pi n} = \dfrac{134.9}{2 \pi \times 10.94} = 1.96 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 7 — Add Safety Factor

Apply a **2× safety factor** (industry standard for medical devices):

$$
T_{design} = 2 \times 1.96 = 3.93 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** A standard 24 V DC gearmotor rated at 5 N·m continuous torque is suitable. This is *why* most commercial standing wheelchairs use 24 V/60 W to 120 W DC gearmotors for the lift mechanism.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Endurance for a Smart Wheelchair

### Step 1 — Define Power Budget

Assume a power wheelchair with:
- Two drive motors: 200 W each (peak)
- One linear actuator: 120 W peak
- Controller + sensors: 20 W
- **Total average draw (cruising):** $P_{avg} = 250$ W

### Step 2 — State Battery Specification

Battery: 24 V, 50 Ah Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄), with depth-of-discharge (DoD) limit of 80% to preserve cycle life.

### Step 3 — Compute Usable Energy

$$
E_{usable} = V \times C \times DoD = 24 \times 50 \times 0.80 = 960 \text{ Wh}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Endurance Time

$$
t_{endurance} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{960}{250} = 3.84 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Range

At average speed $v = 1.0$ m/s:

$$
D_{range} = v \times t_{endurance} = 1.0 \times 3.84 \times 3600 = 13,824 \text{ m} \approx 13.8 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Relevance:** A 13.8 km range covers a full day of typical community use. This is the *engineering justification* for the 24 V / 50 Ah specification seen in commercial models like the Permobil M3 Corpus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MFW Sub-Types

| Parameter | Standing W/C | Stair-Climbing W/C | Smart W/C | Reclining W/C |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Primary Actuator | Linear (24V) | Track / leg | None (sensor) | Linear (back) |
| Control Input | Joystick + button | Mode selector | Joystick + AI | Tilt sensor |
| Power Budget | 250 W | 800 W | 150 W | 100 W |
| Stability Risk | **HIGH** (CG rise) | **MEDIUM** (pitch) | LOW | LOW |
| Battery Endurance | 3–4 h | 1.5–2 h | 6–8 h | 8+ h |
| ISO 7176 Class | Class A (indoor) | Class C (outdoor) | Class A/B | Class A |
| Typical Cost (₹ / USD) | ₹4L / $5K | ₹8L / $10K | ₹3L / $4K | ₹2L / $2.5K |

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example — Multi-Concept Problem

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 style):**
> A standing multifunctional wheelchair with total mass 120 kg (user + chair) must climb a 5° ramp. The wheel radius is 0.2 m, motor rated speed is 120 RPM, and the controller uses PWM at 24 V. Calculate: (i) the required tractive force, (ii) the motor torque, and (iii) the stability margin if the CG height is 0.9 m and wheelbase is 0.6 m.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Tractive Force on the Ramp**

The component of weight along the ramp is:

$$
F_{tractive} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) = 120 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5^\circ) = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 102.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance**

Assuming coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_r = 0.015$ (rubber tire on smooth surface):

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) = 0.015 \times 120 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5^\circ) = 17.59 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Tractive Force**

$$
F_{total} = F_{tractive} + F_{roll} = 102.6 + 17.59 = 120.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor Torque (per wheel, two-wheel drive)**

$$
T = F_{total} \cdot \dfrac{r}{2} = 120.2 \times \dfrac{0.2}{2} = 12.02 \text{ N·m per motor}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor Speed Check**

Motor rated speed: 120 RPM = $2\pi \times 120 / 60 = 12.57$ rad/s
Wheel speed: $\omega_{wheel} = 12.57$ rad/s
Chair speed: $v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r = 12.57 \times 0.2 = 2.51$ m/s (≈ 9 km/h, slightly above indoor safe limit of 6 km/h, so **gear reduction of 1.5:1 is recommended**)

**Step 6 — Stability Margin**

With $d = 0.3$ m, $h_{cg} = 0.9$ m:

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.3}{0.9}\right) = \arctan(0.333) = 18.43^\circ
$$

Since the ramp is only $5^\circ$ and tipping occurs at $18.43^\circ$, the **stability margin is $13.43^\circ$** — *ample safety*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often forget to *add* rolling resistance to the tractive force. The grade force alone is *not* enough — the total opposing force is the *sum* of grade force + rolling resistance + air drag (neglected at low speed). Marks are lost on this step.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Subsystem Interaction Matrix

| Subsystem | Mechanical | Electrical | Control | Software |
|-----------|------------|------------|---------|----------|
| Drive Motors | Wheel hubs, gears | BLDC, MOSFETs | PWM driver IC | PID speed loop |
| Lift Actuator | Lead-screw, frame | 24V DC motor | H-bridge relay | On/Off + limit switches |
| Sensors | Mounting brackets | 3.3V/5V supply | ADC, I2C, GPIO | Sensor fusion algorithm |
| Battery | Mounting tray | Cells, BMS | SOC monitoring | Coulomb counting |
| Safety Brake | Brake disc, spring | Solenoid | Relay driver | Watchdog supervision |
| User Interface | Joystick mount | Potentiometer | ADC input | Mode state machine |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**

**Q1.** Define a *Multifunctional Wheelchair*. List any **four** distinct functional classes with one clinical benefit of each.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **Multifunctional Wheelchair (MFW)** is a powered, mechatronically integrated mobility device that combines multiple assistive functions — such as standing, reclining, stair climbing, and autonomous navigation — within a single unified platform. **(1 mark for definition)**

The four functional classes are:

1. **Standing Wheelchair** — Clinical benefit: relieves sitting pressure, improves circulation and bone density. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Stair-Climbing Wheelchair** — Clinical benefit: provides architectural barrier access for community participation. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Power-Assisted Wheelchair** — Clinical benefit: reduces upper-limb strain and prevents repetitive-strain injuries. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Smart Wheelchair** — Clinical benefit: enables safe mobility for users with cognitive or visual impairment. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2.** Explain the role of **sensors** and **microcontroller** in a smart multifunctional wheelchair. Name two sensors commonly used.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The **microcontroller** is the central decision-making unit of the smart wheelchair. It receives input from the user (joystick, buttons), processes sensor data in real-time, executes control algorithms (PID, fuzzy logic), and generates command outputs to the motor drivers. **(1.5 marks)**

**Sensors** provide the wheelchair with *perception* of its environment, enabling safe navigation. They convert physical quantities (distance, tilt, speed) into electrical signals readable by the microcontroller. **(1 mark)**

Two commonly used sensors:
- **Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04):** obstacle detection in the 2–400 cm range
- **IMU (MPU6050):** measures tilt/roll/pitch for stability monitoring and anti-tip cutoff
**(0.5 mark for naming two)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Model]**

> **A multifunctional standing wheelchair has the following design data:**
> Total mass (user + chair) m = 110 kg, wheel radius r = 0.18 m, motor rated speed 100 RPM, wheelbase 2d = 0.6 m, CG height h_cg = 1.0 m (standing), battery 24 V / 40 Ah LiFePO₄, average power draw 200 W.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the maximum tractive force required to climb a 6° ramp. Also compute the motor torque per wheel (two-wheel drive). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the static stability tipping angle. Comment on whether the chair can safely climb a standard ADA ramp (1:12 = 4.76°). Compute the battery endurance in hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Tractive Force & Motor Torque (7 marks)

**Step 1: Tractive (Grade) Force**

$$
F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(6^\circ) = 110 \times 9.81 \times 0.1045 = 112.8 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 2: Rolling Resistance** (with $\mu_r = 0.015$)

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(6^\circ) = 0.015 \times 110 \times 9.81 \times 0.9945 = 16.10 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3: Total Tractive Force**

$$
F_{total} = F_{grade} + F_{roll} = 112.8 + 16.10 = 128.9 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 4: Torque per Wheel**

$$
T = F_{total} \times \dfrac{r}{2} = 128.9 \times \dfrac{0.18}{2} = 11.60 \text{ N·m per motor} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Apply 1.5× safety factor → design torque = **17.4 N·m**, well within the capability of a 24 V / 200 W BLDC gearmotor.

> **Part (a) Answer Box:**
> $F_{total} = 128.9$ N; $T_{per\_motor} = 11.6$ N·m

---

### Part (b) — Stability & Battery Endurance (7 marks)

**Step 1: Tipping Angle**

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h_{cg}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.3}{1.0}\right) = \arctan(0.3) = 16.70^\circ \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 2: Stability Comment**

Since $\theta_{ramp} = 6^\circ \ll 16.70^\circ = \theta_{tip}$, the chair has a **stability margin of 10.7°** and can safely climb the 6° ramp. The chair is also safe on the standard ADA ramp (4.76°) with a margin of **11.94°**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Battery Endurance**

Usable energy (80% DoD):

$$
E_{usable} = 24 \times 40 \times 0.80 = 768 \text{ Wh} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

Endurance:

$$
t_{endurance} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{768}{200} = 3.84 \text{ hours} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

> **Part (b) Answer Box:**
> $\theta_{tip} = 16.70^\circ$; safe on ADA ramp; endurance = **3.84 h**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **mechatronic architecture** of a smart multifunctional wheelchair. Label the four major subsystems and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A stair-climbing wheelchair uses a triangular track configuration. The chair plus user mass is 130 kg. Each step has a riser height of 0.15 m. The track contact length on the step is 0.4 m. Mechanical efficiency is 30%. Calculate the force required to negotiate one step. If the climb must be completed in 4 seconds, what is the average power output? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Mechatronic Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (description in text form for the answer sheet):**

```
[ USER INPUTS ] → [ MICROCONTROLLER ] → [ ACTUATOR DRIVERS ] → [ ACTUATORS / MOTORS ]
       ↑                     ↑                      ↓
       |              [ SENSORS ] ←——————————————— [ WHEELCHAIR FRAME ]
       ↓                     ↓
[ SAFETY WATCHDOG ]    [ FEEDBACK ]
```

**Four Major Subsystems:**

1. **Mechanical Subsystem (2 marks):** Frame (Al 6061-T6), wheels, tracks/legrests, linear actuators, anti-tip casters. Function: *provides physical structure and load-bearing capability*.

2. **Electrical / Power Subsystem (2 marks):** Battery (24V LiFePO₄), BMS, drive motors (BLDC), motor drivers (MOSFET H-bridge). Function: *converts stored chemical energy into mechanical motion*.

3. **Electronic Control Subsystem (1.5 marks):** ARM Cortex microcontroller, joystick, sensors (ultrasonic, IMU, encoder), emergency stop. Function: *gathers user intent and environment data*.

4. **Software / Decision Subsystem (1.5 marks):** PID controller, obstacle avoidance algorithm, mode state machine, battery SOC monitor. Function: *executes control logic and safety interlocks*.

---

### Part (b) — Stair-Climbing Force & Power (7 marks)

**Step 1: Stair-Climbing Force**

Using the formula:

$$
F_{stair} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot h_{step}}{L_{contact} \cdot \eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{130 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}{0.4 \times 0.30} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}
$$

$$
F_{stair} = \dfrac{191.295}{0.12} = 1594.1 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Energy Required per Step**

$$
W_{step} = m \cdot g \cdot h_{step} = 130 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 191.3 \text{ J} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3: Average Power Output (over 4 seconds)**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{W_{step}}{t} = \dfrac{191.3}{4} = 47.83 \text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

The mechanical power required at the *wheel rim* is 47.8 W. With 30% mechanical efficiency, the *electrical input power* is:

$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{47.83}{0.30} = 159.4 \text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

> **Part (b) Answer Box:**
> $F_{stair} = 1594.1$ N; $P_{mech} = 47.8$ W; $P_{elec} = 159.4$ W

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Grade Force with Total Tractive Force** (loses 1–2 marks): The grade component $mg\sin\theta$ is *only one part*; rolling resistance $F_{roll}$ must be added. **Always compute BOTH and SUM.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor** (loses 0.5–1 mark): KTU expects a 1.5× to 2× safety factor on motor torque/force for medical devices. Stating the *raw* value without a safety factor loses credit.
> 3. **Using °C instead of ° for angles, or radians in $\sin$** (loses 0.5 mark): Ensure the calculator is in **degree mode** when computing $\sin(6°)$ or $\cos(5°)$.
> 4. **Not specifying units** (loses 0.5 mark per quantity): A force written as "128.9" without "N" is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step** (loses 1 mark): A numerical answer with no engineering interpretation (e.g., "safe on ramp" or "endurance of ~4 h") is marked down. **Always conclude with a one-line design comment.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Must Memorize for KTU:**

- ☐ A **Multifunctional Wheelchair** integrates ≥ 2 functions (standing, climbing, smart-nav, recline) on a single powered platform.
- ☐ **Four Pillars of Mechatronic Architecture:** Mechanical, Electrical, Electronic, Software.
- ☐ **Standard Battery:** 24 V DC, SLA or LiFePO₄, 40–75 Ah typical.
- ☐ **Standard Drive Motor:** BLDC, 200–600 W per motor, 24 V.
- ☐ **Key Control Algorithm:** PID for speed, Fuzzy Logic for obstacle avoidance, Kalman Filter for sensor fusion.
- ☐ **Standard Sensors:** Ultrasonic (HC-SR04), IR Proximity, IMU (MPU6050), Wheel Encoders.
- ☐ **Stability Tipping Angle Formula:** $\theta_{tip} = \arctan(d / h_{cg})$
- ☐ **Tractive Force on Ramp:** $F = mg\sin\theta + \mu_r mg\cos\theta$
- ☐ **Motor Torque:** $T = F \cdot r$ (for wheel) or $T = P/\omega$
- ☐ **Battery Energy:** $E = V \times Ah$ (in Wh); apply **80% DoD** for usable energy.
- ☐ **Standard ADA Ramp:** 1:12 gradient = **4.76°**.
- ☐ **Standard Step Riser:** 150–200 mm.
- ☐ **ISO 7176 Series:** Safety and performance standard for all wheelchairs (electrical, mechanical, climatic).
- ☐ **Safety Factor:** Always apply **1.5× to 2×** on motor/actuator sizing for medical devices.
- ☐ **Anti-Tip Wheels:** Mandatory on standing wheelchairs — they provide a 50–100 mm rear safety margin.
- ☐ **Degrees vs Radians:** KTU expects angles in **degrees** unless explicitly asked otherwise.
- ☐ **Stair-Climbing Force:** $F = (mgh_{step}) / (L_{contact} \cdot \eta)$ — efficiency typically **30%** for tracked systems.
- ☐ **Clinical Benefits of Standing:** Pressure relief, bone density preservation, improved digestion, psychological well-being, social eye-level interaction.
- ☐ **Clinical Benefits of Tilt-in-Space:** Shear reduction, postural management for users with poor trunk control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Module 3 questions on Multifunctional Wheelchairs almost always combine (1) a definition/classification question, (2) a block diagram question, and (3) a numerical problem involving stability, tractive force, or battery endurance. Master all three formats and you will secure full marks on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Limb Prosthetic Devices: Components of the Upper Limb Prosthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Major Limb Prosthetic Devices: Components of the Upper Limb Prosthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upper Limb Prosthesis:** An externally applied artificial device that replaces all or part of a missing upper extremity (from finger level up to shoulder disarticulation), designed to restore functional prehension (grasping), manipulation, and cosmetic appearance, while accommodating the unique biomechanical and neurological demands of the human arm.

A complete upper limb prosthesis is a **modular assembly** of discrete functional sub-systems. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, the principal components are:

| S.No. | Component | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Terminal Device (TD)** | The "end-effector" – replaces the hand (cosmetic glove, mechanical hand, or hook) |
| 2 | **Wrist Unit** | Connects TD to forearm; provides rotation and quick interchange |
| 3 | **Forearm Shell / Socket** | Distributes load from residual limb to prosthesis |
| 4 | **Elbow Unit** | Provides flexion–extension at the elbow (if amputation is above-elbow) |
| 5 | **Upper-Arm Shell / Humeral Section** | Houses the elbow mechanism for trans-humeral prostheses |
| 6 | **Shoulder Saddle / Socket** | Distributes weight over shoulder girdle (for shoulder disarticulation) |
| 7 | **Suspension Harness** | Anchors the prosthesis to the body via straps and cables |
| 8 | **Control System** | Body-powered cables, myoelectric electrodes, switches, or hybrid controllers |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Construction Crane:**
> Think of an upper limb prosthesis as a **miniature, body-worn construction crane**:
> - The **socket** is the *crane's base* (transmits all loads to the ground → here, to the residual limb).
> - The **harness + cable** is the *operator pulling levers* (body-powered control).
> - The **elbow joint** is the *boom-lifting hydraulic ram*.
> - The **wrist** is the *slewing ring* (rotational joint).
> - The **terminal device** is the *hook or grabber* at the end of the boom.
> - The **sensors (EMG electrodes)** are like the *remote-control buttons* of a modern crane — they tell the motors when to lift, rotate, or release.
>
> Just as a crane is only as good as its weakest joint, a prosthesis is judged by the seamless integration of **all its components** into one coordinated kinematic chain.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Numerical Standards)

- **Prehension Force (Functional Hook):** **50 N to 100 N** for adult ADLs (Activities of Daily Living).
- **Cable Operating Force (Body-Powered):** Typically **20 N to 50 N** by the contralateral shoulder.
- **Myoelectric Signal Threshold:** **20 µV to 200 µV** (RMS) for surface EMG detection.
- **Body-Powered Cable Efficiency (η):** **40 % to 80 %** depending on routing and friction.
- **Working Length of Cable (Functional):** Approximately **50 cm** for shoulder disarticulation.
- **Body-Powered ROM Ratio (3:1 or 7:1):** A standard excursion ratio used to multiply limited body motion.

## 1.4 Classification of Upper Limb Amputation Levels (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Always define the level of amputation first, because **the component list and the control strategy are 100 % determined by it.**
>
> - **Trans-radial (Below-Elbow, BE)** — preserves elbow → simpler components, no elbow unit needed.
> - **Trans-humeral (Above-Elbow, AE)** — no elbow → requires elbow + forearm lift mechanism.
> - **Shoulder Disarticulation (SD)** — entire humerus missing → requires shoulder + elbow + TD.
> - **Forequarter (Interscapulothoracic)** — entire shoulder girdle missing → most complex, body-powered control extremely difficult.
> - **Wrist Disarticulation** — preserves forearm rotation → can use "short" or "long" trim lines.
> - **Partial Hand / Finger** — cosmetic or task-specific prostheses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Terminal Device (TD) — The "Business End" of the Prosthesis

The TD is the only component that actually contacts objects, so its design is the most critical for the user's functional outcome.

### 2.1.1 Two Principal TD Geometries

**(a) The Hook (Functional / Working TD)**
- Two or three opposed steel fingers (Dorrance, Hosmer, TRS brands).
- Lightweight (**~ 70 g to 200 g**), high visibility of the object being grasped.
- Provides **pinch grip** and **hook grip** for tools, keys, utensils.
- Standard force rating: **22 N (adult), 9 N (child)** voluntary-opening.

**(b) The Mechanical / Cosmetic Hand**
- Foam-filled latex glove over an internal mechanism.
- Voluntary-opening (VO) or Voluntary-Closing (VC) modes.
- Voluntary-Closing offers *graded* grip — the harder the user pulls, the harder the grasp → allows the user to **"feel" the grip force kinesthetically** (called *stereognosis*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Voluntary Opening (VO):** TD is closed at rest; user pulls cable to OPEN. A rubber-band or spring provides the gripping force. Simple, reliable, but grip force is **fixed** (cannot modulate).
>
> **Voluntary Closing (VC):** TD is open at rest; user pulls cable to CLOSE. The user's own pull IS the grip force. **Grip force is proportional to cable tension** — superior for fragile objects (egg, paper cup).

### 2.1.2 Functional Assessment Criteria for a TD

A TD is evaluated on the **"Three Fs"**:
1. **Function** — pinch force, pinch precision, speed of operation.
2. **Feedback** — visual + proprioceptive cues transmitted to the user.
3. **Form** — cosmetics, weight, durability.

## 2.2 The Wrist Unit

The wrist unit connects the TD to the distal end of the forearm shell. It must allow **pronation / supination** (rotation) and ideally **quick interchange** of TDs.

| Wrist Type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Friction Wrist** | Rotates against a friction washer | Most common; settable resistance |
| **Quick-Disconnect Wrist** | Bayonet or pin lock; pull to release | Allows swapping TD in < 5 s |
| **Locking Wrist** | Spring-loaded detent at 0°, 45°, 90° | Holds position under load |
| **Flexible / Multiplex Wrist** | Allows small off-axis angulation | Useful for bilateral amputees |
| **Powered (Electric) Wrist** | Motorised rotation, controlled by myoelectric signal | Used in advanced prostheses (e.g., Michelangelo, i-Limb) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wrist Disarticulation Trick:** When the amputation is at the wrist, the user retains the **distal radio-ulnar joint** → he/she can still actively pronate/supinate. The prosthetist uses a **"short" socket** that does NOT block this rotation, effectively *transferring* the natural rotation through the TD.

## 2.3 The Elbow Unit

Required for **trans-humeral (AE)** and **shoulder disarticulation (SD)** cases.

### 2.3.1 Mechanical Elbow (Body-Powered)
- **Single-axis hinge** or **polycentric (multi-axis)** mechanism.
- **Lifting Assist:** Internal spring (constant-force spring) or elastic strap that pre-loads the forearm in flexion, reducing the cable force the user must pull to lift the forearm against gravity.
- **Lock Mechanism:** A "step-up" lever or pull-cable that engages a ratchet so the elbow holds the chosen flexion angle (e.g., when carrying a briefcase).
- **Turntable:** Below the elbow, allowing the *forearm* to rotate about the humeral axis.

### 2.3.2 External-Power (Electric / Hydraulic) Elbow
- Driven by a **DC motor + harmonic-drive gear** or a **miniature hydraulic actuator**.
- Controlled by EMG, force-sensing resistor (FSR), or myo + switch hybrid.
- Typical lifting capacity: **1.5 kg to 5 kg** at full reach.

## 2.4 The Socket (Interface)

The socket is the **only** part of the prosthesis in continuous contact with the residual limb. Its design determines comfort, suspension, and force transmission.

- **Trans-radial:** Münster or Northwestern University-type self-suspending socket (anatomically contoured over the olecranon, medial/lateral epicondyles).
- **Trans-humeral:** Typically a "step-up" brimmed socket (proximal-medial brim captures the acromion).
- **Interface Materials:** Silicone liner, gel liner, pelite (foam), or hard laminate shell.

## 2.5 The Harness and Control Cable (Body-Powered Systems)

The **figure-of-8 or figure-of-9 harness** is the cornerstone of body-powered control.

- **Anchor point:** The **contralateral axilla** (opposite armpit) → provides the reaction force.
- **Inversion of control** (for above-elbow): the *same* cable that flexes the elbow ALSO operates the TD through a "fair-lead" cable split.
- **Materials:** Dacron-woven cord inside Teflon-lined tubing; nylon webbing for straps.

## 2.6 Control System Theory — The Three Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield — Master this triad:**

### 2.6.1 Body-Powered (Mechanical / Cable-Driven)
- **Principle:** Body movement → cable pull → mechanical action.
- **Pros:** Robust, cheap (~$2k–$5k), no batteries, immediate proprioceptive feedback.
- **Cons:** Requires ~ 5–7 cm of shoulder excursion; cosmetically bulky.
- **Mathematical model:** Force & excursion ratios governed by lever-arm geometry (see Section 3).

### 2.6.2 Externally Powered (Myoelectric)
- **Principle:** Surface EMG electrodes on antagonist muscle pair (e.g., biceps/triceps in AE) → signal drives motor.
- **Signal Processing Chain:** Detection → Amplification (gain 1000×) → Rectification → Smoothing (low-pass) → Threshold logic → Motor command.
- **Pros:** High grip force, no harness, good cosmetics.
- **Cons:** Expensive ($20k+), batteries, sweat affects electrodes, no proprioceptive feedback (sensation of force is absent).
- **Threshold:** Active muscle contraction must exceed **~ 20–200 µV RMS**.

### 2.6.3 Hybrid
- **Body-powered elbow + myoelectric TD** (or vice versa) — combines the heavy lifting of mechanical with the fine control of myo.
- Used for **high-level amputations** (SD, forequarter) where cable control is impractical for all joints.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Board-Friendly Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 equations — they cover ~ 80 % of KTU 2024 numerical questions on this module.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Variable | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Force Ratio** = Output Force / Input Force | $F_{out}/F_{in}$ | Mechanical advantage of the cable/lever system | $2:1$ to $7:1$ |
| 2 | **Excursion Ratio** = Output Motion / Input Motion | $x_{out}/x_{in}$ | How much TD moves per cm of body motion | $3:1$ to $7:1$ |
| 3 | **Cable Efficiency** | $\eta = F_{out}\cdot x_{out} / (F_{in}\cdot x_{in})$ | Energy delivered to TD vs. input at body | $0.40$ to $0.80$ |
| 4 | **EMG Threshold** | $V_{th}$ | Min signal to trigger motor ON | $20\text{ to }200\,\mu V$ |
| 5 | **Prehension Force** | $F_p$ | Maximum grip force of TD | $50\text{ to }100\,N$ |
| 6 | **Capstan (Belt-Friction) Equation for Cable Routing** | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$ | Friction loss over a curved cable path | $\mu = 0.1$–$0.3$, $\theta$ in rad |

> **Capstan Equation note:** $F_1$ is the tension on the taut (user-pull) side, $F_2$ on the slack side, $\mu$ the coefficient of friction between cable and housing, $\theta$ the total wrap angle in radians. **Always use radians, never degrees.**

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These component principles extend far beyond prosthetics:
- **Cable-driven robotics** (Surgical snake robots, haptic interfaces) → same Bowden-cable dynamics.
- **EMG-based HCI** (controlling cursors, wheelchairs, drones) → identical myoelectric front-end.
- **Body-powered exoskeletons** for industrial workers → same harness/cable force-multiplication logic.
- **Capstan equation** governs automotive belt drives, traction drives in CVTs, winch systems, and even fishing-reel drag systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Ratio in a Body-Powered TD Lever

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A voluntary-opening terminal device (e.g., a Hosmer Dorrance hook) uses a lever arm to multiply the user's cable force $F_{in}$ into an output pinch force $F_{out}$. Derive the force ratio if the cable is attached $a = 3 \text{ cm}$ from the pivot and the fingers contact the object $b = 9 \text{ cm}$ from the pivot.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the mechanical system.** We model the hook as a class-1 lever rotating about a fixed pivot. Two forces act on the lever:
- The cable force $F_{in}$ applied perpendicular to the lever at distance $a$ from the pivot.
- The object's reactive force $F_{out}$ applied perpendicular to the lever at distance $b$ from the pivot.

**Step 2 — Apply static equilibrium (rotational).** For the lever not to rotate, the sum of moments about the pivot must be zero:

$$F_{in} \cdot a - F_{out} \cdot b = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $F_{out}$:**

$$F_{out} \cdot b = F_{in} \cdot a$$

$$F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \frac{a}{b}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values** ($a = 3 \text{ cm}$, $b = 9 \text{ cm}$, $F_{in} = 40 \text{ N}$):

$$\text{Force Ratio} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$F_{out} = 40 \times \frac{1}{3} = 13.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.** The lever **sacrifices force for speed** (excursion). If instead the cable were attached at $b$ and the fingers at $a$, the ratio would flip to $3:1$ — a *force multiplier* but a *speed divider*. **This is the fundamental force-excursion trade-off** in all body-powered systems.

**Step 6 — Boundary check.** For $a = b$, force ratio = 1, excursion ratio = 1 → a 1:1 "neutral" linkage used in some VC terminal devices.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Excursion Ratio & Cable Efficiency for a Trans-Humeral Prosthesis

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A trans-humeral amputee uses a body-powered prosthesis with a Bowden cable to operate a hook. When the contralateral shoulder moves $7 \text{ cm}$, only $2.8 \text{ cm}$ of cable is delivered to the TD. The user pulls with $35 \text{ N}$ of force, and the measured grip force is $50 \text{ N}$. Calculate: (a) Excursion ratio, (b) Force ratio, (c) Overall cable efficiency.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Excursion Ratio.**

$$\text{Excursion Ratio} = \frac{x_{out}}{x_{in}} = \frac{2.8 \text{ cm}}{7.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.40 = \frac{2}{5}$$

The system is **speed-reducing** (the TD moves less than the shoulder). This is the typical case when the body joint has a *larger* available excursion than needed.

**Step 2 — Force Ratio.**

$$\text{Force Ratio} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{50 \text{ N}}{35 \text{ N}} = 1.43$$

The system *amplifies* the user's force — a typical mechanical advantage.

**Step 3 — Overall Cable Efficiency.** Definition: useful work out ÷ work in.

$$\eta = \frac{F_{out} \cdot x_{out}}{F_{in} \cdot x_{in}} = \frac{50 \times 2.8}{35 \times 7.0} = \frac{140}{245} = 0.5714$$

$$\eta \approx 57.14\%$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.** About **43 % of the user's mechanical work is lost** — mainly to friction inside the Bowden cable sheath, hysteresis in the cable's Dacron fibres, and seal friction in the elbow. This is why KTU expects you to know that body-powered efficiencies of 40–80 % are *typical and acceptable*, not design failures.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capstan (Euler-Eytelwein) Equation for Cable Routing

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
A Bowden cable passes over a pulley whose wrap angle is $\theta = 180°$ (i.e., $\pi$ radians). The coefficient of friction between the cable and the housing is $\mu = 0.25$. The user pulls with $F_1 = 50 \text{ N}$ on the taut side. Find the tension $F_2$ on the TD side, and comment on the loss.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the Capstan equation.**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$$

where $F_2$ is the **larger** tension (the side resisting motion).

**Step 2 — Convert angle to radians.** $\theta = \pi \text{ rad} \approx 3.1416 \text{ rad}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\mu\theta = 0.25 \times 3.1416 = 0.7854$$

**Step 4 — Compute $e^{\mu\theta}$.**

$$e^{0.7854} \approx 2.193$$

**Step 5 — Compute $F_2$.**

$$F_2 = 50 \times 2.193 = 109.65 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The TD-side tension is **2.19 × larger** than the user-pull, *amplifying* force. The remaining 109.65 N is what reaches the TD. So the *net* efficiency through this single pulley bend is:

$$\eta_{pulley} = \frac{1}{e^{\mu\theta}} = \frac{1}{2.193} = 45.6\%$$

Each additional bend multiplies a new loss factor. **Designers minimise bends to keep $\eta_{system} > 0.50$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** "If the cable is reversed, which side is the input?" → The side **moving** under the user's pull is the *taut* side ($F_1$); the stationary side is $F_2$. Reverse the equation as $F_1 = F_2 / e^{\mu\theta}$ accordingly.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EMG Signal-to-Noise Chain

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement
Surface EMG electrode picks up a raw differential signal of $V_{raw} = 50 \text{ µV RMS}$, contaminated by $50 \text{ Hz}$ power-line noise of $V_n = 10 \text{ µV RMS}$. The amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 1000$ and a common-mode rejection ratio $\text{CMRR} = 100 \text{ dB}$. Compute the SNR at output.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the CMRR as a linear ratio.**

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20} = 10^{100/20} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective noise gain.** The noise is reduced by CMRR:

$$A_n = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}}} = \frac{1000}{100{,}000} = 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Output signal.**

$$V_{s,out} = V_{raw} \cdot A_d = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 0.050 \text{ V} = 50 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Output noise.**

$$V_{n,out} = V_n \cdot A_n = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01 = 0.1 \text{ µV} = 100 \text{ nV}$$

**Step 5 — Output SNR.**

$$\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{V_{s,out}}{V_{n,out}} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ µV}}{0.1 \text{ µV}} = 5 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5\times 10^5) \approx 113.98 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Good differential amplifier + high CMRR turns a noisy 5:1 input SNR into a 5×10⁵ output SNR — **essential for reliable threshold detection** in myoelectric controllers.

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Component-vs-Parameter Summary

| Component | Key Design Parameter | Governing Equation | Typical Numeric Range | Failure Mode if Mis-designed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TD (Hook)** | Pinch force, weight | $F_p = F_{in} \cdot (a/b)$ | $F_p$ = 22–100 N | Slippage of held objects |
| **Wrist** | Rotational friction torque | $T_f = \mu_r \cdot F_{clamp}$ | 0.5–2.0 N·m | TD spins under load |
| **Elbow (mech.)** | Lifting spring constant | $F_{lift} = k \cdot x$ | 5–15 N pre-load | Forearm "drops" under load |
| **Cable (Bowden)** | Working length, friction | Capstan equation | $\eta$ = 40–80 % | Sticking, jerky motion |
| **EMG Front-End** | CMRR, gain, threshold | $V_{out} = V_{raw} \cdot A_d$ | $A_d$ = 1000–10,000 | False triggering / dead control |
| **Socket** | Pressure on residual limb | $\sigma = F/A_{contact}$ | < 30 kPa (capillary closure) | Skin breakdown, ulceration |
