# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List and briefly define the major components of an upper-limb prosthesis for a trans-humeral amputee. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per point):**

1. **Socket / Interface** — The custom-fabricated shell that fits the residual limb and transmits all loads (weight, cable reaction, socket-suspension forces) to the user's body. (1 mark)
2. **Suspension and Harness** — A figure-of-8 or figure-of-9 webbing harness anchored over the contralateral shoulder, which suspends the prosthesis and provides the anchor for body-powered control cables. (1 mark)
3. **Elbow Unit + Forearm Shell + Wrist Unit + Terminal Device** — The articulating mechanical chain (elbow joint with lock, forearm lift assist, friction/quick-disconnect wrist, and hook or cosmetic hand as the end-effector) that restores prehension and manipulation. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between Voluntary-Opening (VO) and Voluntary-Closing (VC) terminal devices. State one advantage of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Voluntary-Opening (VO) | Voluntary-Closing (VC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Rest state** | Fingers CLOSED (held by spring/elastic) | Fingers OPEN |
| **User action** | Pulls cable to OPEN | Pulls cable to CLOSE |
| **Grip force** | Fixed by spring constant (cannot modulate) | Proportional to cable pull — graded |
| **Advantage** | Simpler, more reliable, lower cognitive load (1 mark) | Allows gentle grasp of fragile objects (egg, paper cup) (1 mark) |
| **Disadvantage** | Cannot modulate force | Requires sustained effort to hold grip |

The remaining mark is for correct tabulation and the opening definition line.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Describe the body-powered control system of an above-elbow prosthesis, with a neat sketch of the figure-of-8 harness. Explain how a single cable operates both the elbow lock and the terminal device. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The body-powered control of a trans-humeral (AE) prosthesis uses a **figure-of-8 harness** worn over the contralateral shoulder. The harness has two loops — an *anterior* (front) loop passing across the chest, and a *posterior* (back) loop passing across the scapula. The control cable is anchored at the **axilla** (armpit) of the sound side, travels through a **fair-lead** at the shoulder, runs inside a flexible Bowden sheath down the outside of the upper-arm socket, and is split by a **crossover lever** at the elbow.

The crossover lever is the key innovation: a **single cable controls two functions sequentially**. When the user abducts the contralateral shoulder by ~ 5 cm with the elbow *extended*, the cable first pulls the **elbow-lock release**, unlocking the elbow so it can flex. Continued shoulder abduction then flexes the elbow. Once the elbow is in the desired position, a small **forearm-lift spring** pre-loaded by the prosthetist holds the elbow against gravity. The user then **shifts the shoulder downward and forward** — this re-tensions the cable past the crossover, which now diverts the cable pull to the **terminal device**, closing (or opening, in VO) the TD. Thus, with one harness and one cable, two functions are operated by **two different body movements** (scapular abduction for elbow, shoulder depression/extension for TD).

*(Award 4 marks: 1 for harness sketch description, 2 for crossover-lever mechanism, 1 for the two distinct body movements.)*

**(b) A body-powered trans-humeral prosthesis delivers a pinch force of 65 N at the terminal device when the user pulls the cable with 30 N. The shoulder excursion is 6 cm and the corresponding TD opening is 2.4 cm. Calculate (i) Force Ratio, (ii) Excursion Ratio, (iii) Overall Efficiency. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Output force (pinch) $F_{out} = 65 \text{ N}$
- Input force (user pull) $F_{in} = 30 \text{ N}$
- Input excursion $x_{in} = 6 \text{ cm}$
- Output excursion $x_{out} = 2.4 \text{ cm}$

**(i) Force Ratio:**

$$\text{FR} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{65}{30} = 2.167 : 1$$

(2 marks for formula and substitution; 1 mark for final value)

**(ii) Excursion Ratio:**

$$\text{ER} = \frac{x_{out}}{x_{in}} = \frac{2.4}{6.0} = 0.40 = \frac{2}{5}$$

(1 mark)

**(iii) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{F_{out}\cdot x_{out}}{F_{in}\cdot x_{in}} = \frac{65 \times 2.4}{30 \times 6.0} = \frac{156}{180} = 0.8667$$

$$\eta = 86.67\%$$

(2 marks for correct substitution and arithmetic; 1 mark for stating the result with a comment — *e.g., "high efficiency indicates a well-routed cable with few bends and good lubrication"*.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of FR/ER** — they are dimensionless ratios. Do NOT write "N" or "cm" beside the number.
> 2. **Mixing up input/output** — re-read the question carefully; "pinch force" is the output (at the TD), "user pull" is the input.
> 3. **Not stating the result in percentage for efficiency** — always convert $\eta$ to % in the final answer.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment"** — KTU 2024 scheme, following NEP-2020 outcome-based education, rewards "interpretive" statements. Just writing numbers earns 6/7; a one-line interpretation earns the 7th mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) With a functional block diagram, describe the working of a myoelectrically controlled upper-limb prosthesis. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A myoelectrically controlled upper-limb prosthesis uses **surface EMG electrodes** placed on an **antagonistic muscle pair** of the residual limb (e.g., biceps and triceps in a trans-humeral case, or wrist flexors/extensors in a trans-radial case). The functional block diagram comprises the following stages:

1. **Signal Acquisition (Electrodes):** A pair of dry or gel electrodes differentially picks up the motor-unit action potentials of the contracted muscle. Typical RMS amplitude: **20 to 200 µV**. A third (ground) electrode establishes a common reference.

2. **Front-End Amplification:** A high-CMRR (≥ 80 dB) instrumentation amplifier boosts the differential signal by a factor $A_d \approx 1000$ to a usable range of **20 to 200 mV**, while rejecting 50 Hz mains noise.

3. **Rectification:** A full-wave rectifier converts the bipolar EMG to a unipolar signal. Used so that signal magnitude (not polarity) drives the next stage.

4. **Smoothing (Low-Pass Filter):** A 2nd-order Butterworth LPF with cutoff $f_c \approx 1$ Hz extracts the *envelope* of the rectified EMG. This envelope is the "muscle effort" signal.

5. **Threshold + Logic Stage:** A comparator checks if the envelope exceeds $V_{th}$ (e.g., 50 µV equivalent at input). If the *flexor* electrode crosses threshold → motor rotates to **close the TD** (or flex the elbow). If the *extensor* electrode crosses threshold → motor rotates the other way → **open / extend**. Hysteresis is added to prevent chatter.

6. **Power Stage + Motor:** A PWM-driven H-bridge powers a DC gearmotor that drives the TD fingers (or elbow) through a harmonic-drive reducer.

7. **Battery:** A small Li-ion cell (7.4 V, ~ 1.5 Ah) provides autonomy of 8–24 h.

*(Award 1 mark per stage described, with extra mark reserved for the block diagram and a concluding sentence such as "The closed-loop TD provides up to 100 N pinch force and is selected by the prosthetist via software thresholds".)*

**(b) Two surface EMG electrodes placed on the biceps record 80 µV RMS of useful signal, but the amplifier has only 60 dB of CMRR and the input gain is 800. Compute (i) the differential output signal, (ii) the common-mode noise that leaks through, and (iii) the output SNR in dB if a 15 µV common-mode interference is present. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{sig} = 80 \text{ µV}$
- $\text{CMRR} = 60 \text{ dB}$
- $A_d = 800$
- $V_{cm} = 15 \text{ µV}$

**(i) Differential output signal:**

$$V_{out,sig} = V_{sig} \cdot A_d = 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 800 = 0.064 \text{ V} = 64 \text{ mV}$$

(2 marks)

**(ii) CMRR as linear ratio:**

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{60/20} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

Noise gain:

$$A_n = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}}} = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.8$$

Output common-mode noise:

$$V_{out,cm} = V_{cm} \cdot A_n = 15 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.8 = 12 \text{ µV} = 0.012 \text{ mV}$$

(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for the result)

**(iii) Output SNR in dB:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = \frac{V_{out,sig}}{V_{out,cm}} = \frac{64 \text{ mV}}{0.012 \text{ mV}} = 5333.33$$

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5333.33) = 20 \times 3.727 = 74.54 \text{ dB}$$

(2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common mistakes in myo questions:**
> 1. **Using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{20}$** for SNR in dB → marks deducted. SNR in **voltage** uses $20\log_{10}$.
> 2. **CMRR in dB vs. linear** — the formula is $\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}$, **not divided by 10**. Many students divide by 10 → 100× error.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert µV → V** → loses 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Not commenting on whether 60 dB CMRR is acceptable** → a one-line remark (*"60 dB is adequate for indoor use, but 80+ dB is preferred for hospital environments with high EMI"*) earns the interpretative mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this list for the night before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **TD is the "business end"** — Hook = functional, Hand = cosmetic; VO = fixed grip, VC = graded grip.
- [ ] **Pinched force** of a typical adult TD = **22–100 N**; required user pull ≈ **20–50 N**.
- [ ] **Three wrist types to remember:** Friction (common), Quick-Disconnect (interchangeable TD), Locking (preset angles).
- [ ] **Elbow unit mandatory** for trans-humeral & shoulder disarticulation only; **always** paired with a **turntable** and a **forearm lift assist** (constant-force spring).
- [ ] **Figure-of-8 harness** is the body-powered control keystone; **crossover lever** lets **one cable operate two functions** (elbow lock + TD).
- [ ] **Body-Powered Force Ratio (FR)** = $F_{out}/F_{in}$ — typically **2:1 to 7:1**.
- [ ] **Body-Powered Excursion Ratio (ER)** = $x_{out}/x_{in}$ — typically **1:2 to 1:3** (output is less than input).
- [ ] **Body-Powered Efficiency (η)** = $(F_{out} \cdot x_{out}) / (F_{in} \cdot x_{in})$ — typically **40 % – 80 %**.
- [ ] **Myoelectric chain:** EMG → Differential Amplifier (Gain 1000, CMRR ≥ 80 dB) → Rectifier → LPF (envelope) → Threshold logic → PWM motor driver.
- [ ] **EMG threshold** = **20–200 µV RMS**; output of differential amp is in **mV** range.
- [ ] **Capstan equation** for Bowden cable bends: $F_2 = F_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$, $\theta$ in **radians**.
- [ ] **CMRR linear conversion:** $\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}$ — *never divide by 10*.
- [ ] **SNR in dB (voltage):** $20 \log_{10}(V_{sig}/V_{n})$ — *not* $10 \log_{10}$.
- [ ] **Wrist-disarticulation trick:** retain the user's natural pronation/supination by trimming the socket short.
- [ ] **Hybrid systems** = Body-powered elbow + Myoelectric TD → used for **high-level** (SD, forequarter) amputations.
- [ ] **The level of amputation dictates the components** — always name the level first in any KTU essay answer.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 must-mention terms:** *prehension, suspension, fair-lead, Bowden cable, voluntary opening, voluntary closing, harness, figure-of-8, crossover lever, myoelectric, terminal device, stump socket, cosmetic glove.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cosmetic Prostheses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cosmetic Prostheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cosmetic Prosthesis** (also called a **passive prosthesis** or **esthetic prosthesis**) is a custom-fabricated, non-functional or partially functional artificial device designed to replace a missing or congenitally absent body part primarily for the purpose of **restoring natural appearance, anatomical contour, and social-psychological confidence** of the user, with secondary restoration of gross motor function.

According to the KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering syllabus, cosmetic prostheses cover:
- **Finger & partial hand prostheses**
- **Facial prostheses** (auricular, nasal, ocular, mid-facial)
- **Breast prostheses** (post-mastectomy)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition (Standard Examiner-Expected Wording):**
> "A cosmetic prosthesis is an externally applied artificial replacement of a body part whose primary goal is to simulate the natural appearance of the lost structure, achieved through anatomical sculpting, intrinsic/extrinsic coloration, and mechanical or adhesive retention, while providing minimal gross functional recovery."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **movie special-effects makeup artist** working on an actor. The actor's job is to deliver dialogue and act, but the *appearance* of a missing ear, scarred face, or amputated finger must look 100% real to the camera. The artist uses:
- A sculpted **mold** of the actual body part
- **Skin-matched silicone** painted in layers
- **Anchors** (glue, implants, or spectacles) so it stays in place

A cosmetic prosthesis is exactly this — but it is **medical-grade, durable for daily wear**, and designed to handle sweat, sunlight, body oils, and mechanical wear for **2–5 years** of continuous use.

> [!NOTE] **Why Cosmetic (and not Functional)?**
> - Functional prosthesis = replaces a **major** limb (arm/leg) for **mechanical work** (e.g., myoelectric hand)
> - Cosmetic prosthesis = replaces a **visible/distal** part (fingertip, ear, nose, eye) where **appearance outweighs utility** in daily life and social reintegration.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Material Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Shore A Hardness** of medical silicone | **10 – 40 Shore A** |
| **Tear strength** (silicone elastomer) | **20 – 50 kN/m** |
| **Tensile strength** | **5 – 10 MPa** |
| **Operating temperature range** | **−20 °C to +200 °C** |
| **UV resistance** | ≥ **500 hours** (ASTM G154) |
| **Skin irritation index** | **0 (non-irritant)** (ISO 10993-10) |
| **Service life** | **2 – 5 years** |

> [!NOTE] These metrics are what the KTU examiner expects a student to associate with the silicone material used in cosmetic prostheses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cosmetic Prostheses (KTU Syllabus-Aligned Tree)

**Cosmetic Prostheses** are classified along **three axes**:

### A. By Anatomical Region
1. **Digit / Hand-level**
   - Finger prosthesis
   - Partial hand (transmetacarpal) prosthesis
2. **Facial Prostheses**
   - **Auricular** (ear)
   - **Nasal** (nose)
   - **Ocular** (eye)
   - **Orbital** (eye + eyelids + surrounding tissue)
   - **Mid-facial** (cheek, lip)
3. **Somatic / Body**
   - **Breast** (post-mastectomy)
   - **Scar camouflage**

### B. By Retention Method (Most Important — KTU High-Yield)
| Retention Type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Anatomical** | Prosthesis hooks into undercuts of tissue | Auricular, partial finger |
| **Adhesive** | Skin-safe medical adhesive (e.g., Pros-Aide, Medical Bond) | Nasal, mid-facial |
| **Mechanical (e.g., on spectacles)** | Built into eyeglass frame | Ocular, orbital |
| **Osseointegrated (Craniofacial implants)** | Titanium abutments into skull bone | All facial, especially auricular/nasal |
| **Magnetic** | Magnet on abutment + counter-magnet in prosthesis | Auricular, nasal |
| **Suction / vacuum** | Negative pressure cavity (for breast forms) | External breast prosthesis |

### C. By Material
- **Silicone elastomer (medical-grade HTV/LSR)** — Gold standard
- **Polyurethane (PU) foam** — older technique, light weight
- **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** — cheap, limited realism
- **Acrylic (PMMA)** — ocular shells only
- **3D-printed photopolymer resins** — emerging (digital workflow)

## 2.2 The "Three Pillars" of Cosmetic Prosthesis Design

> [!IMPORTANT] Every KTU answer on cosmetic prostheses must mention these three pillars:

1. **Anatomical Accuracy** — Sculpted from a mold of the **contralateral (mirror) side** or from patient CT/impression data.
2. **Color Match (Intrinsic + Extrinsic Coloration)** — Multi-layer painting: deep tissue tones → mid-dermal → epidermal → superficial (capillaries, freckles).
3. **Retention & Comfort** — Must withstand perspiration, **35–40 °C** skin temperature, daily wear ≥ **8 hours**, and not induce contact dermatitis.

## 2.3 The Manufacturing Workflow (Five-Stage Process)

**Stage 1 → Impression Taking**
- Alginate or silicone impression of the defect site + the contralateral side.
- Cast poured in **Type IV dental stone** to obtain a working model.

**Stage 2 → Sculpting**
- Wax-up of the prosthesis mirroring the contralateral anatomy.
- Verified on patient for **size, position, contour**.

**Stage 3 → Molding**
- Investment of wax pattern in a multi-part **stone or 3D-printed mold**.
- Dewaxing at **120 °C** for 30 minutes.

**Stage 4 → Silicone Curing & Intrinsic Coloration**
- Medical-grade **HTV silicone** (e.g., MDX4-4210, Silastic 44210) loaded with **silica filler**.
- **Pigments + flock fibers** mixed in for dermal simulation.
- Cured at **~80–100 °C for 1–2 hours**.

**Stage 5 → Extrinsic Coloration & Finishing**
- Air-brushed surface coloration (Artistotle, Factor II pigments).
- Final matte/gloss topcoat to match skin reflectance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] Although cosmetic prostheses are descriptive, KTU examiners often test the **material indices**. Master this table.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of medical silicone | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | MPa |
| $\sigma_{TS}$ | Tensile strength (silicone) | $\sigma_{TS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | MPa |
| $T_{tear}$ | Tear strength | $T_{tear} = \dfrac{F}{d}$ (force per unit thickness) | kN/m |
| $H_S$ | Shore A hardness (intrinsic softness) | Indent depth at $35^{\circ}$ durometer | Shore A (0–100) |
| $L_{uv}$ | UV lifetime rating | $L_{uv} = k \cdot t_{exp}$ | hours |
| $W_{service}$ | Service life | Empirical: $W_{service} \approx 2 \text{ to } 5 \text{ years}$ | years |
| $A_{contact}$ | Skin–prosthesis contact area | Determines adhesive force: $F_{adh} = \mu \cdot N$ | cm² |
| $F_{retention}$ | Total retention force | $F_{retention} = F_{adh} + F_{mech} + F_{undercut}$ | N |
| $Q_{sweat}$ | Sweat degradation coefficient | $Q_{sweat} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_0 \cdot \Delta t}$ | 1/day |

> [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE for the table above**: In any row where the *raw* formula uses a vertical bar (e.g., for set notation, absolute value, or conditional "such that"), KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE requires the use of $\vert$ or $\mid$ in place of the literal pipe `|` symbol. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking. The KTU high-yield formulas in the table above have been pre-validated to contain no bare pipes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anaplastology** — the medical-art discipline behind facial prostheses
- **Maxillofacial prosthetics** — dental/MF surgery departments
- **Burn rehabilitation** — silicone pressure masks + cosmetic finger caps
- **Oncology rehabilitation** — post-mastectomy breast forms
- **Digital twin + 3D printing** — current industry shift, allowing **CAD-based mirroring** from a photograph of the healthy side

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Required Adhesive Retention Force for a Nasal Prosthesis

> Goal: Compute the **minimum adhesive force** needed to keep a nasal prosthesis attached against gravity and the shear caused by head movement.

**Given (typical patient data):**
- Mass of nasal prosthesis: $m_p = 35 \text{ g} = 0.035 \text{ kg}$
- Effective contact area (silicon–skin): $A_c = 12 \text{ cm}^2 = 12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Coefficient of friction (silicone on skin with medical adhesive): $\mu = 0.6$
- Safety factor (KTU standard): $n = 3$

### Step 1 — Weight Force of the Prosthesis

The downward gravitational force on the prosthesis is its mass multiplied by the gravitational acceleration.

$$
F_g = m_p \cdot g
$$

Substitute the given values.

$$
F_g = 0.035 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_g = 0.34335 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Required Static Friction Force

To keep the prosthesis from sliding downward, the static friction force (provided by adhesion + normal force of the skin) must be **at least equal to** the weight. We also include a safety factor $n$ to account for sweat, dynamic head movement, and aging of the adhesive.

$$
F_{friction,\,min} = n \cdot F_g
$$

$$
F_{friction,\,min} = 3 \times 0.34335
$$

$$
F_{friction,\,min} = 1.03005 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3 — Required Normal (Pressing) Force from Adhesive Bond

Coulomb's law of friction (the basic model of dry friction between two surfaces) states that the friction force equals the coefficient of friction times the normal force.

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N
$$

We invert this to find the normal force the adhesive bond must generate.

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{F_{friction,\,min}}{\mu}
$$

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{1.03005}{0.6}
$$

$$
N_{min} = 1.71675 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4 — Required Adhesive Shear Strength per Unit Area

The adhesive must generate a normal-direction holding force distributed over the contact area. Therefore we convert the required normal force into a pressure-like quantity (force per unit area) by dividing by the contact area.

$$
\tau_{adh} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{A_c}
$$

$$
\tau_{adh} = \dfrac{1.71675}{12 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\tau_{adh} = 1430.6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Convert to more practical units of $\text{kPa}$.

$$
\tau_{adh} \approx 1.43 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT] The required adhesive shear strength is approximately **1.43 kPa**. Standard medical-grade silicone skin adhesives (e.g., Pros-Aide, Hollister) typically provide **2.0 – 4.0 kPa** of shear strength. Therefore this design has a **2×–3× safety margin** — which is essential because sweat, sebum, and thermal cycling reduce the effective adhesion by **30–50 %** in real-world wear.

### Final Result Summary Table

$$
\boxed{\tau_{adh,\,min} \approx 1.43 \text{ kPa}}
$$

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Weight of prosthesis | $F_g$ | $0.343 \text{ N}$ |
| Min friction force | $F_{friction,min}$ | $1.030 \text{ N}$ |
| Min normal force | $N_{min}$ | $1.717 \text{ N}$ |
| Min adhesive shear stress | $\tau_{adh}$ | $1.43 \text{ kPa}$ |

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Retention Method

> This matrix is a **favourite KTU long-answer framework**. The examiner will reward students who can justify a retention method with engineering reasoning rather than just listing it.

| Retention Type | **Shear Strength (kPa)** | **Daily Convenience** | **Surgical Risk** | **Sweat Resistance** | **Longevity** | **Best Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Skin Adhesive | 2 – 4 | Medium (daily re-apply) | **None** | Low | 1–2 yrs | Nasal, mid-facial |
| Anatomical (undercut) | 5 – 8 | High | None | Medium | 3–5 yrs | Auricular, finger |
| Spectacle-mounted | n/a (mechanical) | High | None | High | 3–5 yrs | Ocular / orbital |
| Osseointegrated (magnetic) | 15 – 30 | **Very High** | Moderate (surgery) | High | 10+ yrs (abutment) | Auricular / nasal |
| Suction cup (breast) | 3 – 6 | High | None | Medium | 2–3 yrs | External breast form |

> [!NOTE] The KTU examiner expects a **justified recommendation** in answer form. For example: *"For a young adult with unilateral ear loss post-burn, osseointegrated retention is preferred because (i) skin is scarred and adhesive retention is poor, (ii) longevity is high, (iii) hygiene access to the abutment is easy."*

## 3.3 Worked Example: Service Life Estimation under Daily UV + Sweat Exposure

A patient wears a silicone **auricular prosthesis** for **10 hours/day**, with average daily UV dose equivalent to **2 hours of midday sun**, and sweats moderately.

**Given:**
- UV-resistance rating of MDX4-4210 silicone: $L_{uv} = 500 \text{ h}$ (ASTM G154)
- Daily UV exposure: $t_{day} = 2 \text{ h}$
- Sweat degradation coefficient: $Q_{sweat} = 0.0005 \text{ /day}$
- Daily wear: $w = 10 \text{ h/day}$

### Step 1 — UV-limited service life

We compute how many days of use the silicone can survive before UV-induced embrittlement reaches failure, by dividing the total UV resistance by the daily UV exposure.

$$
D_{uv} = \dfrac{L_{uv}}{t_{day}}
$$

$$
D_{uv} = \dfrac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 2 — Sweat-limited life (mass-loss model)

Empirically, a silicone prosthesis is considered failed when **5 %** of its mass is lost to sweat leaching. Using a first-order decay model.

$$
m(t) = m_0 \cdot e^{-Q_{sweat} \cdot t}
$$

We set the mass ratio equal to 0.95 (i.e., 5 % mass loss threshold) and solve for the time $t$ to reach that mass.

$$
0.95 = e^{-Q_{sweat} \cdot t_{sweat}}
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$
\ln(0.95) = -Q_{sweat} \cdot t_{sweat}
$$

Solve for the time $t_{sweat}$.

$$
t_{sweat} = \dfrac{-\ln(0.95)}{Q_{sweat}}
$$

$$
t_{sweat} = \dfrac{0.05129}{0.0005} \approx 102.6 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 3 — Bottleneck identification

The shorter of the two lifetimes governs the actual service life (the weakest-link principle, applied to parallel degradation mechanisms).

$$
W_{service} = \min(D_{uv},\; t_{sweat}) = \min(250,\; 102.6) \approx 103 \text{ days}
$$

Convert to years for clinical context.

$$
W_{service} \approx \dfrac{103}{365} \approx 0.28 \text{ years} \approx 3.4 \text{ months}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Conclusion:** Sweat is the **bottleneck degradation mode** in this case. In real clinical practice, the patient is given a **night-time cleaning protocol + desiccant storage** which raises $Q_{sweat}$ down to **0.0001 /day**, extending life to **~500 days (~1.4 years)** — matching the industry-stated **2–5 year** service life under proper care.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Cosmetic Prosthesis Design & Fabrication Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end engineering workflow** for fabricating a cosmetic (e.g., auricular) prosthesis, from patient consultation to final delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Consultation and Clinical Assessment] --> B[Defect Site Examination and Contralateral Measurement]
    B --> C[Impression Taking using Alginate or Medical Silicone]
    C --> D[Pour Type IV Dental Stone to Obtain Working Cast]
    D --> E[CAD Mirroring from Contralateral Side Optional]
    E --> F[Wax Sculpting of Prosthesis Anatomy]
    F --> G[Try On Wax Pattern on Patient Verify Shape and Position]
    G --> H{Approved by Patient}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Investment in Multi Part Stone Mold]
    I --> J[Dewaxing at 120 degree Celsius for 30 minutes]
    J --> K[Intrinsic Coloration Mixing Silicone with Pigments and Flock]
    K --> L[Inject Medical Grade HTV Silicone and Cure at 90 degree Celsius for 1 to 2 hours]
    L --> M[Demold and Trim Flash]
    M --> N[Extrinsic Coloration by Airbrush Layered Painting]
    N --> O[Apply Matte or Gloss Topcoat for Skin Reflectance]
    O --> P[Attach Retention Mechanism Adhesive or Magnet or Spectacle Clip]
    P --> Q[Final Fitting and Patient Training on Wear and Hygiene]
    Q --> R[Follow Up at 1 week, 1 month, 6 months]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Retention Method Decision Tree

This Mermaid block diagram shows the engineering decision logic for selecting the most appropriate retention method for a cosmetic prosthesis, based on anatomical site and clinical condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Defect Site Identified] --> T{Is Skin Healthy and Free of Scars}
    T -- Yes --> U{Is Bone Quality Adequate for Implants}
    T -- No --> V[Use Adhesive Retention or Anatomical Undercut]
    U -- Yes --> W{Is Patient Willing for Surgery}
    U -- No --> X[Use Adhesive or Mechanical Retention]
    W -- Yes --> Y[Osseointegrated Titanium Abutments with Magnetic Prosthesis]
    W -- No --> Z[Use Adhesive Bonding as Non Invasive Option]
    V --> AA{Is Site Near Eyeglasses}
    X --> AA
    Z --> AA
    AA -- Yes --> AB[Spectacle Mounted Retention]
    AA -- No --> AC{Is Anatomical Undercut Available}
    AC -- Yes --> AD[Anatomical Retention Hooks into Tissue Undercut]
    AC -- No --> AE[Adhesive as Final Fallback Option]
```

## 4.3 Layered Cross-Section of a Silicone Cosmetic Prosthesis (Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representing the multi-layer construction of a finished cosmetic silicone prosthesis, mapping material layers and their engineering functions from inner to outer surface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Skin Contact Surface] --> L2[Layer 2: Medical Adhesive Interface or Implant Abutment Coupling]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Base Silicone Body HTV MDX4 4210 with Silica Filler Shore A 25]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Intrinsic Pigmentation Deeper Flesh Tones Encapsulated in Silicone]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Mid Dermal Coloration Layer with Flock Fibers for Capillary Simulation]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Epidermal Tint Layer Airbrushed Translucent Pigments]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Surface Topcoat Matte or Gloss for Matching Surrounding Skin Reflectance]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8: Optional UV Stabilizer Additives for Outdoor Use]
```

> [!NOTE] The seven-layer construction is the **industry standard** for high-end facial prostheses. Each layer is **cured separately** to prevent pigment bleeding between layers, with the total thickness being **3 – 6 mm** for an auricular prosthesis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] KTU Part A questions test direct recall and 2–3 sentence definitions. Memorize these exactly.

### Question 1: Define cosmetic prosthesis and distinguish it from a functional prosthesis. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A *cosmetic prosthesis* is a custom-fabricated external artificial device designed to replace a missing or absent body part **primarily for the restoration of appearance and anatomical contour**, with minimal gross functional recovery.

A *functional prosthesis* (e.g., a myoelectric hand) is designed to **restore active mechanical work and dexterity** of the lost limb, with appearance being a secondary concern.

| Feature | Cosmetic | Functional |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary goal** | Appearance | Mechanical function |
| **Power source** | None (passive) | Body, battery, pneumatic |
| **Examples** | Auricular, nasal, finger | Myoelectric hand, C-leg |
| **Weight** | Light (<100 g typical) | Heavier (1–3 kg) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High |

---

### Question 2: List any four materials used in the fabrication of cosmetic prostheses with one key advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Medical-grade silicone (HTV / LSR):** Gold standard — skin-like softness, biocompatible, colorable, durable for 2–5 years.
2. **Polyurethane (PU) foam:** Lightweight and inexpensive — used historically for finger prostheses.
3. **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC):** Very low cost and easily molded — used in short-term or pediatric prostheses.
4. **Acrylic (PMMA):** Rigid and optically clear — used exclusively for **ocular (eye) shells**.
5. **3D-printed photopolymer resin:** Enables digital workflow, fast fabrication, patient-specific mirroring.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 scheme: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice within the module).

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Auricular Prosthesis Workflow

**`(a) [7 Marks]` Describe the step-by-step fabrication procedure of a silicone auricular (ear) prosthesis. Include the materials, equipment, and quality-control checkpoints at each stage.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Clinical Assessment & Imprint (1 mark)**
The defect site is examined, and the contralateral (healthy) ear is photographed from **frontal, lateral, and posterior** angles. An alginate or medical-grade silicone impression is taken of both the defect site and the contralateral ear. A **Type IV dental stone cast** is poured to obtain a positive working model.

**Step 2 — Wax Sculpting (1 mark)**
A wax blank is softened and hand-sculpted over the defect cast, mirroring the anatomy of the healthy ear (helix, antihelix, tragus, concha, lobule). The wax pattern is **tried on the patient** for size, angulation, and contour approval.

**Step 3 — Mold Making & Dewaxing (1 mark)**
The approved wax pattern is invested in a 3-part stone or 3D-printed mold. The mold is placed in a hot air oven at **120 °C for 30 min** to remove the wax completely, leaving a clean negative cavity.

**Step 4 — Silicone Mixing & Intrinsic Coloration (1.5 marks)**
Medical-grade **HTV silicone (MDX4-4210)** is mixed with **silica filler, intrinsic pigments, and rayon flock fibers** to simulate dermal coloration. Multiple color batches are prepared to represent deep tissue, mid-dermis, and epidermal tones.

**Step 5 — Curing (1 mark)**
The colored silicone is injected into the closed mold and cured at **~90 °C for 1–2 hours** in a hot air oven or press. Slow cooling prevents internal stress.

**Step 6 — Demolding, Trimming, Extrinsic Coloration (1 mark)**
The cured prosthesis is demolded, flash is trimmed, and **airbrushed surface coloration** is applied in 4–6 thin layers (Artistotle/Factor II pigments), sealed with a **matte or gloss topcoat** to match the patient's skin reflectance.

**Step 7 — Quality Control & Delivery (0.5 marks)**
- Visual match: size, position, color under daylight + fluorescent light
- Tensile / tear spot-test on leftover silicone
- Patient training on insertion, removal, hygiene, and night-time storage
- Follow-up at 1 week, 1 month, 6 months

---

**`(b) [7 Marks]` Discuss the various retention methods available for auricular prostheses, with their relative advantages and limitations. Recommend the most suitable method for a young adult with post-burn scar tissue around the ear.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Retention Methods for Auricular Prosthesis:**

1. **Anatomical Retention (0.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Prosthesis edge hooks into natural undercuts of remnant ear tissue.
   - *Advantage:* No surgery, no daily adhesive, immediate use.
   - *Limitation:* Requires adequate tissue undercuts; **fails in post-burn scar cases** where tissue is flat.

2. **Skin Adhesive (0.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Medical adhesive (Pros-Aide, Hollister Bond) applied daily.
   - *Advantage:* Non-invasive, low cost.
   - *Limitation:* Daily reapplication; **poor on scarred skin** (low adhesion, irritation); sweat-sensitive.

3. **Spectacle / Headband Mechanical (0.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Prosthesis attached to eyeglass temple or elastic band.
   - *Advantage:* No skin contact, removable.
   - *Limitation:* Visible band, uncomfortable, shifts during activity.

4. **Osseointegrated Implants with Magnetic or Bar-Clip Coupling (2.5 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* **2–3 titanium fixtures** are surgically placed into the mastoid/temporal bone; after 3–6 months of osseointegration, a **magnetic abutment or bar-clip** is fitted, and the prosthesis snaps on via a counter-magnet.
   - *Advantage:* **Excellent retention** (15–30 kPa shear strength), no adhesive, **waterproof**, hygienic, long abutment life (10+ years).
   - *Limitation:* Requires surgery, cost, contraindicated in irradiated bone or uncontrolled diabetes.

5. **Adhesive + Implant Hybrid (0.5 marks)**
   - Used in transition period during osseointegration.

**Recommendation for the Young Adult with Post-Burn Scar (0.75 marks):**

> **Recommendation: Osseointegrated implant with magnetic retention** is the most suitable method. Justification:
> - Scarred skin **rules out adhesive** (poor bond, irritation).
> - Scarred, flattened tissue **eliminates anatomical undercuts**.
> - Patient is young, otherwise healthy, and a **good surgical candidate**.
> - Long-term retention, hygiene, and aesthetic outcome are superior.
> - The prosthesis can be easily removed for cleaning the abutment, reducing infection risk.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Materials & Coloration

**`(a) [7 Marks]` Explain the role of medical-grade silicone in cosmetic prostheses. Discuss its desirable properties, common grades used, and limitations.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Medical-Grade Silicone (1.5 marks):**
Silicone elastomer is the **gold-standard material** for cosmetic prostheses because it uniquely combines **softness, biocompatibility, colorability, and durability**. It is used in facial, finger, and breast prostheses and constitutes >80% of modern cosmetic prosthetic prescriptions.

**Desirable Properties (3 marks):**
1. **Biocompatibility (ISO 10993):** Non-toxic, non-irritant, hypoallergenic — safe for **8–16 hours of daily skin contact**.
2. **Mechanical softness (Shore A 10–40):** Mimics the compliance of skin and cartilage, preventing pressure necrosis on underlying tissue.
3. **Chemical inertness:** Resistant to sweat, sebum, soaps, cosmetics, and mild chemicals.
4. **Colorability:** Can be intrinsically pigmented (during mixing) and extrinsically painted (after curing) to match the full spectrum of human skin tones.
5. **Thermal stability:** Operates from **−20 °C to +200 °C** without losing elasticity, allowing autoclave sterilization.
6. **UV stability (with additives):** Color and mechanical properties retained for **500+ hours** of UV exposure.
7. **Aging resistance:** Service life of **2–5 years** under proper care.

**Common Grades Used (1.5 marks):**
| Grade | Type | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **MDX4-4210** | HTV (High-Temp Vulcanizing) | Most common — facial, finger |
| **Silastic 44210** | HTV | Similar, FDA-approved |
| **MED-4950 / MED-4940** | LSR (Liquid Silicone Rubber) | Injection-molded parts |
| **Factor II A-2000** | HTV | Custom facial prosthetics |

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Cost:** Medical-grade silicone is **5–10× costlier** than industrial silicone.
- **Mechanical weakness:** Tear strength (20–50 kN/m) is low compared to natural cartilage — vulnerable to **mechanical tearing** if mishandled.
- **Discoloration over time:** Pigments fade with UV, sweat, and cosmetics — extrinsic paint needs **annual refresh**.
- **Adhesion difficulty:** Silicone-to-silicone bonding is poor — requires **primer** (e.g., Dow Corning 1200) for layered curing.
- **Not biodegradable** — environmental concern at end-of-life.

---

**`(b) [7 Marks]` Describe the coloration techniques used to achieve realistic skin matching in a facial prosthesis. Why is single-tone coloration inadequate?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Why Single-Tone Coloration is Inadequate (1 mark):**
Human skin is **not a single uniform color** — it is a multi-layered optical structure with **translucent epidermis, vascularized dermis, subcutaneous fat, and surface specular highlights**. A single tone results in a **flat, doll-like, artificial appearance**, which defeats the entire purpose of a cosmetic prosthesis. The KTU standard requires **at least 4–6 layers** of color for realism.

**Intrinsic Coloration (Pigments Mixed into Silicone Before Curing) — 2.5 marks:**
Pigments are added to the bulk silicone to give the body of the prosthesis a deep, translucent color that does not wear off.
- **Pigments used:** Silicone-compatible Artistotle or Factor II pigments — **red, yellow, blue, white, black, brown**.
- **Stages of intrinsic layering:**
  - **Layer 1 — Deep tissue:** Reddish-brown (vascular base).
  - **Layer 2 — Mid-dermis:** Peach/flesh tone with **rayon flock fibers** simulating capillary networks.
  - **Layer 3 — Sub-epidermal:** Lighter pink with translucency.
  - **Layer 4 — Epidermal tint:** Almost-transparent silicone with subtle warm tone.
- **Result:** Even if surface paint wears off, the prosthesis still looks natural.

**Extrinsic Coloration (Airbrush Painting After Curing) — 2.5 marks:**
Surface painting adds the **fine, patient-specific details** that intrinsic coloration cannot achieve.
- **Equipment:** Airbrush (0.3–0.5 mm nozzle), at **1.5–2.5 bar** pressure, held **10–15 cm** from the surface.
- **Layering technique:** 4–6 thin, **translucent** coats (not opaque), each lighter than the last:
  - Base skin tone
  - Sub-surface vascular blush (temple, cheek)
  - Freckles / pigmented spots (liver spots, beauty marks)
  - Capillary red lines
  - Final matte or gloss topcoat matching surrounding skin reflectance
- **Sealing:** Topcoat protects paint from sweat, sebum, and abrasion.

**Verification (1 mark):**
- Compared under **daylight (D65), fluorescent, and incandescent** light to account for **metamerism** (two colors matching under one light but differing under another).
- Photographic comparison with contralateral side.
- Patient approval under social lighting (café, office, sunlight).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where students lose marks in Cosmetic Prosthesis questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "cosmetic" with "functional"** — A cosmetic prosthesis is *not* a passive object; it can have minimal gross function (e.g., holding glasses). Don't define it as "non-functional" in the absolute sense.
> 2. **Listing retention methods without justification** — KTU examiners reward *clinical reasoning* (why a method suits the patient), not a bullet list.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor** in retention force calculations — Always include a **factor of safety ≥ 3** in design calculations.
> 4. **Confusing Shore A with Shore D hardness** — Prosthetic silicone uses **Shore A only** (soft elastomer). Shore D is for rigid plastics.
> 5. **Omitting the multi-layer coloration logic** — Stating "paint to match skin color" is worth **1 mark**; explaining **intrinsic + extrinsic 4–6 layer technique with metamerism** is worth **5–6 marks**.
> 6. **Skipping the contralateral mirroring concept** — A facial prosthesis is *always* sculpted from the **healthy opposite side**, not from imagination.
> 7. **Missing biocompatibility standards** — Always cite **ISO 10993** for medical-grade materials.
> 8. **No follow-up plan in long answers** — Always close with a **1-week / 1-month / 6-month follow-up** mention for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] This is your **last-30-minutes-before-exam checklist**. Read it twice.

- [ ] **Definition** — Cosmetic prosthesis = appearance-first replacement; minimal gross function.
- [ ] **Three pillars** — Anatomical accuracy + Color match (intrinsic + extrinsic) + Retention & comfort.
- [ ] **Material** — Medical-grade **silicone (MDX4-4210)** is the **gold standard**.
- [ ] **Shore A hardness** of prosthetic silicone: **10 – 40 Shore A**.
- [ ] **Service life:** **2 – 5 years** (with care); **UV limit: 500 h** (ASTM G154).
- [ ] **Biocompatibility standard:** **ISO 10993**.
- [ ] **Impression** → Type IV dental stone cast.
- [ ] **Wax sculpting** is the design stage — must be tried on patient.
- [ ] **Dewaxing** at **120 °C for 30 min**.
- [ ] **Silicone cure** at **~90 °C for 1–2 h**.
- [ ] **Coloration** is **multi-layered** (4–6 layers), with intrinsic (bulk) + extrinsic (surface) painting.
- [ ] **Metamerism check** under D65 daylight + fluorescent + incandescent.
- [ ] **Retention methods** — Anatomical / Adhesive / Mechanical (spectacle) / Osseointegrated (magnetic) / Suction.
- [ ] **Osseointegrated retention** uses **titanium implants** in mastoid bone — strongest retention (15–30 kPa).
- [ ] **Craniofacial implant brand** — **Brånemark System** (Nobel Biocare) is the KTU textbook reference.
- [ ] **Always use a safety factor ≥ 3** in retention force calculations.
- [ ] **Auricular (ear)**, **nasal (nose)**, **ocular (eye shell)**, and **finger** are the **KTU-exam-high-yield** cosmetic prosthesis types.
- [ ] **Post-mastectomy breast prosthesis** can be **external (bra-pocket silicone form)** or **surgical implant** (not strictly a "prosthesis" in KTU terminology).
- [ ] **Sculpting source** — Always from **contralateral (mirror) side**, never from imagination.
- [ ] **Common silicone adhesives** — **Pros-Aide, Hollister Bond, Dow Corning 1200 primer**.
- [ ] **Failure mode** — Sweat leaching + UV degradation are the dominant failure modes.
- [ ] **Standard follow-up schedule** — 1 week, 1 month, 6 months.
- [ ] **KTU examiner loves** — justifications, multi-layer reasoning, and ISO/biocompatibility citations.
- [ ] **Avoid** — confusing cosmetic vs. functional, listing without justifying, single-tone coloration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of the Lower Limb Prosthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of the Lower Limb Prosthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 3 — Components of the Lower Limb Prosthesis

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lower Limb Prosthesis** is an externally applied biomechanical device that replaces, as a whole or in part, a missing or non-functional segment of the human lower extremity (foot, ankle, shank, knee, thigh, or hip) lost due to **amputation, congenital deficiency (transverse/ longitudinal limb deficiency), or trauma**. It is engineered to restore **static weight-bearing support, dynamic gait propulsion, shock attenuation, energy storage & return, and cosmesis**, thereby reinstating the user's **mobility, stability, and quality of life**.

The prosthesis is mechanically interfaced to the residual limb (stump) through a precision-fabricated **socket**, retained by a **suspension system**, structurally transmitted through a **pylon (shank)**, and terminated by an **ankle-foot assembly** (and optionally a **knee unit** for transfemoral levels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Lower Limb Prosthesis — components: socket, suspension, pylon/shank, foot-ankle assembly, and knee unit (for trans-femoral). Materials used. Alignment principles (bench, static, dynamic)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eiffel Tower of the Body"

Imagine the lower limb prosthesis as the **engineered leg of a steel-and-carbon robotic exoskeleton**:

- The **socket** is the *custom-molded cockpit seat* — the only place where human and machine physically meet, transferring every Newton of body weight.
- The **suspension** is the *harness/seatbelt* that prevents the cockpit from falling off during movement.
- The **pylon** is the *central load-bearing column* (think of the Eiffel Tower's iron shaft) — it carries vertical load while remaining light.
- The **foot-ankle** is the *spring-loaded shoe* — cushioning ground impact and pushing the body forward at toe-off.
- The **knee unit** is the *mechanical hinge with intelligent brakes* — allowing the leg to bend during swing and lock during stance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Significance:**
> Lower limb amputations in India occur most commonly due to **diabetic foot complications, road-traffic accidents, peripheral vascular disease, and industrial trauma**. Globally, the **trans-tibial (below-knee)** level accounts for ~60% of major lower limb amputees, while **trans-femoral (above-knee)** accounts for ~25%.

## 1.3 Classification of Amputation Levels (Quick Reference)

| Level | Standard Abbreviation | Components Required |
|---|---|---|
| Partial Foot | PF | Toe-filler / forefoot prosthesis |
| Syme's | — | Socket + Syme's foot |
| Trans-tibial (Below Knee) | TT / BK | Socket + Suspension + Pylon + Foot |
| Knee Disarticulation | KD | Socket + Knee unit + Pylon + Foot |
| Trans-femoral (Above Knee) | TF / AK | Socket + Suspension + Knee + Pylon + Foot |
| Hip Disarticulation | HD | Hip joint + Socket + Knee + Pylon + Foot |

> [!TIP]
> The **higher** the level of amputation, the **greater** the loss of natural joints and muscles, and consequently the **more prosthetic components** are needed to substitute for the lost biomechanics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Component Architecture

### 2.1.1 The Socket (Interface)

The **socket** is the *single most critical* component because it directly controls **comfort, pressure distribution, proprioception, and energy expenditure**. A poorly fitted socket causes pain, skin breakdown, and abandonment of the prosthesis.

**Types of Sockets (Trans-tibial):**

| Type | Description | Weight-Bearing Areas |
|---|---|---|
| **PTB (Patellar Tendon Bearing)** | Traditional; load on patellar tendon | Patellar tendon, medial tibial flare, fibular shaft, posterior calf |
| **TSB (Total Surface Bearing)** | Hydrostatic loading across entire stump | Entire residual limb surface |
| **PTS (Patellar Tendon Supracondylar)** | PTB + medial/lateral supracondylar grip | Same as PTB + femoral condyles |
| **KBM (Kondylen Bettung Münster)** | German variant with condylar wedge | Same as PTS |
| **ICEROSS / Seal-In (Suction)** | Silicone liner with locking pin/seal | Total surface (hydrostatic) |

**Materials used for sockets:**
- **Thermoplastics:** Polypropylene (PP), polyethylene, copolymer — *light, cheap, thermoformable*.
- **Thermosets:** Epoxy resins reinforced with fiberglass or carbon fiber — *stronger, more durable, used for definitive prostheses*.
- **Laminated composites:** Carbon fiber + epoxy + acrylic resin — *high strength-to-weight ratio, used in sport/recreational prostheses*.
- **3D-Printed sockets:** Using CAD-CAM scanning and selective laser sintering (SLS) with **Polyamide-12 (PA-12)** or **PEEK**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Distribution Principle:**
> The socket must apply **low pressure on load-tolerant areas** (patellar tendon, medial tibial flare, anterior tibial condyles) and **high pressure on load-intolerant areas** (tibial crest, fibular head, peroneal nerve). This is the **"high-low pressure"** philosophy.

### 2.1.2 The Suspension System

Suspension holds the prosthesis onto the residual limb during swing phase and prevents pistoning (vertical movement) during gait.

**Principal Methods:**

| Method | Mechanism | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Suction Suspension** | Air-tight seal, negative pressure on swing | Mature, cylindrical stumps |
| **Pin Lock (Shuttle Lock)** | Pin on liner clicks into a lock in socket | Active users, transfemoral |
| **Lanyard strap** | Strap threaded through socket and pulled out | Hip disarticulation, very short stumps |
| **Supracondylar / Suprapatellar (SC/SP)** | Socket extends above condyles | Short transtibial stumps |
| **Sleeve suspension** | Neoprene/silicone sleeve with gasket seal | Vacuum systems, active users |
| **Vacuum-Assisted (Harmony, Unity)** | Active pump evacuates air on every step | TSB sockets, diabetic patients |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:**
> **Elevated Vacuum Suspension** (e.g., Ottobock Harmony® system) maintains **-550 to -760 mmHg** of sub-atmospheric pressure, reducing daily volume fluctuation by **up to 80%** and improving gait symmetry.

### 2.1.3 The Pylon (Shank)

The pylon is the **structural backbone** between socket and foot, transmitting axial load and torque.

**Two Architectural Types:**

- **Exoskeletal (Crustacean / "Crab" / Shell):** The structural shell is on the outside; made of wood, polyurethane foam, or fiberglass. The cosmetic shape is the structure itself. *Advantages:* rugged, water-resistant, low cost. *Disadvantages:* heavy, difficult to adjust alignment, limited modularity.
- **Endoskeletal (Modular / "Skeleton"):** A central tubular pylon (usually aluminum, titanium, or carbon fiber) is *inside*, surrounded by a soft foam cover. *Advantages:* light, alignment-adjustable via clamp adapters, cosmesis customizable, interchangeable feet. *Disadvantages:* less durable, foam cover degrades.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention **"modular endoskeletal** systems are the contemporary clinical standard" — examiners reward this exact phrasing.

**Materials Comparison:**

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum 7075-T6 | 2.81 | 71.5 | Standard pylons |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 113.8 | High-strength pylons |
| Carbon fiber composite | 1.55–1.85 | 70–150 | Sport / dynamic response |
| Stainless Steel 316L | 7.99 | 193 | Heavy-duty exoskeletal |

### 2.1.4 The Foot-Ankle Assembly

The foot-ankle assembly must replicate **plantarflexion at heel-strike, dorsiflexion at mid-stance, and energy return at toe-off**.

**Evolutionary Categories:**

| Foot Type | Ankle Motion | Energy Return | Indications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel)** | None (rigid keel, compressible heel wedge) | Low | Low-activity elderly, K1–K2 |
| **Single-Axis (SA)** | Plantar/Dorsi via mechanical pivot | Low | K2, requires stability |
| **Multi-Axis (MA)** | Plantar/Dorsi + Inversion/Eversion | Low-Medium | Uneven terrain, K2–K3 |
| **Dynamic Response (DR) / Energy-Storing** | Carbon-fiber keel deflects | High | Active K3–K4, athletes |
| **Microprocessor / Powered** | Active joint control | High | K4, transfemoral elite |

> [!NOTE]
> **SACH Foot** = **S**olid **A**nkle **C**ushioned **H**eel — the most widely prescribed foot globally due to low cost (~₹3,000–₹5,000 in India) and high durability.

### 2.1.5 The Knee Unit (Transfemoral / Knee Disarticulation)

The prosthetic knee must provide **stability in stance phase** (preventing buckling) and **smooth swing-phase control** (matching walking speed).

**Mechanism Categories:**

| Type | Stance Stability Mechanism | Swing Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Lock** | Manual pin engaged | Locked during gait |
| **Single-Axis Constant Friction** | Alignment posterior to TKA line | Constant friction pad |
| **Weight-Activated Stance Control** | Friction brake triggered by axial load | Friction |
| **Polycentric (4-bar, 5-bar, 7-bar)** | Geometric locking of linkage | Friction |
| **Hydraulic** | Fluid damping (passive) | Variable damping via fluid |
| **Pneumatic** | Air-cylinder damping | Variable air-flow |
| **Microprocessor-Controlled (C-Leg, Genium, Rheo)** | Real-time sensor feedback (servo) | Active damping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Polycentric Knee Stability:**
> Polycentric knees incorporate a **mechanical Instant Centre of Rotation (ICR)** that, when positioned **posterior and superior to the line of ground reaction force**, creates a *geometric extension moment* during early stance — passively preventing buckling **without any friction or hydraulic lock**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Key Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body-weight load through socket | $F_{axial} = m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | N; $\theta$ = socket flexion angle |
| 2 | Plantarflexion moment at heel-strike | $M_{heel} = F_{GRF} \cdot d_{keel}$ | N·m; $d_{keel}$ = keel lever arm |
| 3 | Energy stored in carbon keel | $E_{st} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot x^{2}$ | J; $k$ = keel stiffness, $x$ = deflection |
| 4 | Energy returned (efficiency) | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{return}}{E_{store}} \times 100$ | %; Carbon DR feet → 80–95% |
| 5 | Pressure on residual limb (PTS) | $P = \dfrac{F_{axial}}{A_{eff}}$ | Pa; $A_{eff}$ = effective load-bearing area |
| 6 | Hydraulic damping force | $F_{damp} = c \cdot v^{n}$ | N; $c$ = damping coefficient, $v$ = piston velocity, $n \in [1,2]$ |
| 7 | Vertical ground reaction force (normal walking) | $F_{GRF} \approx 1.2 \cdot BW$ | N; peak = 1.2× body weight at terminal stance |
| 8 | Sub-atmospheric vacuum pressure | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - \Delta P$ | Pa; $\Delta P = 550$–$760$ mmHg in active systems |
| 9 | Polycentric ICR stability | $\vec{r}_{ICR} > \vec{r}_{GRF}$ | Vector criterion; ICR must lie posterior to GRF |
| 10 | Sagittal-plane socket flexion | $\theta_{flex} \approx 5^{\circ}$–$10^{\circ}$ for TT | Degrees; built into socket wall |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Clinical Relevance

The **socket-pressure–gait-speed-energy-expenditure** trade-off is the central engineering problem. Every component addresses one optimization goal:

- **Socket** → minimize interface stress (skin breakdown)
- **Suspension** → minimize pistoning & vertical displacement
- **Pylon** → minimize mass while maximizing buckling strength
- **Foot** → maximize energy return at push-off
- **Knee** → maximize stance stability AND swing smoothness simultaneously

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Reality:** A transfemoral amputee expends **~60–100% more metabolic energy** than an able-bodied person walking at the same speed. Component selection directly modulates this cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Storage & Return in a Dynamic-Response Foot

The dynamic-response (DR) foot uses a **carbon-fiber composite keel** that deflects during stance and recoils at toe-off. The energy-return behavior is modeled as a **spring-mass-damper system**.

### 3.1.1 Equivalent Mechanical Model

The carbon keel is modeled as a **linear elastic spring** with effective stiffness $k$. During the **stance phase** (heel-strike to toe-off), the keel deflects vertically by a maximum displacement $x_{max}$ under the vertical ground reaction force $F_{GRF}$.

$$
F_{GRF} - k \cdot x(t) - c \cdot \dot{x}(t) = m_{eff} \cdot \ddot{x}(t)
$$

where $m_{eff}$ is the effective mass of the keel + foot assembly, $c$ is the structural damping coefficient, and $k$ is the effective stiffness of the composite laminate.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Maximum Stored Strain Energy

At the point of **maximum deflection** (typically at mid-stance, ~50% of the gait cycle), the velocity $\dot{x}(t) \to 0$ instantaneously. Therefore, **all the work done by the GRF is stored as elastic strain energy** in the keel.

$$
E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} F_{GRF} \, dx
$$

Assuming quasi-static loading and a linear spring force-displacement relation $F = kx$, the stored energy becomes:

$$
E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} kx \, dx = \left. \dfrac{k x^{2}}{2} \right|_{0}^{x_{max}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, k \, x_{max}^{2}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Energy Returned During Toe-Off

During the **terminal stance to toe-off** phase, the keel recoils. The energy returned to the user equals the elastic energy stored minus the energy lost to **internal damping** (viscoelastic losses in the resin matrix, micro-cracking in fibers, and heat dissipation).

$$
E_{returned} = E_{stored} - E_{dissipated}
$$

The dissipated energy over one deflection cycle is:

$$
E_{dissipated} = \oint c \cdot \dot{x} \, dx = \pi \cdot c \cdot \omega \cdot X^{2}
$$

where $\omega$ is the gait-cycle angular frequency and $X$ is the amplitude of oscillation.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Foot Efficiency

The energy-return efficiency is therefore:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{E_{returned}}{E_{stored}} \times 100 = \left( 1 - \dfrac{\pi c \omega X^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} k X^{2}} \right) \times 100 = \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \pi c \omega}{k} \right) \times 100
$$

This gives the **mechanical efficiency** of the foot:

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \pi c \omega}{k} \right) \times 100 \%
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

For a typical **Ottobock Triton® carbon-fiber foot** used by a 70 kg transfemoral amputee:

- $k = 250 \times 10^{3}$ N/m
- $c = 1500$ N·s/m
- $\omega = 2\pi f$; with cadence = 110 steps/min, $f = 110/60 \approx 1.83$ Hz

$$
\omega = 2\pi \times 1.83 = 11.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 11.5}{250 \times 10^{3}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1.084 \times 10^{5}}{2.5 \times 10^{5}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - 0.4336 \right) \times 100 = 56.64 \ \%
$$

The remaining ~43% is dissipated as heat and viscoelastic losses.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, modern **carbon DR feet** achieve **80–95% elastic efficiency** because the resin matrix is engineered to minimize viscoelastic damping while maintaining stiffness.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Peak Deflection and Peak Force

For a 70 kg subject, body weight $W = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$ N. Peak GRF during walking:

$$
F_{GRF,peak} \approx 1.2 \cdot BW = 1.2 \times 686.7 = 824.04 \text{ N}
$$

Peak deflection of the keel:

$$
x_{max} = \dfrac{F_{GRF,peak}}{k} = \dfrac{824.04}{250 \times 10^{3}} = 3.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.30 \text{ mm}
$$

This ~3 mm deflection is consistent with published experimental measurements on dynamic-response feet.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Logic

| Patient Factor | Socket Choice | Suspension | Foot Choice | Knee Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young, active TT | TSB, carbon | Pin-lock / Suction | DR carbon | N/A |
| Elderly, diabetic TT | Soft liner + TSB | Vacuum-assisted | SACH / SA | N/A |
| Short TT stump | PTS / KBM | SC supracondylar | SACH | N/A |
| Active TF | Ischial-containment | Pin-lock / Suction | Multi-axis DR | Hydraulic / MP |
| Elderly TF | Quadrilateral | Suction / Sleeve | SACH | Single-axis, weight-activated |
| Bilateral TF | Custom molded | Pin-lock | SACH for stability | Manual lock preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
