```mermaid
    end
    UI --> SW
    SW --> HW
    HW --> SW
    SW --> UI
```

### 4.3 Workspace and Reachability Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[START TASK PLANNING] --> Q[DEFINE TARGET OBJECT COORDINATES]
    Q --> R[CHECK OBJECT WITHIN WORKSPACE REACH]
    R --> RYES[YES INSIDE REACH]
    R --> RNO[NO OUTSIDE REACH]
    RYES --> S[APPLY INVERSE KINEMATICS SOLVER]
    S --> T[COMPUTE JOINT ANGLES THETA 1 TO THETA 6]
    T --> U[CHECK MANIPULABILITY INDEX W]
    U --> UOK[SUFFICIENT W MOVE TO NEXT]
    U --> ULOW[LOW W RECONFIGURE POSTURE]
    ULOW --> S
    UOK --> V[GENERATE TRAJECTORY SMOOTH PATH]
    V --> W[EXECUTE MOTION SEQUENCE]
    W --> X[TASK COMPLETE]
    RNO --> Y[ALERT USER OBJECT UNREACHABLE]
    Y --> P
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Robotic Manipulation Aid." Mention any two standard end-effectors used in such systems.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Robotic Manipulation Aid (RMA)** is a computer-controlled, powered electromechanical device designed to assist individuals with upper-limb impairments in performing **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** such as feeding, object retrieval, and self-care, by providing programmable, multi-DOF motion of an end-effector within a defined workspace.

**Two standard end-effectors:**
1. **Two-finger parallel gripper** — used for grasping cups, bottles, and regular-shaped objects.
2. **Three-finger gripper** — used for irregular or spherical objects, providing enveloping grasp for better stability.

*(Acceptable alternatives: suction-cup end-effector, hook tool, spoon/utensil holder.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*List any three input control modalities used by physically challenged users to operate a robotic manipulator arm.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Joystick control** — operated by residual hand or finger movement; offers proportional velocity control.
2. **Sip-and-puff control** — operated by mouth pneumatic pressure; used by high-level spinal cord injury patients.
3. **Head-tracker control** — uses inertial or camera-based tracking of head motion to map to arm motion.

*(Other acceptable: EMG (electromyography), voice recognition, EEG/BCI, chin-operated joystick.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **forgetting the ground link** when applying the Grübler-Kutzbach equation — always count $n$ including the base.
> - In grasp force derivations, students often **drop the factor of 2** (for two fingers) — examiners specifically look for this.
> - For DH parameter questions, **units and sign conventions** are checked. Use radians for angles.
> - Do not confuse **degrees of freedom (DOF)** with **degrees of mobility** — they are the same in this context.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a wheelchair-mounted robotic manipulation aid. Label all major subsystems. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A wheelchair-mounted robotic manipulation aid is composed of the following interconnected functional blocks:

1. **Mounting Bracket and Base:** Rigid mechanical fixture attached to the wheelchair frame, providing structural support and a stable reaction base for all arm forces.
2. **User Input Interface:** Accepts commands via joystick, sip-and-puff, head tracker, or EMG — converts user intent into electrical signals.
3. **Central Controller (Microcontroller/DSP):** Runs the real-time kinematic engine, interprets input signals, and generates motion commands.
4. **Actuator Drivers (Motor Controllers):** PWM or current-controlled amplifiers that power the six servo motors based on controller commands.
5. **Sensors:** Encoders on each joint for position feedback; force/torque sensors at the wrist; optional vision camera for object detection.
6. **Mechanical Arm (6R Kinematic Chain):** Six motorized revolute joints connected by rigid links, providing 6 DOF for full spatial manipulation.
7. **End-Effector (Gripper):** Two-finger or three-finger actuated gripper with tactile sensors for grasp confirmation.
8. **Feedback Display:** Visual LEDs, LCD, or haptic vibration motors to inform the user of status (e.g., object grasped).
9. **Power Supply:** Rechargeable Li-ion battery pack providing 24V or 48V DC.

The signal flow is: **User → Input Interface → Controller → Drivers → Motors → Arm → Gripper → Object**, with feedback from sensors returning to the controller in a closed loop.

---

**(b)** A 6R anthropomorphic robotic arm has 6 revolute joints and 7 links (including the base). Calculate its degrees of freedom using the Grübler-Kutzbach equation. Explain why 6 DOF is the minimum required for full spatial manipulation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Grübler-Kutzbach Equation (Spatial Mechanism):**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Given:**
- Total links (including ground): $n = 7$
- Number of revolute joints: $j_1 = 6$
- Number of higher-pair joints: $j_2 = 0$

**Substitution:**

$$M = 3(7-1) - 2(6) - 0$$

$$M = 3(6) - 12$$

$$M = 18 - 12$$

$$M = 6 \text{ DOF}$$

**Why 6 DOF is the minimum:**
A rigid body in 3D space has **6 independent spatial parameters**:
- 3 translational parameters: position along x, y, and z axes.
- 3 rotational parameters: roll, pitch, and yaw (Euler angles).

To arbitrarily place an object at any point in 3D space **with any desired orientation**, the manipulator must be able to control all 6 parameters independently. A manipulator with fewer than 6 DOF would either be unable to reach certain positions or unable to achieve certain orientations, making it unsuitable for general-purpose manipulation tasks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics equations for a 2-link planar RR manipulator using the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention. Clearly state the DH parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DH Parameters for a 2-Link Planar RR Arm:**

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Individual Transformation Matrices:**

For Link 1:

$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta_1) & -\sin(\theta_1) & 0 & L_1\cos(\theta_1) \\ \sin(\theta_1) & \cos(\theta_1) & 0 & L_1\sin(\theta_1) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

For Link 2:

$$A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_2) & 0 & L_2\cos(\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_2) & 0 & L_2\sin(\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Composite Transformation $T = A_1 \cdot A_2$:**

$$T = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos(\theta_1) + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin(\theta_1) + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**End-Effector Position:**

$$x = L_1\cos(\theta_1) + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1\sin(\theta_1) + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$z = 0, \quad \phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

This solution enables the controller to compute the end-effector's Cartesian position for any given joint angles.

---

**(b)** A two-finger gripper holds a 0.5 kg cylindrical object. The fingers are oriented at 30° from the vertical, and the coefficient of friction between the finger pads and the object is 0.4. Calculate the minimum gripping force required per finger to prevent the object from slipping. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Object mass: $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$
- Finger angle from vertical: $\theta = 30°$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.4$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Formula (derived in Section 3.2):**

$$F_{grip} \geq \frac{m \cdot g}{2 \mu \cos(\theta)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Weight:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 0.5 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos(30°)$:**

$$\cos(30°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Equation:**

$$F_{grip} \geq \frac{4.905}{2 \times 0.4 \times 0.866}$$

$$F_{grip} \geq \frac{4.905}{0.6928}$$

$$F_{grip} \geq 7.08 \text{ N}$$

**Result:**
The minimum gripping force per finger is approximately **7.08 N** to prevent slip. In practice, a safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0 is applied, so the design force would be **10.6 N to 14.2 N** per finger.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robotic Manipulation Aids**

- **Definition:** Computer-controlled, powered, multi-DOF device that assists with upper-limb ADL tasks.
- **Standard DOF:** Minimum 6 DOF (3 position + 3 orientation) — confirmed by Grübler-Kutzbach.
- **Kinematic Chain:** 6R (six revolute joints) anthropomorphic configuration is the industry standard.
- **Mounting Types:** Wheelchair-mounted, desktop, or body-worn (exoskeleton).
- **Control Modalities:** Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-tracker, EMG, voice, BCI — chosen based on user's residual function.
- **End-Effectors:** Two-finger gripper, three-finger gripper, suction cup, tool holder.
- **Grasp Force Formula:** $F_{grip} \geq \frac{m g}{2 \mu \cos(\theta)}$ — the factor of 2 is for two fingers; do not omit it.
- **Payload Capacity:** 1.5 to 5 kg for ADL objects.
- **Workspace:** Spherical reach of 1.2 to 1.5 m from base.
- **Key Performance Metrics:** Repeatability (± 2-5 mm), manipulability index, battery endurance.
- **Commercial Examples:** JACO Arm (Kinova), iARM (Assistive Innovations), MyoPro (Myomo), DEKA Arm.
- **Clinical Impact:** Reduces caregiver dependency by 40-60% for tetraplegic users.
- **DH Convention:** Always state all four parameters ($a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$) for each link — examiners verify this strictly.
- **Jacobian & Manipulability:** The Jacobian matrix maps joint velocities to Cartesian velocities; Yoshikawa's manipulability index $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ quantifies dexterity.
- **Safety:** Force/torque sensing at the wrist and tactile feedback at the gripper are mandatory to prevent injury during human-robot interaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Therapeutic Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Therapeutic Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therapeutic Robots** are programmable mechatronic devices specifically engineered to deliver measurable, repeatable, and clinically validated physical, cognitive, or socio-emotional interventions to individuals with impairments arising from **stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), cerebral palsy, autism spectrum disorder (ASD), traumatic brain injury (TBI), or age-related degeneration**. They operate as **active medical devices** (typically FDA Class II) that assist, augment, or replace lost motor/sensory function through **sensorimotor coupling** between the robot's end-effector and the patient's anatomical segment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OEBMT834), therapeutic robots are differentiated into three functional tiers:

| Tier | Function | Target User | Example |
|------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Rehabilitation Robot** | Restores lost function through repetitive task practice | Stroke, SCI, post-surgical | Lokomat, Armeo |
| **Assistive Robot** | Compensates for permanent loss | Chronic disability, elderly | Wheelchair-mounted arm (JACO) |
| **Socially Assistive Robot (SAR)** | Provides engagement, motivation, cognitive scaffolding | Autism, dementia, paediatrics | PARO, NAO, KASPAR |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Therapeutic Robot as a "Patient, Tireless Physical Therapist":**
> Imagine you are recovering from a stroke and need to perform 1,000 repetitions of "reaching for a cup" to rewire your brain (neuroplasticity). A human therapist tires after 50 reps and cannot move at the same speed, angle, or force every single time. A therapeutic robot, however, can do **10,000 perfect reps** a day, with **sub-millimetre precision**, while measuring every Newton of your arm's force and every degree of joint angle.
> 
> Just like an autopilot "co-pilots" a plane — it doesn't replace the pilot (the patient's nervous system), but it **assists, corrects, and measures** so the human can relearn the skill safely.

## 1.3 Why Therapeutic Robots Matter

- **Dose-intensity problem**: Clinical trials demand **3,000–4,000 movements/session** for neuroplastic recovery — impossible manually.
- **Quantitative outcome metrics**: Force, position, stiffness, smoothness — all digitally logged.
- **Standardization**: Same therapy delivered in **Kerala, Tokyo, or Boston**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Standard Reach Workspace for Upper Limb Therapy:** **0.5 m radius** hemisphere centred at the shoulder.
- **Maximum Safe End-Effector Force on Human Limb:** **50 N** (per ISO 13482 / ISO/TS 15066).
- **Maximum Safe End-Effector Speed for Therapeutic Contact:** **250 mm/s**.
- **Repetition Density Target for Neuroplasticity:** **> 800 movements/session**.
- **Typical Backdriveable Impedance Range:** **0–500 N/m** stiffness, **0–50 Ns/m** damping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy of Therapeutic Robots

### A. By Mechanical Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **End-Effector Based Robot:** Robot holds the patient's distal segment (hand/foot). Example: **InMotion ARM (MIT-Manus)**. Simple mechanics, but cannot isolate single-joint motion.
> - **Exoskeleton Robot:** Robot segments are anthropomorphic and align with patient's anatomical joints. Example: **Lokomat** (lower limb), **Armeo Power** (upper limb). Higher precision, but requires joint alignment (misalignment = patient discomfort / injury).
> - **Wearable Soft Robot:** Fabric/textile-based actuators. Example: **Myosuit** (lower limb assistance). Lightweight, but lower force output (< 30 N).

### B. By Control Philosophy

The robot's behaviour is governed by one of three classical control paradigms:

| Control Type | Robot Behaviour | Clinical Use |
|--------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Position Control (Robotic) | Robot dictates trajectory; patient is passive | Acute flaccid paralysis |
| **Impedance Control** | Robot behaves like a **virtual spring-damper**; patient can deviate but feels restoring force | Active-assisted therapy (most common) |
| **Task-Oriented (Error-Augmenting) Control** | Robot deliberately **amplifies** patient's deviation to force adaptation | Advanced motor re-learning |

## 2.2 Impedance Control — The Heart of Therapy

> [!NOTE]
> **Impedance Control** models the robot-patient interaction as a **mechanical admittance/impedance network**: the robot measures the patient's applied force $F_h$ and produces a velocity $v$ (or position $x$), or vice-versa. The relationship is governed by a **second-order mass-spring-damper equation**.

The classic impedance equation at the robot's end-effector:

$$
M(\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K(x - x_d) = -F_h
$$

Where:
- $M$ = **virtual inertia** (kg)
- $B$ = **virtual damping** (Ns/m)
- $K$ = **virtual stiffness** (N/m)
- $x$ = actual end-effector position
- $x_d$ = desired/reference trajectory
- $F_h$ = force applied **by** the patient **on** the robot

**Physical meaning:** The robot behaves like a programmable spring. If $K$ is **high**, the robot is stiff and corrects deviations strongly (good for guiding a weak limb). If $K$ is **low**, the robot is compliant and lets the patient lead (good for measuring true intention).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below contains every equation you may be required to reproduce or apply in the KTU ESE for Module 4.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Impedance Law** | $M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_h$ | $M$ [kg], $B$ [Ns/m], $K$ [N/m], $F_h$ [N], $e = x - x_d$ [m] | Foundation of compliant therapeutic control |
| 2 | **Admittance Law** | $M\ddot{x} = F_h + F_{robot}$ | $F_{robot}$ is controller output | Opposite of impedance — robot senses force, outputs motion |
| 3 | **Critically Damped Condition** | $B = 2\sqrt{MK}$ | B in Ns/m | Boundary between oscillation and sluggishness |
| 4 | **Natural Frequency** | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ | rad/s | Determines responsiveness of virtual spring |
| 5 | **Damping Ratio** | $\zeta = B / (2\sqrt{MK})$ | dimensionless | 0 < ζ < 1 = underdamped (preferred for therapy) |
| 6 | **Haptic Stiffness Range (KTU-safe)** | $K \in [0,\,500]$ N/m | N/m | Clipped per ISO 13482 safety limits |
| 7 | **Repeat Dose for Neuroplasticity** | $N \geq 800$ reps/session | dimensionless | Kleim & Jones, 2008 — basis for robotic justification |
| 8 | **Fitts' Law (Therapy Targeting)** | $T = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ | $T$ time [s], $D$ distance [m], $W$ target width [m] | Used to grade robotic game difficulty |
| 9 | **Wolf Motor Function Test Score Improvement** | $\Delta_{WMFT} = k \cdot N_{sessions}$ | dimensionless | Empirical metric cited in robot therapy RCTs |
| 10 | **Power Consumption (Pneumatic Soft Robot)** | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$ | $\Delta p$ pressure [Pa], $Q$ flow [m³/s] | Selection of compressor for soft therapeutic robots |
| 11 | **Coulomb Friction Compensation** | $\tau_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{q})$ | $\tau$ [Nm], $\mu$ coefficient, $F_N$ [N] | Required for backdriveability |
| 12 | **Workspace Volume (Spherical)** | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | $r$ [m] | Bounds reachable therapy space |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer scripts, **never write bare absolute-value bars** $ \vert x \vert $ inside a markdown table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or "mod x" instead — this preserves table formatting integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Therapeutic robots are deployed in **three engineering-rich contexts**:

1. **Hospital Rehabilitation Wards** — Lokomat, Armeo Spring, InMotion ARM used 8 hours/day by 10+ patients (utilisation drives ROI).
2. **Home-Based Tele-Rehabilitation** — InMotion HOME, Hocoma Valedo — connects via cloud so a therapist in Trivandrum can monitor a patient in Kannur.
3. **Pediatric ASD Therapy** — NAO, QTrobot, KASPAR — uses social embodiment to elicit turn-taking, joint attention, and imitation in autistic children.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Second Law to Impedance Equation

The therapeutic robot's end-effector is modelled as a single mass $M$ interacting with the human limb. We start from Newton's second law applied to the **combined robot-patient system at the contact point**:

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the contact mass:**

Three forces act at the end-effector:
- The **restoring force** of the virtual spring: $-K(x - x_d)$
- The **damping force** of the virtual damper: $-B(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d)$
- The **external force** applied by the patient's hand: $F_h$

Newton's second law states $F = ma$:

$$
M\ddot{x} = -K(x - x_d) - B(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + F_h
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange all controller-dependent terms to the LHS:**

Subtract the controller forces from both sides:

$$
M\ddot{x} + B(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K(x - x_d) = F_h
$$

**Step 3 — Define the tracking error $e = x - x_d$:**

Recognise that $\dot{e} = \dot{x} - \dot{x}_d$ and $\ddot{e} = \ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d$ (since $x_d$ is the desired trajectory, which is assumed smooth). Substituting:

$$
M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = F_h
$$

**Step 4 — Apply KTU sign convention (force exerted BY robot ON patient is negative of force exerted BY patient ON robot):**

The force the patient applies on the robot is $F_h$; the force the robot applies back on the patient is $-F_h$ (Newton's third law). Therefore, in the **standard KTU/Hogan impedance convention**, we write:

$$
\boxed{M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_h}
$$

This is the **canonical impedance equation** tested in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Damping Boundary

A therapeutic robot must never oscillate at the end-effector, because oscillation in a robotic exoskeleton will cause **involuntary muscle spasms** in the patient.

A standard second-order system $M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = 0$ has the characteristic equation:

$$
Ms^2 + Bs + K = 0
$$

Solving for the roots $s$:

$$
s = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4MK}}{2M}
$$

The system is **critically damped** when the discriminant equals zero — i.e., when the square root vanishes:

$$
B^2 - 4MK = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; B = 2\sqrt{MK}
$$

Therefore, the **critical damping coefficient** is:

$$
\boxed{B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{MK}}
$$

The **damping ratio** is defined as:

$$
\zeta = \frac{B}{B_{crit}} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU:**
- $\zeta < 1$ → underdamped → oscillatory (avoid)
- $\zeta = 1$ → critically damped → fastest non-oscillatory return (ideal)
- $\zeta > 1$ → overdamped → sluggish (annoying to patient)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Impedance Parameters by Patient Condition

> [!TIP]
> The matrix below is a frequently-tested KTU long-answer (14-mark) sub-question. Memorise the parameter ranges.

| Patient Condition | Motor Capacity (MRC Scale) | $K$ (N/m) | $B$ (Ns/m) | Therapy Mode |
|-------------------|----------------------------|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Acute Flaccidity (Stroke day 1–7)** | 0/5 | 400–500 | 15–25 | Position-control + high impedance |
| **Subacute (week 2–6)** | 1–2/5 | 200–350 | 10–20 | Impedance, soft guidance |
| **Chronic (month 3+)** | 3–4/5 | 50–150 | 5–15 | Error-augmenting / low stiffness |
| **Near-Normal (sports rehab)** | 4+/5 | 0–50 | 2–5 | Free motion / measurement only |

**Reading the matrix:** As the patient regains strength, **lower the stiffness** and **damping** so the robot demands more of the patient. This forces the CNS to recruit more motor units, accelerating cortical reorganisation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A therapeutic end-effector robot is configured with virtual inertia $M = 2$ kg and virtual stiffness $K = 200$ N/m. The patient applies a constant force $F_h = 10$ N opposing the desired motion. Calculate (i) the critical damping coefficient, (ii) the damping ratio if the actual damping is $B = 35$ Ns/m, and (iii) the steady-state position error.

**Solution:**

**(i) Critical damping:**

$$
B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{MK} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 200} = 2\sqrt{400} = 2 \times 20 = 40 \text{ Ns/m}
$$

**(ii) Damping ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \frac{B}{B_{crit}} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875
$$

Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — the patient's hand will oscillate slightly around the target before settling. Clinically acceptable but not ideal.

**(iii) Steady-state error:**

At steady state, $\ddot{e} = \dot{e} = 0$. The impedance equation reduces to:

$$
Ke_{ss} = -F_h \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e_{ss} = -\frac{F_h}{K} = -\frac{10}{200} = -0.05 \text{ m} = -5 \text{ cm}
$$

The patient can displace the robot by **5 cm** from the desired trajectory against a 10 N push — clinically desirable, since this confirms the patient has volitional control.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Therapeutic Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Limb / Anatomical Segment] -->|Force / Position| B[Sensor Array FSR Encoders EMG]
    B -->|Raw Signals| C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    C -->|Conditioned Data| D[Controller DSP MCU x86 Industrial PC]
    D -->|Reference vs Actual Error| E[Impedance Engine Virtual M B K]
    E -->|Control Signal| F[Driver Stage PWM H-Bridge Servo]
    F -->|Power| G[Actuators DC Motor Pneumatic McKibben]
    G -->|Mechanical Output| H[End Effector or Exoskeleton Joint]
    H -->|Therapeutic Movement| A
    D -->|Telemetry| I[Clinical Dashboard Therapist GUI]
    I -->|Session Tuning Parameters| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as a closed loop:** Patient moves → sensor measures → controller computes error → impedance engine decides corrective action → actuator delivers safe motion → patient feels guided movement. The therapist observes the dashboard and remotely tunes $M$, $B$, $K$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a therapeutic robot. Differentiate between an end-effector based robot and an exoskeleton based robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A therapeutic robot is a programmable mechatronic device that delivers clinically validated physical or cognitive interventions to patients with impairments, operating as an FDA Class II active medical device. (1 mark)

**End-effector based robot:** The robot makes contact with the patient only at the distal segment (e.g., hand or foot); the limb's proximal joints move freely in response to the end-effector's motion. Simple mechanical structure, but cannot isolate a single joint. Example: MIT-Manus. (1 mark)

**Exoskeleton based robot:** The robot has anthropomorphic links that align with each anatomical joint of the patient. Each joint of the robot maps 1:1 to a joint of the patient. Allows precise single-joint isolation but requires accurate sizing/alignment. Example: Lokomat. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is impedance control? Why is it preferred over rigid position control in stroke rehabilitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Impedance control is a control strategy in which the robot behaves as a programmable **mass-spring-damper** system, modulating the relationship between the force exerted by the patient and the resulting motion of the robot, governed by $M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_h$. (2 marks)

It is preferred because it allows safe, compliant interaction; accommodates the patient's involuntary tremors and spasms; and supports active patient participation, which is essential for **neuroplasticity-driven motor recovery**. Rigid position control would forcibly correct the limb and could injure the patient or suppress volitional effort. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the architecture of a therapeutic robot. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the impedance control equation $M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_h$ starting from Newton's second law and explain each physical parameter. For a stroke rehabilitation robot, justify the typical choice of $M = 2$ kg, $B = 20$ Ns/m, and $K = 200$ N/m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram & Architecture (7 marks):**

The therapeutic robot consists of six interconnected blocks:

1. **Patient-Interface Module** — End-effector gripper, hand cuff, or exoskeleton shell. This is the physical contact between robot and patient. (1 mark)
2. **Sensor Suite** — Optical encoders for joint position; 6-axis force/torque sensor (e.g., ATI Nano17) for end-effector force; surface EMG for muscle activity; IMU for limb orientation. (1.5 marks)
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplifies low-level sensor signals (mV range EMG), filters 50/60 Hz mains noise, digitises via 16-bit ADC at ≥ 1 kHz. (1 mark)
4. **Controller (DSP / Industrial PC)** — Runs real-time control loop at 1 kHz; computes error between actual and desired trajectory; runs impedance engine. (1 mark)
5. **Driver Stage** — PWM servo drivers, H-bridges for DC motors, valve drivers for pneumatic muscles. (1 mark)
6. **Actuator Stage** — Brushless DC servos with harmonic drives for high torque; series elastic actuators (SEA) for inherent compliance and safety; pneumatic McKibben muscles for soft robots. (1 mark)

The therapist's GUI sits on top of the controller for parameter tuning and outcome display (Fugl-Meyer, WMFT scores). (0.5 marks)

**(b) Impedance Equation Derivation & Justification (7 marks):**

**Derivation:** *(Reproduce the 4-step derivation from Section 3.1 verbatim — 4 marks)*

**Parameter Justification (3 marks):**

- **$M = 2$ kg** — virtual inertia at the end-effector. A low virtual inertia is chosen so the robot does not "feel heavy" to the patient. Real-world robot link mass may be 5–8 kg, but the controller cancels the apparent inertia via feed-forward, leaving only 2 kg of perceived inertia. This matches the inertia of a typical human forearm (~1.5–2.5 kg).
- **$B = 20$ Ns/m** — provides **under-damped** behaviour (ζ ≈ 0.71 with $K = 200$). This gives smooth, slightly compliant motion without overshoot — ideal for an actively participating subacute patient.
- **$K = 200$ N/m** — provides **moderate guidance**. The patient can deviate by 5 cm per 10 N of applied force — sufficient to record volitional intent but low enough to correct severe spasticity.

These parameters keep the **steady-state error within ±5 cm**, satisfying both the **ISO 13482 safety limit on end-effector force** (< 50 N) and the **clinical requirement for volitional engagement**.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Classify therapeutic robots based on their function and explain with examples how socially assistive robots (SAR) are used in autism therapy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A therapeutic robot end-effector is programmed with $M = 3$ kg, $K = 300$ N/m. The patient pushes with $F_h = 15$ N. Calculate (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the critical damping coefficient, and (iii) the steady-state error. Comment on the clinical acceptability of the steady-state error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification & SAR for Autism (7 marks):**

**Classification (4 marks):**
- **Rehabilitation Robots** — restore function (Lokomat, Armeo Power)
- **Assistive Robots** — substitute lost function (JACO arm, Obi feeder)
- **Socially Assistive Robots (SAR)** — provide social/cognitive engagement (NAO, PARO, KASPAR)

**SAR for Autism (3 marks):**
SARs like **NAO (Aldebaran/SoftBank)** and **KASPAR (Univ. of Hertfordshire)** engage autistic children through predictable, simplified social cues. The robot performs turn-taking games, imitation exercises ("robot copies child, then child copies robot"), and joint-attention tasks. Compared to a human therapist, the SAR is **infinitely patient, never emotionally reactive, and offers reduced sensory overload** (no facial micro-expressions, no accent variation). Clinical RCTs (e.g., Pennisi et al., 2016) show improved **joint attention** and **imitation scores** in ASD children after 8–12 weeks of SAR sessions.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $M = 3$ kg, $K = 300$ N/m, $F_h = 15$ N.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{300}{3}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Critical damping:**

$$
B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{MK} = 2\sqrt{3 \times 300} = 2\sqrt{900} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Ns/m}
$$

**(iii) Steady-state error:**

$$
e_{ss} = -\frac{F_h}{K} = -\frac{15}{300} = -0.05 \text{ m} = -5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Clinical Acceptability (1 mark):** A 5 cm deviation in a typical upper-limb reaching task (workspace ≈ 50 cm) represents a **10% spatial error** — clinically acceptable for subacute patients as it confirms volitional motor effort without losing task accuracy. For high-precision tasks (e.g., writing), stiffness should be increased to reduce this error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (as observed in KTU Board Valuation):**
> 1. **Sign error in impedance equation** — writing $Ke = +F_h$ instead of $-F_h$. Costs **1 full mark** in derivation questions.
> 2. **Confusing admittance and impedance control** — Admittance senses force and outputs motion (position-controlled robot); Impedance senses motion and outputs force (torque-controlled robot). Many students write the equations swapped.
> 3. **Writing bare vertical bars $|x|$ inside a markdown table** — this breaks KTU digital valuation portals. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or "mod x".
> 4. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet** — every KTU model answer script **must include SI units** in square brackets. Unanswered unit = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Naming a non-existent robot** — never invent model names. Stick to validated devices: Lokomat, Armeo, MIT-Manus, NAO, PARO, InMotion ARM.
> 6. **Skipping the clinical justification** — engineering answers without a one-line "why this matters to the patient" sentence lose the **application-level marks** at the top of Bloom's pyramid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Therapeutic Robots (Module 4)**

- [x] A **therapeutic robot** is a programmable mechatronic device delivering clinically validated physical or cognitive therapy.
- [x] Three tiers: **Rehabilitation**, **Assistive**, **Socially Assistive (SAR)**.
- [x] Two architectures: **End-effector** (distal contact) vs **Exoskeleton** (anthropomorphic joint mapping).
- [x] Master equation: $\;M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_h\;$ — impedance control.
- [x] Critical damping: $\;B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{MK}\;$ ; Damping ratio: $\;\zeta = B / B_{crit}\;$.
- [x] Steady-state error: $\;e_{ss} = -F_h / K\;$ — inversely proportional to stiffness.
- [x] Natural frequency: $\;\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}\;$ — therapeutic responsiveness.
- [x] Neuroplasticity dose: **≥ 800 reps/session** (Kleim & Jones principle).
- [x] Safety ceiling: **< 50 N end-effector force**, **< 250 mm/s speed** (ISO 13482).
- [x] Examples to memorise: **Lokomat** (lower-limb exoskeleton), **Armeo Power** (upper-limb exoskeleton), **MIT-Manus / InMotion ARM** (end-effector), **NAO** & **PARO** (SAR), **JACO** (assistive).
- [x] Key clinical metrics: **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA)**, **Wolf Motor Function Test (WMFT)**, **Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS)**.
- [x] SAR for autism: predictable, simplified social cues; reduced sensory overload; improved joint attention and imitation.
- [x] Always **sign-convention-check** the impedance equation and **always include units** in every KTU answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telecommunications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Telecommunications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Telecommunications in Rehabilitation Engineering** refers to the interdisciplinary branch of biomedical/rehabilitation engineering concerned with the design, adaptation, and deployment of electronic communication systems, devices, and protocols that enable individuals with sensory, motor, speech, or cognitive impairments to transmit and receive information over distance with efficacy equivalent to able-bodied users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> Telecommunications for rehabilitation encompasses the modification of standard telecommunication infrastructure (PSTN, mobile networks, IP-based systems) and the engineering of specialized peripheral devices (TTY/TDD, relay gateways, hearing-aid compatible handsets, AAC-integrated phones) that compensate for sensory, speech, or motor deficits, thereby ensuring compliance with universal access standards such as FCC Section 255, WCAG 2.1, and ITU-T E.161.

### Sub-Domains Covered Under the KTU Module
| Sub-Topic | Primary Disability Addressed |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| TTY / TDD Systems | Deaf / Hard-of-Hearing |
| Text Telephony (V.18, V.21) | Deaf / Speech-Impaired |
| Relay Services (TRS, VRS, IP-CTS) | Deaf, Hard-of-Hearing, Deaf-Blind |
| Hearing Aid Compatibility (HAC) | Hearing Aid Wearers |
| AAC Phone Integration | Speech-Impaired (ALS, Cerebral Palsy) |
| Augmentative Mobile Access (Smart Phones) | Visual, Motor, Cognitive |
| Accessible Video Communication | Deaf, Hard-of-Hearing, Deaf-Blind |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Universal Bridge" Analogy**
> Imagine a city's main highway system (the regular phone network) — but the entrance ramps are too high for wheelchairs, the road signs are written in a foreign language for some drivers, and the toll booths don't have sound amplifiers. **Rehabilitation Telecommunications is the engineering team that rebuilds the ramps, translates the signs, and installs amplifiers** so *every* citizen — regardless of disability — can travel the same information highway.
> 
> Concretely: A **TTY (TeleTYpewriter)** is to a deaf person what **ramps** are to a wheelchair user. A **relay operator** is to a deaf caller what a **sign-language interpreter** is to a foreign dignitary at a press conference — a real-time human (or AI) bridge between two different "languages" of communication.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used Throughout This Module**
> - **Standard Voice Frequency Band:** **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** (ITU-T G.711, Public Switched Telephone Network)
> - **Reference Sound Pressure Level:** **20 µPa** (Threshold of hearing, 0 dB SPL)
> - **Reference Magnetic Field Strength:** **1 A/m** (HAC Telecoil T-rating reference)
> - **Standard Data Rate (Baud) for 45.45 bps TTY:** Originate mode = **1270 Hz**, Answer mode = **2225 Hz** (ITU-T V.18)
> - **Hearing Aid Compatibility M-Rating Range:** **M1 to M4** (Higher = Better RF immunity)
> - **Hearing Aid Compatibility T-Rating Range:** **T1 to T4** (Higher = Stronger telecoil coupling)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Engineering Pillars of Rehabilitation Telecommunications

### Pillar 1: Signal Transformation (Input/Output Substitution)
When a user cannot produce or receive one modality of signal (e.g., voice), the system must **transform** it to an accessible modality (text, large print, vibration, Braille).

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **Acquire** the input signal from the user (typed text, eye-gaze, sip-and-puff, brain-computer interface).
2. **Encode** the signal into a standard telecommunications protocol (ASCII, Baudot, SIP packets).
3. **Transmit** via the chosen physical layer (copper pair, RF, optical fiber, satellite).
4. **Decode** at the receiving end.
5. **Render** the signal in the recipient's accessible format (synthesized speech, refreshable Braille, captioned video).

### Pillar 2: Channel Adaptation (Bandwidth & Modulation)
Standard voice channels (4 kHz analog, 64 kbps PCM digital) were designed assuming a verbal caller. For text/symbol users, the channel must be re-engineered.

**Why this matters (KTU high-yield):**
- Baudot code (5-bit) → 45.45 bps over voice channel
- ASCII (7-bit) → 300 bps minimum (ITU-T V.18)
- Modern IP-based SIP → 64 kbps – 2 Mbps
- Video for sign language → minimum 256 kbps (H.264) – 2 Mbps (H.265) for natural signing

### Pillar 3: Compatibility & Coupling (HAC)
A hearing aid user must couple their in-ear device to the phone without acoustic feedback. Two coupling modes exist:
- **Acoustic (M-rating):** Microphone picks up phone speaker output.
- **Inductive (T-rating):** Telecoil in hearing aid picks up phone's magnetic field (eliminating acoustic feedback path).

## 2.2 TTY/TDD Operational Theory

The **TeleTYpewriter / Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (TDD)** is the cornerstone rehabilitative telecommunications terminal. It predates the internet and is still mandated by many regulatory frameworks.

**How it works (the "Why" and "How"):**
- A keyboard generates a 5-bit Baudot code.
- A modem (acoustic coupler or direct-connect) converts digital bits into two-tone **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** tones within the 300–3400 Hz voice band.
- The *originating* device transmits at **1270 Hz (mark) / 1070 Hz (space)**.
- The *answering* device transmits at **2225 Hz (mark) / 2025 Hz (space)**.
- Half-duplex: only one party types at a time; a GA (Go Ahead) signal yields the line.

## 2.3 Relay Services Theory

A **Telecommunications Relay Service (TRS)** is a human- or AI-mediated bridge between a TTY/text user and a voice user.

**Variants (KTU must-know):**
| Relay Type | Acronym | End-User Pair | Mechanism |
|-----------|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Text Relay | TRS | TTY ↔ Voice | Operator types/reads |
| Voice Carry Over | VCO | Deaf speaker ↔ Hearing listener | User speaks; operator transcribes |
| Hearing Carry Over | HCO | Hearing speaker ↔ Deaf listener | User listens; operator types |
| Speech-to-Speech | STS | Speech-impaired ↔ Voice | Trained operator re-voices |
| Video Relay Service | VRS | Sign-language ↔ Voice | Live sign-language interpreter via webcam |
| IP Captioned Telephone | IP-CTS | Hard-of-Hearing ↔ Voice | Automated speech-to-text captions |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are high-frequency KTU exam topics. Master these.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Standard |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/sec, B in Hz |
| 2 | SNR in dB | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | dB |
| 3 | SNR from voltage (HAC test) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | dB |
| 4 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right),\ p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB SPL |
| 5 | Magnetic Field Coupling (HAC T) | $L_H = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{H}{H_0}\right),\ H_0 = 1\ \text{A/m}$ | dB (A/m) |
| 6 | TTY Baudot bit rate | $R_b = 45.45$ bps = 5 bits × 9.09 char/s | bits/sec |
| 7 | Minimum video bit rate (sign) | $R_{min} = 256$ kbps (H.264 CIF) | kbps |
| 8 | Bandwidth of FSK signal (Carson's rule) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Hz |
| 9 | Modem symbol rate (Nyquist) | $R_s = 2B$ for noiseless channel | baud |
| 10 | Hearing Aid Sum Rating | $\text{HAC}_{\text{TOTAL}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{rating}} + T_{\text{rating}}}{2}$ | — |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Shannon Capacity (Eq 1)** is used by rehabilitation engineers to design *minimum-viable* video codecs for sign-language transmission over constrained rural bandwidths.
- **HAC equations (Eq 3, 5)** are mandated by **FCC 47 CFR §68.4** for every cell phone sold in the USA and increasingly required under Indian Universal Design standards.
- **Carson's rule (Eq 8)** is the engineering reason TTY tones (1070–2225 Hz) fit comfortably in the 4 kHz voice band without bleeding into adjacent channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: TTY Tone Spacing Using Carson's Rule

**Problem (KTU-style):** A TTY modem uses FSK tones at 1070 Hz (space) and 1270 Hz (mark) for the originate mode. The modulating data rate is 45.45 bps. Calculate the transmission bandwidth using Carson's rule and verify the signal stays within the standard voice band (300 – 3400 Hz).

**Step 1 — Identify the frequency deviation $\Delta f$.**
The deviation is half the peak-to-peak separation between the two FSK tones.

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\vert 1270 - 1070 \vert}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum modulating frequency $f_m$.**
For binary NRZ data at $R_b = 45.45$ bps, the fundamental spectral component is:

$$
f_m = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{45.45}{2} = 22.725\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Carson's rule.**

$$
BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(100 + 22.725) = 2(122.725) = 245.45\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify spectral containment.**
The lower band edge is $1070 - (BW/2) = 1070 - 122.725 = 947.275$ Hz.
The upper band edge is $1270 + (BW/2) = 1270 + 122.725 = 1392.725$ Hz.

Since $947.275\ \text{Hz} \geq 300\ \text{Hz}$ and $1392.725\ \text{Hz} \leq 3400\ \text{Hz}$, the TTY signal **fits cleanly** within the PSTN voice channel. This is a derivation the KTU examiner loves.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: HAC M-Rating SNR Calculation

**Problem:** A cell phone's RF emission is measured. The signal voltage (induced in a hearing aid pickup coil) is $V_s = 2.5$ mV, and the noise voltage is $V_n = 0.5$ mV. Calculate the SNR in dB and determine the M-rating per ANSI C63.19.

**Step 1 — Apply the voltage-SNR formula.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{0.5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(5)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\log_{10}(5) = 0.69897
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \times 0.69897 = 13.98\ \text{dB} \approx 14\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to M-rating per ANSI C63.19 (KTU reference table).**

| SNR (dB) | M-Rating |
|----------|----------|
| > 20 | M4 (Best) |
| 16 – 20 | M3 |
| 12 – 16 | M2 |
| 8 – 12 | M1 |
| < 8 | M0 (Fail) |

Our calculated **14 dB → M2** classification.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Minimum Bandwidth for Sign-Language Video

**Problem:** A VRS service delivers 30 fps, 352×288 (CIF) resolution, 24-bit color, H.264 compressed at ratio 50:1. Compute the bit rate and verify against the 256 kbps minimum sign-language threshold.

**Step 1 — Uncompressed raw bit rate.**

$$
R_{raw} = W \times H \times f \times b = 352 \times 288 \times 30 \times 24
$$

$$
R_{raw} = 352 \times 288 = 101{,}376\ \text{pixels/frame}
$$

$$
R_{raw} = 101{,}376 \times 30 = 3{,}041{,}280\ \text{pixels/sec}
$$

$$
R_{raw} = 3{,}041{,}280 \times 24 = 72{,}990{,}720\ \text{bits/sec} \approx 73\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed bit rate (50:1).**

$$
R_{comp} = \frac{73\ \text{Mbps}}{50} = 1.46\ \text{Mbps} = 1460\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with sign-language threshold.**

$$
R_{comp} = 1460\ \text{kbps} \gg 256\ \text{kbps}\ \text{(minimum for VRS)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The link comfortably supports natural signing with head, hand, and facial-expression detail (mandatory for grammatical sign languages such as ASL/ISL).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Device Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination-ready comparison table — memorize the relative order.**

| Parameter | TTY/TDD | IP-CTS | VRS (H.264) | Smart-Phone AAC App |
|-----------|---------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Bandwidth | 0.3 – 3.4 kHz | 64 kbps | 256 kbps – 2 Mbps | 64 kbps – 2 Mbps |
| Latency | < 250 ms | 1 – 3 s (captioning) | 200 – 500 ms | Variable |
| Code/Protocol | Baudot 45.45 bps | SIP + STT | SIP + H.264 | SIP/VoIP |
| User Modality | Text ↔ Text | Voice ↔ Captions | Sign ↔ Voice | Synthesized speech ↔ Voice |
| Operator Required | Yes (TRS) | No (Automated) | Yes (Certified) | No |
| Disability Served | Deaf | Hard-of-Hearing | Deaf (sign users) | Speech-impaired |
| Power | 5 V, < 1 W | Mains/USB | Mains/USB | Battery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: TTY/TDD System Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Keyboard Input] --> B[Baudot Encoder 5-bit]
    B --> C[FSK Modem 1070/1270 Hz]
    C --> D[Acoustic Coupler or Direct Connect]
    D --> E[PSTN Voice Channel 300-3400 Hz]
    E --> F[Remote FSK Demodulator]
    F --> G[Baudot Decoder]
    G --> H[Printer or Visual Display]
    H --> I[Remote TTY User]
    J[Voice Caller Microphone] --> E
    E --> K[Earpiece Speaker]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):** A text user presses keys, characters are encoded to 5-bit Baudot, modulated as FSK tones, fed into the standard phone line, demodulated at the far end, decoded back to text, and printed/displayed. A voice caller can be bridged only via a relay operator (TRS).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Video Relay Service (VRS) End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DeafUserSide["Deaf User Side"]
        A1[Sign Language Video Camera] --> A2[H.264 Encoder]
        A2 --> A3[SIP Client Mobile App]
    end
    subgraph RelayCenter["VRS Relay Center"]
        B1[SIP Router] --> B2[Certified Sign Interpreter]
        B2 --> B3[VoIP Gateway]
    end
    subgraph HearingUserSide["Hearing User Side"]
        C1[POTS or Mobile Phone] --> C2[Voice Audio Path]
    end
    A3 -->|Internet| B1
    B3 -->|PSTN| C1
    C2 -->|Return audio| B3
    B3 -->|Return video| A3
```

**Engineering note:** The asymmetric bandwidth allocation (256 kbps upstream for sign video, 64 kbps downstream voice) is engineered to match human signing speed (~1 sign per second, ~5–8 handshapes per second).

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hearing Aid Compatible (HAC) Phone Coupling Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phone Audio Source] --> B{Mode Selector}
    B -->|Acoustic Path| C[Phone Speaker]
    C --> D[Air Gap Vibration]
    D --> E[Hearing Aid Microphone]
    B -->|Inductive Path| F[Telecoil Magnetic Loop]
    F --> G[Hearing Aid Telecoil T-coil]
    E --> H[Amplified Audio to Ear]
    G --> H
```

**Why two paths?** The acoustic path is universal (works for any hearing aid), but suffers from acoustic feedback ("whistling") and ambient noise. The inductive telecoil path bypasses the microphone entirely, yielding cleaner signal-to-noise ratio — which is exactly what the **T-rating** quantifies.

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: IP Captioned Telephone Service (IP-CTS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hard-of-Hearing User Speaks] --> B[VoIP Client on Phone]
    B --> C[Cloud SIP Server]
    C --> D[ASR Engine Speech-to-Text]
    D --> E[Captions Displayed on Phone Screen]
    F[Hearing User Voice] --> B
    B -->|Audio Return| F
```

**Real-world deployed examples (for KTU context):** Hamilton CapTel, CaptionCall, InnoCaption — all U.S. FCC-mandated services.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define TTY/TDD. List any four features that make it accessible for a deaf user.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A TTY (TeleTYpewriter) or TDD (Telecommunications Device for the Deaf) is a peripheral device that transmits and receives typed text over standard telephone lines using Baudot-coded FSK tones in the 300–3400 Hz voice band.
- **Features (½ mark each, any four):**
  1. Visual character display for read-back of received text
  2. Standard 4-row QWERTY keyboard for familiar typing
  3. Direct-connect or acoustic coupler for line interface
  4. Auto-answer capability with prerecorded GA (Go Ahead) message
  5. Built-in printer for hard-copy record
  6. Half-duplex handshake protocol (GA/GA SK)

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a Telecommunications Relay Service (TRS)? Distinguish between VRS and IP-CTS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TRS Definition (1 mark):** A TRS is a service that enables deaf, hard-of-hearing, deaf-blind, or speech-impaired users to communicate with voice telephone users via the assistance of a relay operator (human or automated).
- **VRS (1 mark):** Video Relay Service uses a live **sign-language interpreter** over video link between a deaf sign-user and a voice caller. Requires broadband video.
- **IP-CTS (1 mark):** IP Captioned Telephone Service uses **automated speech recognition (ASR)** to display real-time captions of the voice caller's speech on the hard-of-hearing user's phone screen. No video needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **operational principle, signal characteristics, and protocol stack** of a TTY/TDD system. Include the FSK tone frequencies, Baudot encoding, and Carson's-rule bandwidth analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **engineering principles of Hearing Aid Compatibility (HAC)** as defined by ANSI C63.19. Explain the M-rating and T-rating, derive the SNR formula, and illustrate the two coupling paths (acoustic and inductive) with a labeled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B Question A

#### Part (a) — TTY/TDD Operational Principle (7 marks)

**1. Purpose and Origin (1 mark):**
The TTY/TDD was invented by Robert Weitbrecht in 1964 (deaf, physicist) to enable deaf individuals to use the existing PSTN. It is a **half-duplex** text terminal.

**2. Signal Encoding (2 marks):**
Characters are encoded using the **5-bit Baudot (ITA-2) code**, giving $2^5 = 32$ possible symbols — sufficient for the 26-letter English alphabet (with a "letters/figures" shift), carriage return, line feed, and control codes. The bit rate is standardized at **45.45 bps** (≈ 60 words per minute typing speed).

**3. FSK Modulation (2 marks):**
The digital Baudot stream is converted to audio using **Frequency Shift Keying**:
- **Originate mode:** Mark (binary 1) = 1270 Hz; Space (binary 0) = 1070 Hz
- **Answer mode:** Mark = 2225 Hz; Space = 2025 Hz
- The two modes are used simultaneously in a two-way call to prevent the near-end signal from being heard on the far-end speaker.

**4. Bandwidth Verification (1 mark):**
Using Carson's rule:
- $\Delta f = (1270 - 1070)/2 = 100$ Hz
- $f_m = 45.45/2 = 22.725$ Hz
- $BW = 2(100 + 22.725) = 245.45$ Hz
- This fits inside the 300–3400 Hz voice channel with comfortable guard bands.

**5. Handshake Protocol (1 mark):**
Half-duplex operation uses a "GA" (Go Ahead) signal: when a user finishes typing, they press the GA key, which transmits a 1.5-second continuous mark tone, yielding the line to the other party.

#### Part (b) — Hearing Aid Compatibility (7 marks)

**1. The Coupling Problem (1 mark):**
A hearing aid user holding a phone to their ear faces two problems: (i) **acoustic feedback** (whistling from the hearing aid microphone picking up its own amplified output), and (ii) **ambient noise** corrupting the signal. HAC engineering addresses both.

**2. The Two Coupling Paths (2 marks):**
- **Acoustic path (M-mode):** Sound waves from the phone speaker vibrate through an air gap to the hearing aid microphone. Universal but noisy.
- **Inductive path (T-mode):** A small coil (telecoil or T-coil) inside the phone generates a **magnetic field** proportional to the audio signal. The hearing aid's telecoil picks up this magnetic field directly, bypassing the microphone and eliminating acoustic feedback.

**3. The M-Rating and T-Rating (2 marks):**
Per ANSI C63.19 (and adopted by FCC 47 CFR §68.4):
- **M1 to M4:** M-rating measures the **immunity** of the hearing aid to RF interference from the phone. M4 = best (immune to > 20 dB SNR).
- **T1 to T4:** T-rating measures the **strength** of the phone's magnetic field coupling to the telecoil. T4 = strongest coupling (> 20 dB above reference 1 A/m).

**4. SNR Derivation (1 mark):**
Given measured signal voltage $V_s$ and noise voltage $V_n$ across a standard test coil:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)
$$

**5. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the HAC coupling block diagram from Section 4.3 of these notes.)* Label: Phone → Speaker → Air → Mic (M-path) AND Phone → Magnetic Loop → T-coil (T-path).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice for Internal Option*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end architecture of a Video Relay Service (VRS)**. Discuss the bandwidth, latency, and codec requirements for natural sign-language transmission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) in the context of mobile telephony**? Describe at least three engineering solutions that enable speech-impaired users (e.g., post-stroke, ALS, cerebral palsy) to make and receive phone calls, with appropriate algorithms or signal-flow descriptions. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B Question B

#### Part (a) — VRS End-to-End Architecture (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):** VRS is a video-mediated relay service enabling deaf sign-language users to communicate with voice telephone users through a certified sign-language interpreter connected via broadband IP.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)* Components: Deaf-side (camera, H.264 encoder, SIP client) → Internet → VRS Center (SIP router, interpreter workstation, VoIP gateway) → PSTN → Voice caller.

**3. Bandwidth (2 marks):** Minimum **256 kbps** for recognizable signing; **512 kbps–2 Mbps** for natural expression including facial grammar (e.g., raised eyebrows for wh-questions in ASL/ISL). H.264 codec at 30 fps, CIF (352×288) resolution is the de facto standard; H.265/HEVC at 1 Mbps gives superior quality at lower bit rate.

**4. Latency (1 mark):** Round-trip latency must be **< 500 ms** to allow natural conversational turn-taking. End-to-end components contributing: camera capture (50 ms) + encoding (100 ms) + network (50–100 ms) + interpreter cognition (200 ms) + decoding (50 ms).

**5. Codec Choice (1 mark):** H.264/AVC preferred over H.265/HEVC for VRS only when legacy device compatibility is required; for new deployments, H.265 saves ~50% bandwidth at equivalent perceptual quality.

#### Part (b) — AAC in Mobile Telephony (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):** AAC integration in mobile telephony refers to engineered phone applications/hardware that synthesize speech from text, symbol, or eye-gaze input, allowing speech-impaired users to place and receive voice calls.

**2. Solution 1 — Text-to-Speech (TTS) Outgoing (2 marks):**
User types (or selects via predictive AAC board) → text is processed by a TTS engine (concatenative or neural) → audio is injected into the phone's voice call stream via the **audio injection API** (e.g., Android `AudioManager.MODE_IN_COMMUNICATION` with synthesized voice replacing mic input). Algorithm: sequence-to-sequence neural TTS (Tacotron 2 + WaveNet) generates ~24 kHz mono PCM at < 200 ms first-packet latency.

**3. Solution 2 — Speech-to-Text (STT) Incoming (2 marks):**
Incoming voice audio is captured → buffered → fed to an on-device or cloud ASR engine (e.g., Whisper, Google Cloud STT) → transcription displayed as scrolling captions on the phone screen. Latency budget: < 1.5 s for usable real-time captioning.

**4. Solution 3 — Predictive Symbol Board Calling (2 marks):**
A grid of high-frequency phrases ("Call my doctor," "I need help") with one-tap dial-and-speak operation. On selection, the AAC app places the call (via SIP/VoIP) and reads the chosen phrase aloud using the phone's speaker (in speakerphone mode) or routes it into the call audio via TTS injection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Traps — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing M-rating with T-rating:** M = Microphone (RF immunity of hearing aid). T = Telecoil (magnetic field strength of phone). Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting Baudot is 5-bit, not ASCII 7/8-bit:** Baudot gives 32 codes only.
> 3. **Wrong FSK frequency pair:** Originate is 1070/1270 Hz. Answer is 2025/2225 Hz. Memorize both.
> 4. **Drawing TTY as full-duplex:** It is **half-duplex** with GA handshake.
> 5. **Using 8 kHz (not 4 kHz) voice band:** PSTN analog bandwidth is 300–3400 Hz, **not** 0–8000 Hz.
> 6. **Shannon formula unit errors:** Use **B in Hz**, S and N in same power units; result in bps.
> 7. **VRS bandwidth mistake:** Do not state 64 kbps — that is voice-only. VRS needs 256 kbps+ for video.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Telecommunications (Module 4)**

- ✅ **TTY/TDD** = Baudot (5-bit, 45.45 bps) + FSK tones (Originate 1070/1270 Hz, Answer 2025/2225 Hz) + half-duplex GA protocol over 300–3400 Hz voice channel.
- ✅ **TRS** = human/AI bridge between text and voice users.
- ✅ **VRS** = sign-language video relay, 256 kbps minimum, < 500 ms latency, H.264 codec.
- ✅ **IP-CTS** = automated ASR captions for hard-of-hearing voice users.
- ✅ **HAC M-rating** = hearing aid RF immunity (M1–M4); **T-rating** = phone telecoil strength (T1–T4).
- ✅ **SNR (dB) for voltage:** $20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ — memorize this differs from the power formula's factor of 10.
- ✅ **Carson's rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — used to verify TTY fits in voice channel.
- ✅ **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — basis for VRS bandwidth budgeting.
- ✅ **PSTN bandwidth = 300–3400 Hz** (not 4 kHz flat, not 8 kHz).
- ✅ **Reference acoustic pressure $p_0 = 20$ µPa; Reference magnetic field $H_0 = 1$ A/m.**
- ✅ **AAC mobile solutions = TTS outgoing + STT incoming + predictive symbol dialer.**
- ✅ **Universal Design standards referenced:** ANSI C63.19, FCC 47 CFR §68.4, ITU-T V.18, ITU-T E.161, WCAG 2.1.
- ✅ **Deaf-Blind users** are served by **TDD + Braille display** combinations (TDD-Braille TTY).
- ✅ **Speech-to-Speech Relay (STS)** is specifically for users with speech impairments (not deafness) — operators re-voice the caller's words more clearly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computers and Web Accessibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computers and Web Accessibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Accessibility** refers to the inclusive practice of designing and configuring computing systems—including hardware, operating systems, application software, and input/output peripherals—such that they are usable by people with the widest possible range of abilities and disabilities. **Web Accessibility**, in turn, is the specialized sub-discipline that ensures websites, web applications, and digital content are perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust for all users, including those relying on assistive technologies.

In the KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering syllabus, the term is governed by the **W3C Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI)** and the **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1/2.2** framework, which codifies the **POUR** principles: **Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Computer and Web Accessibility is not merely a "social good"—it is an **engineering design constraint** with measurable compliance metrics (WCAG Conformance Levels: A, AA, AAA) and a global legal mandate (e.g., the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016** in India, Section 44–46, and the global **UN CRPD Article 9**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### The Analogy of the "Ramp and the Sidewalk"

Imagine a city that wants everyone to reach a famous library downtown. The building itself is the *software* (the library's services and books). Now ask: 

- Can a person in a wheelchair reach the entrance? (This is the **ramp**—an *assistive technology*.)
- Are the sidewalks free of potholes and step-kerbs? (This is *accessibility of the journey*—the **web environment**.)
- Is the door handle a lever rather than a knob? (This is *operable design*.)
- Is the signage in Braille and large print? (This is *perceivable design*.)
- Will the building still be usable if a fire breaks out and lights fail? (This is *robustness*.)

In this analogy:
- The **library** = the *computer/application*
- The **ramps, Braille, lever handles** = *assistive technologies* (screen readers, alternative input devices)
- The **sidewalks, kerb-cuts, road markings** = *web accessibility compliance* (semantic HTML, ARIA, captions)
- The **city planners** = *rehabilitation engineers and web developers*

The unifying engineering insight is that **accessibility is a property of the system + environment taken together**, not of the user alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> 
> | Term | Meaning |
> |------|---------|
> | **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Any device, software, or equipment that maintains or improves the functional capabilities of a person with a disability. |
> | **Universal Design (UD)** | The design of products and environments to be usable by all people, without the need for adaptation or specialized design. |
> | **POUR** | The four pillars of WCAG: **P**erceivable, **O**perable, **U**nderstandable, **R**obust. |
> | **WCAG** | Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — published by the **W3C** as the de facto global standard. |
> | **Screen Reader** | Software that renders text and image content as **synthesized speech** or **Braille output**. |

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Rehabilitation Engineering

For a rehabilitation engineer, computer and web accessibility is the **interface layer** between the user with a disability and the digital world. Without it:

- A screen reader user cannot operate a job portal.
- A person with motor impairment cannot submit a tax return.
- A deaf student cannot access lecture videos without captions.

The rehabilitation engineer's role is to **select, customize, integrate, and evaluate** the AT + accessibility pipeline that makes these interactions possible.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of WCAG — POUR

The WCAG 2.1/2.2 standard organizes every accessibility requirement under the four POUR principles. This is the **central conceptual map** for the entire module.

### 2.1.1 PERCEIVABLE — "Information must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive."

**Why:** A blind user cannot see visual content. A deaf user cannot hear audio. A low-vision user cannot read small text.

**How (Key Guidelines):**
- Provide **text alternatives** for non-text content (alt text for images: `alt="Diagram showing..."`).
- Provide **captions and transcripts** for audio/video.
- Ensure **sufficient color contrast** (minimum ratio **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text under WCAG AA).
- Do not rely on **color alone** to convey information.
- Make content **adaptable** (semantic structure, reflow at 320 CSS pixels).

**Contrast Ratio Formula (WCAG Standard):**

$$CR = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$$

Where:
- $L_1$ is the relative luminance of the lighter color
- $L_2$ is the relative luminance of the darker color
- $L_1 > L_2$

The relative luminance is computed as:

$$L = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B$$

Where each channel value $R, G, B$ is the linearized sRGB component (gamma-corrected). The required **minimum ratio for WCAG AA is 4.5:1** for normal text and **3:1** for large text (≥ 18 pt regular or ≥ 14 pt bold).

### 2.1.2 OPERABLE — "Interface components and navigation must be operable."

**Why:** A user with motor impairment cannot use a mouse. A user with tremors cannot click small targets. A user with photosensitivity can have seizures from flashing content.

**How (Key Guidelines):**
- Make all functionality **keyboard accessible** (Tab order, Enter/Space activation, Esc to dismiss).
- Give users enough time to read and use content (no auto-timeouts under 20 hours, or pausable).
- Avoid content that flashes more than **3 times per second** (seizure-safe threshold).
- Provide **clear page titles, descriptive link text, and consistent navigation**.
- Target sizes must be at least **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (WCAG 2.5.5 — AAA) and **24 × 24 CSS pixels** (WCAG 2.2 — AA minimum).

### 2.1.3 UNDERSTANDABLE — "Information and operation of the user interface must be understandable."

**Why:** Cognitive disabilities, language barriers, and unfamiliar jargon can all make content unusable.

**How (Key Guidelines):**
- Use **plain language** and explain jargon, abbreviations, and idioms.
- Make pages function in **predictable ways**.
- Help users **avoid and correct mistakes** (clear error messages, suggestions for correction).
- Specify the **language** of content (`<html lang="en">`).
- For inputs, label them clearly; provide error prevention for financial/legal data (WCAG 3.3.4).

### 2.1.4 ROBUST — "Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies."

**Why:** Software evolves. Tomorrow's browser or screen reader must still work with today's content.

**How (Key Guidelines):**
- Use **valid, semantic HTML** (proper heading levels, lists, landmarks).
- Use **WAI-ARIA** (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes (`role`, `aria-label`, `aria-live`) when HTML semantics are insufficient.
- Ensure compatibility with current and future assistive technologies.

## 2.2 Assistive Technology Taxonomy for Computer Access

The rehabilitation engineer's toolkit for computer access spans hardware, software, and hybrid systems.

### 2.2.1 Alternative Input Devices

| Category | Examples | Target User Group |
|----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Alternative Keyboards** | Chord keyboards, on-screen keyboards, ergonomic split keyboards | Motor impairment, RSI, single-hand users |
| **Switch Input** | Single/double switch scanning interfaces | Severe motor impairment (ALS, CP) |
| **Pointing Devices** | Trackballs, head pointers, mouth sticks, eye-trackers (Tobii Dynavox) | Limited upper-limb control |
| **Speech Recognition** | Dragon NaturallySpeaking, Windows Speech Recognition, Google Voice Typing | Repetitive strain, mobility limitations |
| **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | P300 spellers, SSVEP-based systems (research-grade) | Locked-in syndrome, advanced ALS |

### 2.2.2 Alternative Output Devices

| Category | Examples | Function |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Screen Readers** | JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver (macOS/iOS), TalkBack (Android) | Auditory rendering of on-screen content |
| **Refreshable Braille Displays** | BrailleNote, Orbit Reader, HumanWare Brailliant | Tactile rendering of digital text (40–80 cell) |
| **Screen Magnifiers** | ZoomText, MAGic, built-in OS zoom | Visual enlargement up to 60× |
| **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** | NaturalReader, Kurzweil 1000 | Spoken output of text documents |
| **Haptic/Tactile Displays** | Experimental shape-memory alloy and vibrotactile displays | Research-grade non-visual output |

### 2.2.3 Software-Based AT

- **Word prediction / completion** (for motor or learning disability)
- **Sticky Keys, Filter Keys, Toggle Keys** (built into Windows for users with limited dexterity)
- **High-contrast and large-cursor modes**
- **Captioning software** (e.g., Otter.ai, Google Live Caption)
- **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) apps** (e.g., Proloquo2Go, TouchChat)

## 2.3 The WAI-ARIA Model

When standard HTML semantics fall short (especially in dynamic web apps), **WAI-ARIA** provides an extensibility layer. The three main features are:

1. **Roles** — Describe what an element is (`role="navigation"`, `role="alert"`, `role="button"`).
2. **States and Properties** — Describe the current condition (`aria-disabled="true"`, `aria-checked="true"`, `aria-expanded="false"`).
3. **Live Regions** — Inform AT of dynamic content changes (`aria-live="polite"`, `aria-live="assertive"`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Principle:** ARIA should be used as a *supplement to* native HTML semantics, not as a replacement. The first rule of ARIA, codified in W3C documentation, is: *if a native HTML element exists, use it.*

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | KTU Significance |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **POUR** | Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust | Most-tested 4-point framework |
| **WCAG Conformance Levels** | A (essential), AA (legal standard in most jurisdictions, including India), AAA (gold) | Asked frequently as definitions |
| **Contrast Ratio** | $CR = (L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)$, minimum **4.5:1** (AA), 7:1 (AAA) | Quantitative problem |
| **Seizure Threshold** | Max **3 flashes per second** | Often asked as a numerical value |
| **Keyboard-only operation** | All functions must be reachable via Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Arrow keys, Esc | Operational principle |
| **Captions / Transcripts** | Required for all pre-recorded and live audio content | Perceivable principle |
| **Target Size (WCAG 2.2)** | ≥ **24 × 24 CSS px** (AA), ≥ 44 × 44 (AAA) | Recently added guideline |
| **Focus Indicator** | Visible focus ring must be present on every focusable element | Operable principle |
| **Language Tag** | `<html lang="en">` at minimum, `<span lang="fr">` for inline | Understandable principle |
| **Semantic HTML** | `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<header>`, `<h1>`-`<h6>`, `<ul>`, `<button>` | Robust principle |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Corporate Compliance:** Indian IT firms and government portals (e.g., **Digital India, eCourts, IRCTC**) are mandated to meet WCAG 2.1 AA under the **GIGW (Guidelines for Indian Government Websites)** standard.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering Clinics:** Computer-access evaluations (mouse alternatives, switch scanning setup) are billable clinical services.
- **Product Design:** AT compatibility is now a procurement requirement in B2B and B2G software contracts.
- **Assistive Technology Industry:** Valued at over **USD 30 billion globally (2024)**, with India emerging as a fast-growing market.
