# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram #1 — HAAT Model Block Diagram

This is the **mandatory** foundational diagram for Module 4. It shows the four-way interaction that defines every Technology Solution selection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[ENVIRONMENTAL CONTEXT]
        E1[Physical Setting]
        E2[Social and Cultural Norms]
        E3[Institutional Policy]
        E4[Economic Access]
    end

    subgraph HUM[HUMAN USER]
        H1[Residual Body Functions]
        H2[Psychosocial Profile]
        H3[Personal Goals]
        H4[Activity Demand]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTIVITY DOMAIN]
        A1[Self Care ADL]
        A2[Mobility and Transfer]
        A3[Communication]
        A4[Learning and Work]
    end

    subgraph TEC[TECHNOLOGY SOLUTION]
        T1[No Tech Low Cost]
        T2[Low Tech Mechanical]
        T3[High Tech Electronic]
        T4[Smart IoT AI]
    end

    H4 --> A1
    H4 --> A2
    H4 --> A3
    H4 --> A4

    A1 --> T1
    A2 --> T2
    A3 --> T3
    A4 --> T4

    T1 --> H1
    T2 --> H1
    T3 --> H1
    T4 --> H1

    E1 -. modulates .-> T1
    E2 -. modulates .-> T2
    E3 -. modulates .-> T3
    E4 -. modulates .-> T4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows = *primary influence flows*. Dashed lines = *contextual modulation*. The user always remains the center of gravity of the system — no Technology Solution is prescribed without considering Human + Activity + Environment.

## 4.2 Diagram #2 — Functional Architecture of an Environmental Control System (ECS)

This block-level functional architecture models how a Smart-Home ECS integrates rehabilitation engineering with mainstream IoT.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER USER INTERFACE]
        UI1[Joystick Switch]
        UI2[Sip and Puff Sensor]
        UI3[Voice Microphone]

        UI5[BCI EEG Headset]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING LAYER CENTRAL CONTROLLER]
        CPU[Microcontroller SBC]
        FW[Intent Classification Firmware]
        DB[User Preference Database]
        ML[Adaptive ML Model]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT ACTUATION LAYER]
        O1[Smart Door Lock Relay]
        O2[Lighting Zigbee Module]
        O3[HVAC IR Control]
        O4[Television HDMI CEC]
        O5[Emergency Alert GSM]
    end

    subgraph FB[FEEDBACK LAYER]
        F1[Visual LED Confirmation]
        F2[Haptic Vibration Motor]
        F3[Audio Speaker Prompt]
        F4[Caregiver Mobile App]
    end

    UI1 --> CPU
    UI2 --> CPU
    UI3 --> CPU
    UI4 --> CPU
    UI5 --> CPU

    CPU --> FW
    FW --> ML
    FW --> DB

    CPU --> O1
    CPU --> O2
    CPU --> O3
    CPU --> O4
    CPU --> O5

    O1 --> F1
    O2 --> F1
    O3 --> F2
    O4 --> F3
    O5 --> F4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Interpretation:** The **redundancy of input modalities** (UI1–UI5) is the engineering hallmark of an accessible ECS — it follows the **Universal Design Principle #1: Equitable Use**. A user with quadriplegia can use Sip-and-Puff; a user with ALS can use Eye-Tracker; a user with locked-in syndrome can use BCI.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term **"Assistive Technology"** as per WHO and the KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering syllabus. List any **four functional categories** of Technology Solutions with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Assistive Technology (AT) is any item, piece of equipment, product, or system, whether acquired commercially, modified, or customized, that is used to **increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities** of individuals with disabilities (WHO / KTU 2024).

**Four Categories (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Mobility** — e.g., Powered Wheelchair
2. **Sensory** — e.g., Hearing Aid
3. **Communication** — e.g., Speech Generating Device (SGD)
4. **ADL** — e.g., Built-up Handle Spoon

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is the **HAAT Model** in Rehabilitation Engineering? List its **four components**.

**Model Answer:**

The **Human–Activity–Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model**, proposed by Cook and Hussey, is a person-centered framework used to evaluate and prescribe Technology Solutions.

**Four Components (0.75 each):**
1. **Human** — the user with residual abilities and impairments.
2. **Activity** — the goal-directed task (ADL, IADL, work, leisure).
3. **Assistive Technology** — the engineered device or system.
4. **Context** — physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Discuss in detail the **classification of Technology Solutions** in Rehabilitation Engineering. Explain the **HAAT Model** with a neat diagram. Also, describe the **ICF Framework** of WHO and how it relates to disability and rehabilitation technology.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Classification of Technology Solutions + HAAT Model with diagram.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** ICF Framework + its relationship to Technology Solutions.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification of Technology Solutions (4 marks):**

Technology Solutions can be classified along **five functional domains**:

| Domain | Purpose | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Mobility | Restore locomotion | Manual / Powered Wheelchair |
| Sensory | Substitute vision/hearing | Braille Display, Hearing Aid |
| Communication | AAC for speech impaired | SGD, Eye-tracking AAC |
| ADL/IADL | Self-care and domestic tasks | Adaptive utensils, Reachers |
| Environmental Control | Interact with surroundings | ECS, Smart Home |

These can also be classified by **technology complexity**:
- **No-tech:** Color-coded labels
- **Low-tech:** Built-up handles, reachers
- **High-tech:** Powered wheelchairs, screen readers
- **Smart-tech:** IoT-based ECS, AI fall detectors

**2. HAAT Model with Diagram (3 marks):**

The **HAAT Model** integrates four components:
- **H — Human** (user's residual function, motivation, social context)
- **A — Activity** (the goal task: ADL, IADL, education, employment)
- **A — Assistive Technology** (the prescribed device)
- **T — Context** (environment)

```
          [CONTEXT]
              |
              v
   [HUMAN] <----> [ACTIVITY]
              ^
              |
       [ASSISTIVE TECHNOLOGY]
```

The diagram shows that all four components are interrelated. A successful technology solution is achieved only when **all four components are aligned**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. ICF Framework (4 marks):**

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** was published by the WHO in 2001. It provides a unified, standard language and framework for describing health and health-related states.

**Structure:**
- **Part 1 — Functioning and Disability:**
  - *Body Functions and Structures* (impairments)
  - *Activities and Participation* (limitations/restrictions)
- **Part 2 — Contextual Factors:**
  - *Environmental Factors* (facilitators/barriers — products, technology, support, attitudes)
  - *Personal Factors* (age, gender, lifestyle, coping)

**2. Relationship to Technology Solutions (3 marks):**

- Technology Solutions are formally classified by ICF under **Environmental Factors → Products and Technology** (codes e115–e125).
- ICF shifts the focus from the **medical model** (disability as individual pathology) to the **biopsychosocial model** (disability as interaction).
- A wheelchair (e120 *Products for mobility*) becomes not a "treatment" but an **environmental facilitator** that enhances participation.
- For KTU, the ICF is essential because it underpins all modern assistive technology prescription frameworks, including the **HAAT model** and the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) model**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Describe the **mobility technology solutions** for persons with locomotor disability. Compare **manual and powered wheelchairs** in terms of engineering design. Explain **seating and positioning biomechanics** with reference to pressure ulcer prevention.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Mobility solutions + comparison of manual vs powered wheelchair.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Seating biomechanics + pressure ulcer prevention.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Mobility Technology Solutions (3 marks):**

For persons with locomotor disability, the following classes of devices are prescribed:
- **Walking aids:** Axillary/elbow crutches, walkers (rollators), canes.
- **Manual wheelchairs:** Folding, rigid-frame, lightweight (ultra-lightweight under 15 kg).
- **Powered wheelchairs:** Rear-wheel, mid-wheel, front-wheel drive; with joystick, chin-control, sip-and-puff, head-array, or eye-gaze input.
- **Scooters:** 3- or 4-wheeled for outdoor community ambulation.
- **Prosthetics:** SACH foot, multi-axial foot, microprocessor knee (C-Leg), myoelectric hand.
- **Orthotics:** KAFO, AFO, hip orthosis.

**2. Comparison: Manual vs Powered Wheelchair (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Manual Wheelchair | Powered Wheelchair |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Propulsion** | User upper-limb pushrims or attendant | Battery + DC motor + controller |
| **Power source** | Human muscle | 24 V lead-acid or Li-ion, 35–75 Ah |
| **Range per charge** | N/A (human-powered) | 15–40 km typical |
| **Max speed** | 4–6 km/h (self-propelled) | 6–10 km/h, up to 15 km/h |
| **User requirement** | Adequate upper-limb strength & endurance | Minimal strength; single-switch control possible |
| **Weight** | 8–15 kg | 50–120 kg with batteries |
| **Cost (INR)** | ₹8,000 – ₹80,000 | ₹80,000 – ₹6,00,000 |
| **Maintenance** | Bearings, tires, casters | Motors, batteries, joystick, controller |

> *Engineering Design Note:* Manual wheelchairs prioritize **stiffness-to-weight ratio** (often aluminum 7000-series or titanium). Powered wheelchairs prioritize **battery capacity, motor torque (Nm), and controller safety (ISO 7176 standards).**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Seating and Positioning Biomechanics (3 marks):**

Proper wheelchair seating distributes body weight over the **largest possible surface area** to minimize peak interface pressure. Three key pressure zones are monitored:
- **Ischial tuberosities (ITs)** — peak pressure ~150–200 mmHg in unsupported sitting.
- **Sacrum and coccyx**
- **Greater trochanters**

The standard seated posture should maintain:
- Pelvic neutral tilt (~5° anterior).
- Spinal curvature preserved.
- Hip flexion ~90°, knee ~90°, ankle ~90° (90-90-90 rule).

**2. Pressure Ulcer Prevention (4 marks):**

A **pressure ulcer (decubitus ulcer)** forms when sustained interface pressure exceeds **capillary closure pressure (~32 mmHg)** for prolonged periods.

**Prevention Technology Solutions:**
- **Cushions:** Foam, gel, air-cell (ROHO), honeycomb, alternating-pressure dynamic cushions.
- **Tilt-in-space** function (rearwards tilt ≥ 30° shifts load from ITs to back).
- **Recline** function.
- **Standing wheelchairs** (periodic weight-bearing reduces pressure and improves circulation).
- **Pressure mapping sensors** for clinical assessment.
- **Reminder apps / smart cushions** with IoT alert systems.

**Formula** — Effective reduction in peak pressure:
$$P_{\text{peak, cushioned}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{contact, cushioned}}}$$

where $A_{\text{contact, cushioned}}$ is the **enlarged contact area** provided by the cushion. A good cushion can increase effective contact area by **2× to 4×**, thereby halving to quartering the peak pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Pressure ulcer prevention is a multi-disciplinary engineering + nursing challenge and is a **favorite 7-mark question** on KTU exams because it combines biomechanics, materials, and IoT technology.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These in Your KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Conflating the ICF with the Medical Model.** Many students write ICF as "just a list of diseases." It is a *classification of functioning*, not disease. ICD-10/11 classifies diseases; **ICF classifies health states related to functioning.**
>
> 2. **Listing devices without justification.** KTU examiners award full marks only when you **justify** the choice using the HAAT model. Writing "Powered wheelchair for paraplegic" without explaining *why* loses **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing AAC with simple speech aids.** AAC = Augmentative and Alternative Communication — covers **unaided** (signs, gestures) and **aided** (low-tech, high-tech, SGD). Do NOT equate AAC with speech therapy alone.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mention context/environment.** The HAAT model has FOUR components. Omitting "Context" is a frequent partial-credit killer.
>
> 5. **Numerical mistakes on tip-over problems.** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians, or mixing up $d_{cg}$ vs $h_{cg}$, costs full marks. Always label your variables and units clearly.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions.** In derivations (e.g., battery endurance), explicitly state: *"Assuming 75% efficiency and 80% depth of discharge for lead-acid batteries."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Technology Solutions**
>
> - **Definition:** AT = any device/system used to *increase, maintain, or improve* functional capabilities of PwDs.
> - **Five Functional Domains:** Mobility, Sensory, Communication, ADL, Cognitive.
> - **HAAT Model:** Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context — *always include all four*.
> - **ICF Model (WHO 2001):** Biopsychosocial, *Body Function + Activity/Participation + Environmental + Personal factors*.
> - **Wheelchair Tip-over:** $\theta_{\text{limit}} = \arctan(d_{cg}/h_{cg})$.
> - **ADA Ramp Slope:** **1:12 (≈ 4.8°)**.
> - **Battery Endurance:** $T = \eta \cdot V \cdot Q / P_{\text{avg}}$.
> - **90-90-90 Seating Rule:** Hip 90°, Knee 90°, Ankle 90°.
> - **Pressure Ulcer Threshold:** Capillary closure at **~32 mmHg**.
> - **Camber Angle:** Typically **0°–7°**, improves lateral stability.
> - **Classification Standards:** ISO 9999 (AT products), ISO 7176 (wheelchairs).
> - **Universal Design Principles (7):** Equitable Use, Flexibility, Simple/Intuitive, Perceptible Info, Tolerance for Error, Low Effort, Size/Space.
> - **BrainPort (sensory substitution):** Tongue-electrode tactile vision for blind users.
> - **C-Leg / Genium:** Microprocessor-controlled knee — flagship high-tech prosthetic.
> - **Tobii Dynavox:** Industry-standard eye-gaze AAC.
> - **Cochlear Implant:** Bypasses damaged hair cells; directly stimulates auditory nerve via 22-channel electrode array.
> - **Differences to remember:** *Prosthesis* (replaces missing limb) vs *Orthosis* (supports existing limb).
> - **SACH Foot:** Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel — simplest prosthetic foot.
> - **Standing Wheelchair:** Allows user to reach eye-level; medical benefits for circulation, bone density, pressure relief.
> - **Hawking's AAC:** Stephen Hawking used a cheek-twitch-driven IR switch to operate a text-to-speech system — iconic example of customized AT.
> - **MPT Model (Scherer):** Adds personality, motivation, lifestyle to AT prescription.
